questions.correct_answer: They were becoming arrogant, exceeding boundaries, and antagonizing weaker kings
questions.explanation: The text describes that as Kuru influence slipped, other kings began becoming arrogant, exceeding their boundaries, and antagonizing weaker rulers.

1.2.2.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu felt impelled to take action. Going before Bhīṣma, he said, “My lord, our dynasty’s greatness has suffered diminution. The world is straying from the path of righteousness established by our ancestors. Irreligion is rising. It is my duty to our noble house, and indeed to the Supreme Lord himself, to go out and check the offenders.”

questions.question: What reason did Pāṇḍu provide to Bhīṣma for needing to leave and take action against other kings?
options: The dynasty's greatness had diminished and irreligion was rising
He desired to expand the Kuru kingdom to new continents
He wanted to escape the royal duties that bored him
The Supreme Lord had appeared to him in a vision demanding war
questions.correct_answer: The dynasty's greatness had diminished and irreligion was rising
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu specifically tells Bhīṣma that the dynasty's greatness has suffered diminution and that irreligion is rising.

questions.question: To whom did Pāṇḍu declare he owed a duty to check the offenders of righteousness?
options: Only to the ancestors of the Bharata line
To the noble house and the Supreme Lord
To the kings who had become arrogant and overstepped boundaries
To the people of the world regardless of their status
questions.correct_answer: To the noble house and the Supreme Lord
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu states it is his duty to their noble house and the Supreme Lord to check the offenders.

1.2.3.paragraph_text: Bhīṣma smiled. Here indeed was a worthy descendent of the great Bharata, after whom the very earth had been named. He replied, “Your desire befits our line and is praiseworthy in every way. Take blessings from the Brahmins and then leave with an army. May victory attend you!”

questions.question: After whom was the earth named, according to the lineage of Bhīṣma and Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the earth was named after the great Bharata.

questions.question: Whose blessings did Bhīṣma instruct Pāṇḍu to seek before departing for his military campaign?
questions.explanation: Bhīṣma tells Pāṇḍu to take blessings from the Brahmins before leaving with his army.

1.2.4.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu quickly assembled a vast force consisting of infantry, horsemen, elephants and chariots. As he left Hastināpura he looked like the king of the gods surrounded by his celestial army. Pāṇḍu marched to the east and defeated the king of the Dāśārhas, who was becoming averse to Hastināpura’s rule. Moving south toward Maghada, where the powerful King Dirgha was assailing many surrounding countries, Pāṇḍu attacked the proud king at his capital, Rajgriha, and killed him.

questions.question: Which specific king did Pāṇḍu defeat during his campaign in the east for showing defiance toward the rule of Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu marched to the east and defeated the king of the Dāśārhas, who was becoming averse to Hastināpura’s rule.

questions.question: In which city did Pāṇḍu kill the powerful King Dirgha?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu attacked the proud king Dirgha at his capital, Rajgriha, and killed him.

questions.question: Which kingdom was King Dirgha attacking before he was defeated by Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu moved south toward Maghada, where the powerful King Dirgha was assailing many surrounding countries.

questions.question: What four types of military forces were assembled by Pāṇḍu for his military campaign?
options: Infantry, cavalry, siege engines, and chariots
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu quickly assembled a vast force consisting of infantry, horsemen, elephants and chariots.

1.2.5.paragraph_text: After that Pāṇḍu subjugated several other warlike kings. He ranged across the globe like a fire, his far-reaching arrows and the splendor of his weapons resembling brilliant flames. As his fame spread, monarchs submitted without resistance. Soon all the world’s kings recognized him as the most powerful ruler on earth. They bowed to him with joined palms and offered tribute of various kinds.

questions.question: Which simile is used to describe Pāṇḍu's movement and military presence as he traveled across the globe?
questions.explanation: The text states Pāṇḍu ranged across the globe like a fire, with his weapons resembling brilliant flames.

questions.question: What items did the world's kings offer to Pāṇḍu upon acknowledging his supreme authority?
questions.explanation: The text describes that the kings bowed to Pāṇḍu and offered tribute of various kinds.

questions.question: What posture did the monarchs of the world adopt when submitting to Pāṇḍu's rule?
questions.explanation: The text notes that when submitting to Pāṇḍu, the monarchs bowed with joined palms.

1.2.6.paragraph_text: After this one-year campaign, Pāṇḍu returned to his capital with the wealth he had obtained. All the Kurus, with Bhīṣma at their head, came out to greet him. They viewed with delight the train of elephants, oxen, camels, horses and chariots, all laden with riches and stretching farther than the eye could see. Pāṇḍu then presented to the Kuru elders the precious stones, pearls, coral, gold and silver piled in heaps, along with millions of cows, horses and other animals. He showed them innumerable costly blankets, rugs and skins from the rare black renku deer.

questions.question: How long did Pāṇḍu’s military campaign last before he returned to the Kuru capital?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu returned to his capital after a one-year campaign.

questions.question: Who led the group of Kurus that came out to greet Pāṇḍu upon his return from his campaign?
questions.explanation: The text notes that all the Kurus, with Bhīṣma at their head, came out to greet Pāṇḍu.

questions.question: What specific type of rare animal skin did Pāṇḍu present to the Kuru elders?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu presented costly blankets, rugs, and skins from the rare black renku deer.

1.2.7.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu bowed at Bhīṣma’s feet as he presented all this wealth, and Bhīṣma tearfully embraced the young king. Surrounded by Brahmins uttering prayers and benedictions, the two men then mounted a golden chariot and proceeded to the royal palace, heralded by a fanfare of trumpets, conches and kettledrums.

questions.question: What formal gesture did Pāṇḍu perform toward Bhīṣma upon presenting his accumulated wealth?
options: He washed his feet with sacred water
He placed his sword on the ground
questions.explanation: The text states that Pāṇḍu bowed at Bhīṣma’s feet while presenting the wealth he had obtained.

questions.question: Which group of people surrounded the two men, offering prayers and benedictions, as they proceeded to the palace?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that Pāṇḍu and Bhīṣma were surrounded by Brahmins uttering prayers and benedictions.

questions.question: What vehicle did Pāṇḍu and Bhīṣma use to proceed to the royal palace?
options: A chariot drawn by white stallions
questions.explanation: According to the passage, the two men mounted a golden chariot to proceed to the palace.

questions.question: Which instruments were used to herald Pāṇḍu and Bhīṣma as they moved toward the palace?
questions.explanation: The passage details the arrival at the palace being heralded by a fanfare of trumpets, conches, and kettledrums.

1.2.8.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu ensured that his two brothers were each given much wealth. He had little personal interest in wealth, being more attracted to forests and plains than to the luxurious palace life. He loved to mount his stallion and ride out on long hunting expeditions. Thus, a few months after returning to Hastināpura, he decided to make his permanent residence upon a hilly slope of the Himālayan range. Taking his two wives with him, he left his magnificent palace and moved to a simple dwelling on the mountainside. The local people would see the monarch roaming the woods with his wives. Encased in beautiful blazing armor and armed with bows and swords, he resembled a god wandering on earth.

questions.question: Where did King Pāṇḍu choose to establish his permanent residence after returning to Hastināpura?
options: A hilly slope of the Himālayan range
The outskirts of the Kuru forest
A secluded island in the ocean
questions.correct_answer: A hilly slope of the Himālayan range
questions.explanation: The text states that Pāṇḍu decided to make his permanent residence upon a hilly slope of the Himālayan range.

questions.question: Which activity did Pāṇḍu particularly prefer over living a luxurious life in the palace?
options: Administering justice in the royal court
questions.explanation: The text notes that Pāṇḍu had little interest in palace luxury and loved to mount his stallion to ride out on long hunting expeditions.

questions.question: How many wives accompanied Pāṇḍu when he moved from the palace to the mountainside?
questions.explanation: The text confirms that Pāṇḍu left his magnificent palace to live on the mountainside taking his two wives with him.

questions.question: When Pāṇḍu was observed roaming the woods, what weaponry was he described as carrying?
questions.explanation: The text describes Pāṇḍu as being encased in armor and armed with bows and swords while roaming the woods.

1.2.9.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra officiated as the king in Hastināpura on behalf of his younger brother. At his command, the forest people would supply Pāṇḍu with every object of enjoyment. Pāṇḍu had mastered his senses, so although he gracefully accepted their gifts, he continued to live simply.

questions.question: Who assumed the role of king in Hastināpura to serve as the deputy for Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra officiated as the king in Hastināpura on behalf of his younger brother, Pāṇḍu.

questions.question: Who was responsible for ensuring that Pāṇḍu received various objects of enjoyment while he resided in the forest?
questions.explanation: The text specifies that at the command of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, the forest people were tasked with supplying Pāṇḍu with every object of enjoyment.

questions.question: How did Pāṇḍu respond to the gifts provided by the forest people?
options: He rejected them to maintain complete asceticism
He accepted them gracefully but maintained a simple lifestyle
He distributed them among the local woodland inhabitants
He returned them to Hastināpura out of duty
questions.correct_answer: He accepted them gracefully but maintained a simple lifestyle
questions.explanation: The text notes that Pāṇḍu had mastered his senses, allowing him to gracefully accept the gifts while continuing to live simply.

1.2.10.paragraph_text: One day Pāṇḍu went out hunting. He saw a couple of large deer mating. They bolted when they saw the king, but he quickly fired five swift arrows after them. As the golden-feathered shafts pierced the male deer, it fell down crying. To the king’s surprise the dying deer began to speak in a pained voice.

questions.question: What activity was King Pāṇḍu engaged in when he encountered the deer?
options: Traversing the forest to visit hermits
Surveying the boundaries of his kingdom
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that one day Pāṇḍu went out hunting.

questions.question: How many arrows did Pāṇḍu fire at the mating deer?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Pāṇḍu quickly fired five swift arrows after the deer.

questions.question: What distinctive feature did the arrows fired by King Pāṇḍu possess?
questions.explanation: The text describes the projectiles used by the king as golden-feathered shafts.

questions.question: What unexpected behavior did the male deer display after being struck by the king's arrows?
options: It transformed into a celestial being
It spoke in a pained voice
It escaped into the deep forest
It stood up and attacked the king
questions.correct_answer: It spoke in a pained voice
questions.explanation: The text notes that to the king’s surprise, the dying deer began to speak in a pained voice.

1.2.11.paragraph_text: “Oh, how shameful! Even degraded men who are slaves to their senses never act so cruelly. No man’s judgment can ever prevail against the ordinance of scripture. How then have you, the king and a descendent of the noble Bharata race, acted so, in conflict with Vedic ordinance?”

questions.question: What ancestral lineage does the deer identify Pāṇḍu as belonging to when reproaching his actions?
questions.explanation: The deer explicitly addresses Pāṇḍu as a descendent of the noble Bharata race while condemning his hunting of the deer.

questions.question: Which set of laws does the deer claim Pāṇḍu has violated by shooting the deer during the mating act?
questions.explanation: The deer questions how a king could act in conflict with Vedic ordinance, which governs proper conduct.

questions.question: According to the deer's rebuke, what is the ultimate power that stands above any man's judgment?
questions.explanation: The deer states that no man's judgment can ever prevail against the ordinance of scripture.

1.2.12.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu stood before the deer, which was weeping bitterly, and replied, “As a king it is my duty to hunt. I thus control the forest, making it safe for the ṛṣis. At the same time, I am able to practice the kingly art of weaponry. Furthermore, even great sages in the past have killed deer in the forest by offering them in sacrifice. O deer, why do you reprove me?”

questions.question: What primary justification does King Pāṇḍu provide for the act of hunting in the forest?
options: To provide food for his army
To control the forest and ensure the safety of the ṛṣis
To test the speed of his chariot horses
questions.correct_answer: To control the forest and ensure the safety of the ṛṣis
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu claims that his duty as a king is to hunt to control the forest, thereby making it safe for the ṛṣis.

questions.question: Besides maintaining forest safety, what personal benefit does Pāṇḍu claim to gain through hunting?
options: The collection of rare medicinal herbs
The practice of the kingly art of weaponry
The expansion of his kingdom's borders
The accumulation of gold from the forest tribes
questions.correct_answer: The practice of the kingly art of weaponry
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu states that hunting allows him to practice the kingly art of weaponry.

questions.question: Which historical precedent does Pāṇḍu cite to defend his actions of killing deer?
options: The legendary hunts of King Bharata
The dietary requirements of his ancestors
The practice of great sages offering deer in sacrifice
The victory of Indra over the demons
questions.correct_answer: The practice of great sages offering deer in sacrifice
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu argues that even great sages in the past have killed deer in the forest by offering them in sacrifice.

1.2.13.paragraph_text: The deer replied that it did not condemn Pāṇḍu for injuring it, but for not taking into consideration that it was mating. The deer then told Pāṇḍu that it was a ṛṣi named Kindama. He had assumed the form of a deer to mate with his wife in the woods. The ṛṣi had no dwelling and could not unite with his wife in a human form as people would criticize. He had therefore transformed himself and his wife into deer. The king had killed him just as he was about to beget a child in his wife’s womb. Kindama continued, “No creature should be attacked at the moment of intercourse. Your act was extremely cruel and sinful, and is liable to lead you to hell. It is especially reproachful as you are a king and are meant to chastise the wicked and protect the tenets of religion.”

questions.question: What was the actual identity of the deer that King Pāṇḍu had injured?
options: A forest demon in animal form
questions.explanation: The deer revealed to King Pāṇḍu that he was actually a ṛṣi named Kindama.

questions.question: For what specific reason did Kindama take on the form of a deer?
options: To hide from a rival sage
To mate with his wife without human criticism
To avoid the dangers of the forest
questions.correct_answer: To mate with his wife without human criticism
questions.explanation: Kindama explained that he transformed into a deer to be with his wife because, lacking a dwelling, he feared public criticism if seen in his human form.

questions.question: What moral principle did Kindama cite regarding the killing of creatures during intercourse?
options: It is only forbidden if the creature is a brahmin
It is acceptable for royalty but not for sages
No creature should be attacked at the moment of intercourse
It is acceptable provided the creature is intended for sacrifice
questions.correct_answer: No creature should be attacked at the moment of intercourse
questions.explanation: Kindama stated that attacking any creature during the act of intercourse is extremely cruel and sinful.

questions.question: Why did Kindama consider Pāṇḍu's action particularly reproachful for a king?
options: Because a king should always be vegetarian
Because a king is meant to chastise the wicked and protect religious tenets
Because a king must avoid all forms of hunting
Because a king should never travel into the deep forest
questions.correct_answer: Because a king is meant to chastise the wicked and protect religious tenets
questions.explanation: Kindama highlighted that Pāṇḍu's status as a king made his sinful act even worse, as kings are duty-bound to protect religious tenets and punish the wicked.

1.2.14.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu gazed at Kindama in shocked silence. He had spoken correctly. It had certainly been sinful to shoot at the deer as it mated. How had he allowed himself to be so overcome with passion? And what would come from having killed a ṛṣi? This was a calamity. The king hung his head in shame.

1.2.15.paragraph_text: Seeing Pāṇḍu’s crestfallen condition, Kindama said, “You need not fear the sin of killing a Brahmin, as you did not know my true identity. But as you killed me when I was indulging in pleasure, so too shall you meet your death at such a time.”

questions.question: Why did Kindama state that Pāṇḍu was exempt from the specific sin of killing a Brahmin?
options: Because Pāṇḍu had performed adequate penance
Because Pāṇḍu was unaware of Kindama's true identity
Because the king was under a spell
questions.correct_answer: Because Pāṇḍu was unaware of Kindama's true identity
questions.explanation: Kindama explicitly tells Pāṇḍu that he need not fear the sin of killing a Brahmin since he did not know his identity.

questions.question: What specific curse did Kindama place upon King Pāṇḍu regarding the circumstances of his death?
options: He would die by the hand of his own son
He would die while engaged in pleasure
He would die in the heat of battle
He would die after losing his kingdom
questions.correct_answer: He would die while engaged in pleasure
questions.explanation: Kindama decrees that just as he was killed while indulging in pleasure, Pāṇḍu will meet his own death at a similar time.

1.2.16.paragraph_text: The ṛṣi wanted to free Pāṇḍu from his sin. By cursing the king, Kindama knew that Pāṇḍu would immediately receive the reaction for his misdeed and thus not have to suffer after death. Struggling to speak as his lifeblood ebbed away, Kindama uttered his fearful imprecation.

questions.question: Why did the ṛṣi Kindama choose to curse King Pāṇḍu?
options: To punish him for failing to rule justly
To free him from his sin
To ensure Pāṇḍu would lose his kingdom
To satisfy the demands of the other sages
questions.correct_answer: To free him from his sin
questions.explanation: Kindama cursed Pāṇḍu specifically so the king would receive the reaction for his misdeed immediately and avoid suffering after death.

questions.question: What was the purpose of Kindama ensuring Pāṇḍu received the reaction for his misdeed during his lifetime?
options: To prevent Pāṇḍu from suffering after death
To ensure Pāṇḍu would be forced to abdicate
To demonstrate the power of a Brahmin's curse
To publicly shame the king for his actions
questions.correct_answer: To prevent Pāṇḍu from suffering after death
questions.explanation: The text states that by receiving the reaction immediately, Pāṇḍu would not have to suffer after death.

1.2.17.paragraph_text: “When next you approach your wife out of desire, you will immediately fall dead. O King, as I was plunged into grief when I was happy, you shall also meet with grief at such a time.”

questions.question: What specifically triggers King Pāṇḍu’s immediate death according to the curse placed upon him by the ṛṣi?
options: Approaching his wife out of desire
Refusing to perform his kingly duties
Entering the forest of the ṛṣis
questions.correct_answer: Approaching his wife out of desire
questions.explanation: The curse dictates that the king will fall dead immediately when he next approaches his wife motivated by desire.

questions.question: What emotional state is the King destined to experience at the moment of his predicted death?
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi stipulates that the King will meet with grief at the time of his death, mirroring the grief felt by the ṛṣi when he was happy.

1.2.18.paragraph_text: The deer then gave up its life. Pāṇḍu stood for some moments unable to move. As he gazed at the deer’s dead body hot tears ran down his cheeks. Gathering his wits, he returned to his hut. He immediately told his wives what had happened. Afflicted by sorrow, the king wept aloud with his two queens. He condemned himself again and again. Holding his head and lamenting, the king spoke in an anguished voice: “I have heard that my father, although born of a virtuous man, was himself a slave of lust and died as a result. Having been begotten in the wife of a lustful king, I too have become afflicted with lust. I have become devoted to sin. My life is simply spent in killing innocent creatures. The gods have forsaken me and I stand cursed.”

questions.question: To what specific cause did Pāṇḍu attribute the death of his own father?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu laments that his father was a slave of lust and died as a result.

questions.question: What reason did Pāṇḍu provide for his own preoccupation with killing innocent creatures?
options: He was born of a lustful king
He was serving the gods of the forest
He had lost his royal kingdom
He was under the influence of a demon
questions.correct_answer: He was born of a lustful king
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu states that because he was begotten in the wife of a lustful king, he has become afflicted with lust and devoted to sin, leading him to kill innocent creatures.

questions.question: What was Pāṇḍu's immediate emotional reaction after witnessing the death of the deer?
options: He felt pride in his hunting skill
He stood unable to move and wept
He immediately returned to his kingdom to perform penance
He felt no remorse for the animal
questions.correct_answer: He stood unable to move and wept
questions.explanation: The text describes Pāṇḍu standing for moments unable to move, with hot tears running down his cheeks as he gazed at the dead body.

1.2.19.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu resolved to live a life of austerity. He declared that from that moment he would accept a vow of celibacy, as Bhīṣma had done. He would seek salvation by renouncing all sexual pleasure, the great impediment to spiritual understanding. Pāṇḍu spoke to his horrified wives, “I shall shave my head and cover my body with dust. Sorrow and joy will be equal to me. I will entirely renounce anger and I shall be devoted to the good of all creatures. I will accept no gifts and I will obtain my food by begging. In this way, I shall transcend the dualities of this world and rise to the highest regions, where the Lord himself resides.”

questions.question: Which historical figure's celibacy vow did Pāṇḍu resolve to emulate as part of his new life of austerity?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu specifically declares that he would accept a vow of celibacy just as Bhīṣma had done.

questions.question: What reason does Pāṇḍu give for renouncing sexual pleasure in his pursuit of salvation?
options: It is a distraction from royal duties
It is the great impediment to spiritual understanding
It was the cause of his father's death
It prevents him from seeking the blessings of the sages
questions.correct_answer: It is the great impediment to spiritual understanding
questions.explanation: The text explicitly identifies sexual pleasure as the great impediment to spiritual understanding.

questions.question: What specific physical practices does Pāṇḍu vow to adopt as part of his renunciate lifestyle?
options: Wearing deer skin and carrying a staff
Shaving his head and covering his body with dust
Painting his body with sacred ash and wearing beads
Living in a cave and eating only roots
questions.correct_answer: Shaving his head and covering his body with dust
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu states his intention to shave his head and cover his body with dust as part of his renunciate vow.

questions.question: How does Pāṇḍu plan to obtain his food while living in austerity?
options: By gathering wild fruits and honey
By hunting only what is necessary for survival
By accepting offerings from visiting hermits
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu resolves to accept no gifts and obtain his food by begging.

1.2.20.paragraph_text: The king asked his wives to return to the capital and inform his elders of the turn of events. They should gratify the Brahmins on his behalf, giving them much wealth. For his part, he would immediately retire into the wilderness and leave society forever.

questions.question: What instructions did Pāṇḍu give his wives regarding the Brahmins before he retired to the wilderness?
options: To seek their blessing for his journey
To invite them to the capital for a final ritual
To gratify them with much wealth on his behalf
To request them to manage the kingdom in his absence
questions.correct_answer: To gratify them with much wealth on his behalf
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu requested his wives to gratify the Brahmins with much wealth on his behalf before leaving for the wilderness.

questions.question: Where did Pāṇḍu instruct his wives to go after his decision to renounce his royal life?
options: To the capital to inform his elders
To the temple to pray for his safety
questions.correct_answer: To the capital to inform his elders
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu asked his wives to return to the capital to inform his elders of the changes in his life.

1.2.21.paragraph_text: Kuntī and Mādrī were torn by grief. They could not face the prospect of separation from their husband. In piteous voices they begged that he allow them to follow his path. Both queens were prepared to accept whatever austerities he accepted. On behalf of them both, Kuntī said, “Let us together accept the order of vānaprastha, retirement in the forest. We shall be happy practising asceticism with you. If you leave us today, we shall not bear our lives any longer.”

questions.question: Which two queens expressed their grief and desire to accompany their husband into the wilderness?
questions.explanation: Kuntī and Mādrī were the two queens described as being torn by grief and desiring to follow their husband.

questions.question: What specific stage of life did Kuntī propose they adopt to remain with their husband in the forest?
questions.explanation: Kuntī proposed that they accept the order of vānaprastha, which refers to retirement in the forest.

1.2.22.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu relented, but he made it clear that he intended to perform the strictest austerities. His wives were not deterred. They wished only to be with him wherever he went.

questions.question: What was Pāṇḍu's primary intention regarding his future way of life after relenting to his wives' request?
options: To distribute his wealth to the Brahmins
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu relented to his wives but explicitly stated his intention to perform the strictest austerities.

questions.question: How did Kuntī and Mādrī respond to Pāṇḍu's decision to pursue a life of strict austerities?
options: They were deterred by the difficulty of the path
They wished only to accompany him
They requested to return to their fathers' kingdoms
questions.correct_answer: They wished only to accompany him
questions.explanation: The text states that Pāṇḍu's wives were not deterred and wished only to be with him wherever he went.

1.2.23.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu resolved to embark on his ascetic life immediately. He would not return to the city. He removed all his royal ornaments and gave them, along with his riches, to the Brahmins. Then he said to his attendants, “Go back to Hastināpura and inform Bhīṣma of everything that has occurred.”

questions.question: What did Pāṇḍu do with his royal ornaments and riches before embarking on his ascetic life?
options: He buried them in the forest for safekeeping.
He distributed them among the Brahmins.
He left them in the city of Hastināpura.
He entrusted them to the care of Bhīṣma.
questions.correct_answer: He distributed them among the Brahmins.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu removed his royal ornaments and gave them, along with his riches, to the Brahmins.

questions.question: Who did Pāṇḍu command his attendants to inform about his decision to live an ascetic life?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu instructed his attendants to return to Hastināpura and inform Bhīṣma of everything that had occurred.

questions.question: What immediate action did Pāṇḍu take regarding his royal status upon resolving to live an ascetic life?
options: He abdicated the throne in favor of his son.
He ordered a ritual sacrifice for the city.
He removed all his royal ornaments.
He requested a formal farewell ceremony in Hastināpura.
questions.correct_answer: He removed all his royal ornaments.
questions.explanation: Upon resolving to lead an ascetic life, Pāṇḍu removed all his royal ornaments.

1.2.24.paragraph_text: The royal attendants and Brahmins left sorrowfully for Hastināpura. The citizens lamented loudly when they learned of Pāṇḍu’s plight. Dhṛtarāṣṭra was especially afflicted. Thinking of Pāṇḍu living so austerely in the forest, he could no longer derive pleasure from the comforts of his beds, servants and royal opulences. He continually wept and brooded over Pāṇḍu.

questions.question: Which specific family member of Pāṇḍu experienced the greatest personal affliction upon hearing of Pāṇḍu's move to a life of austerity?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra was especially afflicted after learning of Pāṇḍu's situation.

questions.question: How did Dhṛtarāṣṭra respond to the news of Pāṇḍu living in the forest?
options: He sent messengers to bring Pāṇḍu back
He engaged in his own royal duties with renewed vigor
He wept, brooded, and lost interest in his own royal comforts
He ordered the attendants to be punished for delivering the news
questions.correct_answer: He wept, brooded, and lost interest in his own royal comforts
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Dhṛtarāṣṭra could no longer derive pleasure from his beds, servants, and royal opulences, and that he continually wept and brooded over Pāṇḍu.

1.2.25.paragraph_text: After the Brahmins and attendants had left, Pāṇḍu made his way into the forest with his wives. They walked steadily north for many days and finally arrived at Saptasrota, the hundred-peaked mountain. There the king constructed a wooden hut and began to engage in severe penance. Many ṛṣis, Siddhas and Cāraṇas lived in that region and Pāṇḍu soon endeared himself to them by his qualities of humility, self-control and devotion to his elders and to God. They would frequently visit his hut to converse with him.

questions.question: Which specific location, characterized by its hundred peaks, did Pāṇḍu reach after traveling north?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu traveled north for many days and arrived at the hundred-peaked mountain known as Saptasrota.

questions.question: Which groups of ascetic beings resided in the region where Pāṇḍu settled in the forest?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Ṛṣis, Siddhas, and Cāraṇas lived in the region of the hundred-peaked mountain where Pāṇḍu built his hut.

questions.question: Which specific personal qualities endeared Pāṇḍu to the inhabitants of the forest region?
options: Eloquence, artistic talent, and physical strength
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu earned the favor of the forest residents through his qualities of humility, self-control, and devotion to his elders and God.

1.2.26.paragraph_text: With his mind fixed only on salvation, Pāṇḍu lived quite simply at Saptasrota. He ate only fruits and roots and drank clear water from mountain springs. His two wives accepted the same vows he had taken, and thus all three lived joyfully on the mountain like celestials descended from the higher regions.

questions.question: What dietary regimen did Pāṇḍu adopt while residing at Saptasrota?
options: Fruits, roots, and clear water from springs
Grains offered by the visiting sages
Milk and herbs provided by the mountain dwellers
questions.correct_answer: Fruits, roots, and clear water from springs
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu ate only fruits and roots and drank clear water from mountain springs while at Saptasrota.

questions.question: What primary objective motivated Pāṇḍu's simple lifestyle at Saptasrota?
options: A desire to return to his kingdom
The need to avoid his enemies
questions.explanation: The passage confirms that Pāṇḍu lived simply because his mind was fixed only on salvation.

questions.question: How did Pāṇḍu's two wives participate in his life on the mountain?
options: They maintained their royal lifestyle separately
They returned to the palace to manage the kingdom
They accepted the same vows as Pāṇḍu
They acted as servants to the visiting ṛṣis
questions.correct_answer: They accepted the same vows as Pāṇḍu
questions.explanation: The passage notes that Pāṇḍu's two wives accepted the same vows he had taken.

1.2.27.paragraph_text: Gradually over time Pāṇḍu thought more about his situation. One day he spoke to the ṛṣis, revealing his fears. “O greatly learned ones, I have heard that a sonless man cannot attain heaven. By having sons one pays his debts to his ancestors. If I die without issue, my forefathers will surely perish, as the śraddhā offerings will cease. I am thus filled with apprehension because I cannot beget children.”

questions.question: What primary spiritual concern did Pāṇḍu express regarding the absence of sons?
options: He feared he would lose his kingdom to rival princes.
He believed a sonless man could not attain heaven.
He worried his wives would suffer in poverty.
He feared he would lose his martial prowess.
questions.correct_answer: He believed a sonless man could not attain heaven.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu stated to the ṛṣis that he had heard a sonless man cannot attain heaven.

questions.question: According to Pāṇḍu, how does a man fulfill his debts to his ancestors?
options: By performing intense meditation on mountain peaks.
By living a life of simplicity and celibacy.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu explicitly mentions that by having sons one pays his debts to his ancestors.

questions.question: What did Pāṇḍu fear would happen to his forefathers if he died without issue?
options: They would be forced to reincarnate immediately.
They would lose their divine status in the higher regions.
They would surely perish due to the cessation of śraddhā offerings.
They would be forgotten by the gods.
questions.correct_answer: They would surely perish due to the cessation of śraddhā offerings.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu stated that if he died without issue, his forefathers would perish because the śraddhā offerings would cease.

questions.question: To whom did Pāṇḍu voice his apprehensions regarding his inability to beget children?
options: His two wives, Kuntī and Mādrī.
The celestial beings on the mountain.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu spoke to the ṛṣis and referred to them as 'greatly learned ones' while revealing his fears.

1.2.28.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu was anxious. He would often perform the śraddhā ceremony for the welfare of his departed ancestors. The Vedas state that such food offerings made to the gods on their behalf ensure their continued happiness in heaven. Pāṇḍu was concerned. What would happen when he died? If he left no children, who would continue the offerings to his forefathers and, indeed, to himself? He folded his palms in supplication. Could the ṛṣis beget children in his wives, just as Vyāsadeva had begotten him and his brothers?

questions.question: What primary purpose did the śraddhā ceremony serve according to the Vedic understanding mentioned by Pāṇḍu?
options: To cleanse the soul of past sins
To ensure the continued happiness of departed ancestors in heaven
To gain favor from the local mountain deities
To seek immortality for the living ruler
questions.correct_answer: To ensure the continued happiness of departed ancestors in heaven
questions.explanation: The text states that Vedas claim food offerings made to gods on behalf of ancestors ensure their continued happiness in heaven.

questions.question: Whose historical example did Pāṇḍu cite when asking the ṛṣis if they could beget children with his wives?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu specifically asked if the ṛṣis could beget children in his wives, just as Vyāsadeva had begotten him and his brothers.

questions.question: What specific fear did Pāṇḍu express regarding the cessation of the śraddhā offerings after his own death?
options: That his kingdom would be conquered by invaders
That he would have no one to offer food to his forefathers or to himself
That his wives would be left without protection
That the gods would punish his subjects for his failures
questions.correct_answer: That he would have no one to offer food to his forefathers or to himself
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu worried about who would continue the offerings to his forefathers and to himself if he left no children.

1.2.29.paragraph_text: Smiling, one of the ṛṣis replied, “By our ascetic powers we have seen that you will undoubtedly have children like the gods themselves. Indeed, your progeny will fulfill the purpose of the celestials. All this can be seen clearly by us. Therefore you should certainly act in such a way as to somehow produce children.”

questions.question: What did the ṛṣi promise regarding the nature of the children Pāṇḍu would have?
options: They would be like the gods themselves
They would be incarnations of the Daityas
They would be the greatest of ascetic sages
They would be rulers of the netherworld
questions.correct_answer: They would be like the gods themselves
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi states that Pāṇḍu will undoubtedly have children who are like the gods themselves.

questions.question: What power did the ṛṣis claim to possess that allowed them to know Pāṇḍu's future children?
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi confirms that through their ascetic powers, they have seen that Pāṇḍu will have children.

questions.question: According to the ṛṣi, what purpose would Pāṇḍu's progeny fulfill?
options: The restoration of the Kuru dynasty
The destruction of the demonic forces
The expansion of the Vedic rituals
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi explicitly mentions that Pāṇḍu's progeny will fulfill the purpose of the celestials.

1.2.30.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu reflected on the ṛṣi’s words. It seemed the sages were amenable to his suggestion. He went to Kuntī and spoke with her in private. “O lady of sweet smiles, I desire offspring, but due to my vicious behavior I have lost my power of procreation. In such instances the scriptures sanction that another worthy man may beget children on my behalf. Please, therefore, accept another man and conceive a child for me.”

questions.question: What reason did Pāṇḍu provide to Kuntī for his inability to father children?
options: A curse placed upon him by a forest sage
A vow of celibacy taken for spiritual advancement
Loss of his power of procreation due to his vicious behavior
A physical illness contracted while hunting in the woods
questions.correct_answer: Loss of his power of procreation due to his vicious behavior
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu specifically explains to Kuntī that his vicious behavior resulted in the loss of his power of procreation.

questions.question: What justification does Pāṇḍu offer for requesting Kuntī to conceive a child with another man?
options: The direct command of the gods
Scriptural sanction allowing another worthy man to beget children in such instances
The advice given by his brothers during their exile
The necessity of maintaining the Kuru line at all costs
questions.correct_answer: Scriptural sanction allowing another worthy man to beget children in such instances
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu appeals to scriptural authority, noting that scriptures sanction having another worthy man beget children when he is unable to do so.

1.2.31.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu pointed out that even he and his brothers had been conceived in this way. Kuntī should thus accept a qualified Brahmin, who was superior to himself, in order to have a child.

questions.question: What historical precedent did Pāṇḍu cite to Kuntī regarding his own and his brothers' birth?
options: They were born of gods through meditation
They were conceived by a qualified Brahmin
They were miraculously brought into existence
They were the product of a specific royal rite
questions.correct_answer: They were conceived by a qualified Brahmin
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu reminded Kuntī that he and his brothers were conceived in the same manner he is now proposing for her.

questions.question: According to Pāṇḍu, what qualification should the man chosen to beget a child with Kuntī possess?
options: He must be a powerful king
He must be a close relative of the Pāṇḍavas
He must be a Brahmin superior to Pāṇḍu
He must be an ascetic living in the forest
questions.correct_answer: He must be a Brahmin superior to Pāṇḍu
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu specified that Kuntī should accept a qualified Brahmin who is superior to himself.

1.2.32.paragraph_text: The noble Kuntī did not like Pāṇḍu’s suggestion. She looked down and spoke softly in reply, “O virtuous one, please do not request this of me. I am your lawful wife, ever devoted to you. I shall certainly not accept another’s embrace, even in my imagination. O King, embrace me to beget offspring. I shall ascend to heaven with you. Who is there superior to you?”

questions.question: How did Kuntī react to Pāṇḍu's suggestion that she conceive a child through another man?
options: She immediately agreed out of duty
She expressed deep disapproval and refused the proposal
She asked for more time to consider the matter
She suggested they consult with a Brahmin first
questions.correct_answer: She expressed deep disapproval and refused the proposal
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly stated she did not like Pāṇḍu's suggestion and asked him not to request it of her.

questions.question: What reason did Kuntī provide for refusing Pāṇḍu's request to conceive a child with another?
options: She considered herself his loyal wife and would not embrace another
She was forbidden by her father from ever seeing another man
She believed the Brahmin was not worthy of her
She feared the social stigma of bearing a child out of wedlock
questions.correct_answer: She considered herself his loyal wife and would not embrace another
questions.explanation: Kuntī declared she was Pāṇḍu's lawful wife, devoted to him, and would not accept another's embrace even in her imagination.

questions.question: What did Kuntī propose to Pāṇḍu as an alternative to his suggestion of conceiving with another man?
options: That they adopt a child from the forest
That they visit a holy pilgrimage site together
That Pāṇḍu himself embrace her to beget offspring
That they renounce the kingdom to live as hermits
questions.correct_answer: That Pāṇḍu himself embrace her to beget offspring
questions.explanation: Kuntī asked Pāṇḍu to embrace her himself to beget offspring, indicating her preference for her husband over another.

1.2.33.paragraph_text: Kuntī recited to her husband an ancient history she had heard. There was once a powerful king who had died without issue. His lamenting wife had embraced his dead body and by the arrangement of the gods she conceived children. Three qualified sons were born of her union with her dead husband. Kuntī asked Pāṇḍu to emulate that king.

questions.question: What ancient history did Kuntī cite to Pāṇḍu regarding the conception of children?
options: A king died without issue and his wife conceived children by his dead body through divine arrangement.
A king requested his queen to perform a sacrifice to gain a boon for a son.
A widow was commanded by a sage to marry a brother of her deceased husband.
A queen sought a blessing from a god to conceive a son while her husband was in exile.
questions.correct_answer: A king died without issue and his wife conceived children by his dead body through divine arrangement.
questions.explanation: Kuntī recounted the story of a powerful king who died without issue, whose wife conceived three sons through the arrangement of the gods by embracing his dead body.

questions.question: How many sons were born to the queen who conceived children after her husband's death?
questions.explanation: The text states that three qualified sons were born of the queen's union with her dead husband.

questions.question: What did Kuntī request of Pāṇḍu after sharing the account of the ancient king?
options: To perform a yajna for a son
To return to the kingdom and reclaim the throne
To emulate the ancient king and allow her to conceive in a similar manner
To seek advice from a learned ṛṣi regarding their situation
questions.correct_answer: To emulate the ancient king and allow her to conceive in a similar manner
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly asked Pāṇḍu to emulate the king mentioned in the ancient history she recited.

1.2.34.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu replied that he did not possess such power. He preferred she conceive a child by uniting with a ṛṣi. He assured her that no sin was attached to such an act. It was his responsibility, as he was commanding her as her husband, and she should not hesitate to comply with his request. The monarch tried in many ways to convince his wife.

questions.question: What method did Pāṇḍu propose to Kuntī for conceiving a child after he admitted he lacked the power to produce heirs himself?
options: By summoning a celestial via a mantra
By adopting a son from the forest
By performing a great yajña for the gods
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu suggests that Kuntī conceive a child by uniting with a ṛṣi after admitting he did not possess the power to conceive children himself.

questions.question: How did Pāṇḍu justify the request for Kuntī to conceive a child through a ṛṣi regarding the potential for sin?
options: He claimed the sin would be transferred to the ṛṣi
He assured her no sin was attached to the act
He stated that the gods would forgive the sin
He insisted that duty overrides all sin
questions.correct_answer: He assured her no sin was attached to the act
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu explicitly tells Kuntī that no sin is attached to the act of conceiving a child with a ṛṣi at his command.

questions.question: What authority did Pāṇḍu invoke to encourage Kuntī to comply with his request to conceive a child with another?
options: His status as a king commanding his subject
The decree of the ancient ancestors
The spiritual requirement for a royal heir
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu asserts his authority by stating he is commanding her as her husband, and she should not hesitate to comply.

1.2.35.paragraph_text: At last, Kuntī, seeing her husband’s determination, said to him, “I shall tell you of a boon I received while in my maidenhood, which may yet prove to be our deliverance. When I was a girl in my father’s house, he would engage me in serving guests there. One day the powerful mystic Durvāsā arrived. By my service and constant attention to all his needs I greatly pleased that ṛṣi. As he was leaving he called me aside and said, ‘O gentle one, I shall confer upon you a boon. Listen as I recite a mantra. This mantra, uttered by you, will summon any of the celestials you desire. Whether agreeable or not they will then be obliged to do your bidding.’”

questions.question: Which specific mystic granted Kuntī the boon of summoning celestials?
questions.explanation: The paragraph identifies the powerful mystic who gave the mantra to Kuntī as Durvāsā.

questions.question: Where was Kuntī residing when she first served the mystic Durvāsā?
options: At a hermitage on the Ganges
questions.explanation: Kuntī explains that she served the mystic while she was a girl living in her father’s house.

questions.question: What must Kuntī do to oblige a celestial to perform her bidding?
questions.explanation: Durvāsā explained to Kuntī that by reciting the mantra he provided, she could summon any celestial and compel them to do her bidding.

questions.question: What initial task did Kuntī's father assign to her regarding visitors?
options: She was to teach them the Vedas
She was to engage in serving guests
She was to protect them from demons
She was to organize their chariot processions
questions.correct_answer: She was to engage in serving guests
questions.explanation: Kuntī mentions that her father would engage her in serving guests who arrived at their house.

1.2.36.paragraph_text: Kuntī already had experience of the mantra’s power. As soon as she had uttered it once and thought of Sūrya, that blazing deity had appeared before her. He had then conceived a son. Kuntī therefore knew that she could summon other gods to produce further children. She had not wanted to reveal this secret, being reluctant to unite even with the gods in place of her own husband. But this was an emergency. It seemed that the time to make good use of Durvāsā’s mantra had come.

questions.question: Which deity did Kuntī summon after first testing the effectiveness of the mantra provided by Durvāsā?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that as soon as Kuntī uttered the mantra once, the blazing deity Sūrya appeared before her.

questions.question: Why had Kuntī been reluctant to use the boon granted by Durvāsā previously?
options: She feared the wrath of the celestials
She did not believe the mantra would function
She was unwilling to unite with gods in place of her husband
She was waiting for her husband to give his express permission
questions.correct_answer: She was unwilling to unite with gods in place of her husband
questions.explanation: Kuntī held back from revealing the secret of the mantra because she was reluctant to unite with gods instead of her husband.

questions.question: What is the primary result of the union between Kuntī and the deity she summoned using the mantra?
options: A divine weapon was granted to her
A kingdom was promised to her descendants
questions.explanation: The text confirms that after the deity Sūrya appeared, he and Kuntī conceived a son.

1.2.37.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu, who had known nothing about the boon, was overjoyed upon hearing Kuntī’s words. This was surely the Lord’s arrangement. Sons from the gods would be far superior to any born of earthly men.

questions.question: What was Pāṇḍu's reaction upon learning of Kuntī’s ability to summon gods for progeny?
options: He was deeply suspicious of her intent
He was overjoyed at the prospect
He felt insulted by her confession
He feared the consequences of divine intervention
questions.correct_answer: He was overjoyed at the prospect
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu was overjoyed upon hearing Kuntī’s words regarding the boon.

questions.question: How did Pāṇḍu perceive children conceived through divine intervention compared to those born of earthly men?
options: He believed them to be less virtuous
He considered them equal to ordinary men
He believed they would be far superior
He was indifferent to their origin
questions.correct_answer: He believed they would be far superior
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu believed that sons born from the gods would be far superior to those born of earthly men.

1.2.38.paragraph_text: Kuntī asked her husband which of the deities she should call.

1.2.39.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu thought for some moments and then said to his wife, “O beautiful lady, you should call the great Dharma, the god of justice. He will never be able to pollute us with sin and his son will undoubtedly be qualified in every way.”

questions.question: Which deity did Pāṇḍu request that Kuntī summon for the purpose of procreation?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu specifically instructed his wife to call upon Dharma, the god of justice.

questions.question: What primary reason did Pāṇḍu give for choosing Dharma as the deity for Kuntī to invoke?
options: Dharma was the most powerful among all the celestial beings.
Dharma was the progenitor of all Kshatriyas.
Dharma would never be able to pollute them with sin.
Dharma promised to grant them a kingdom of their own.
questions.correct_answer: Dharma would never be able to pollute them with sin.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu stated that Dharma is the god of justice and would not pollute them with sin.

questions.question: How did Pāṇḍu characterize the expected outcome regarding the son born from Dharma?
options: He would be the greatest warrior in the world.
He would be qualified in every way.
He would possess the power of the wind.
He would rule over the heavens and the earth.
questions.correct_answer: He would be qualified in every way.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu expressed that a son born from Dharma would undoubtedly be qualified in every way.

1.2.40.paragraph_text: Kuntī assented to Pāṇḍu’s request. She sat in meditation and thought of Dharma, reciting the mantra Durvāsā had given her. Within moments the deity appeared, riding on a resplendent chariot. Shining like the sun, he smiled and said to Kuntī, “What should I do for you at this time?”

questions.question: What method did Kuntī use to summon the deity Dharma?
options: She sat in meditation and recited a specific mantra
She traveled to a remote mountain peak to pray
She offered gold and incense at an altar
questions.correct_answer: She sat in meditation and recited a specific mantra
questions.explanation: Kuntī sat in meditation and thought of Dharma while reciting the mantra previously given to her.

questions.question: From whom did Kuntī originally receive the mantra used to summon the deity?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Kuntī recited the mantra that Durvāsā had previously given her.

questions.question: How did the deity Dharma travel when he appeared before Kuntī?
options: Walking upon a path of lotus flowers
Descending on a cloud of fire
By appearing instantly out of thin air
questions.explanation: Upon being summoned, the deity appeared riding on a resplendent chariot.

1.2.41.paragraph_text: Awed by the god’s splendor, Kuntī replied in a trembling voice, “I desire a child by your illustrious self.”

questions.question: When addressing the god Dharma, what request did Kuntī make?
options: She requested a boon of eternal life.
She asked for a child by the deity.
She begged for the protection of her husband Pāṇḍu.
She sought knowledge of the future of the Kuru dynasty.
questions.correct_answer: She asked for a child by the deity.
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly asks Dharma for a child by his illustrious self.

1.2.42.paragraph_text: Dharma at once united with her in his spiritual form and then disappeared. Kuntī conceived and in due course gave birth. During the eighth month of the year, at noon on a full moon day, when the auspicious star Jyeṣṭhā was in the ascendant, she brought forth a lustrous son. As soon as he was born a celestial voice was heard in the sky: “This child will be the best and most virtuous of men. He will be devoted to truth, highly powerful and famous throughout the three worlds. Known as Yudhiṣṭhira, this boy will rule the earth.”

questions.question: Which deity united with Kuntī in his spiritual form to conceive her first son?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Dharma united with Kuntī in his spiritual form.

questions.question: Under what specific astrological condition was Yudhiṣṭhira born?
options: When the star Rohiṇī was in the ascendant
When the star Jyeṣṭhā was in the ascendant
When the star Puṣya was in the ascendant
When the star Maghā was in the ascendant
questions.correct_answer: When the star Jyeṣṭhā was in the ascendant
questions.explanation: The passage confirms that Yudhiṣṭhira was born when the auspicious star Jyeṣṭhā was in the ascendant.

questions.question: What prophecy was delivered by the celestial voice immediately following the birth of Yudhiṣṭhira?
options: He would conquer the entire universe through war
He would be the best and most virtuous of men
He would be the last of the Kuru dynasty
He would live for one hundred years
questions.correct_answer: He would be the best and most virtuous of men
questions.explanation: The celestial voice declared that the child would be the best and most virtuous of men.

questions.question: Besides being famous throughout the three worlds, what other specific attributes were foretold for Yudhiṣṭhira at his birth?
options: Devoted to truth and highly powerful
Skilled in all weaponry and chariot combat
Rich in wealth and protector of the Vedas
Immune to fire and invincible in battle
questions.correct_answer: Devoted to truth and highly powerful
questions.explanation: The celestial voice described the infant as being devoted to truth and highly powerful.

questions.question: At what time of day was Yudhiṣṭhira born?
questions.explanation: The text identifies the time of Yudhiṣṭhira's birth as noon on a full moon day.

1.2.43.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu was overjoyed. The gods had not forsaken him after all! Here was a child worthy of his great dynasty. He no longer had to be anxious.

1.2.44.paragraph_text: Then he began to consider the situation. He saw that by Durvāsā’s boon Kuntī could obtain more powerful sons, born of the gods themselves, who would ensure the Kuru’s welfare and become great leaders in the world. Pāṇḍu approached Kuntī again, a year after Yudhiṣṭhira’s birth, and said, “The wise have declared that a ruler should possess strength as well as righteousness. Therefore, please invoke the wind-god, Vāyu, the strongest of the gods. By him we shall get a son who will be the most powerful man upon this earth.”

questions.question: What primary reason did Pāṇḍu provide for requesting Kuntī to invoke the wind-god Vāyu?
options: To fulfill a prophecy made by Durvāsā
To ensure the birth of a son who would be the most powerful man upon the earth
To appease the ancestors of the Kuru dynasty
To gain protection against the impending threats from neighboring kingdoms
questions.correct_answer: To ensure the birth of a son who would be the most powerful man upon the earth
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu instructs Kuntī to invoke Vāyu specifically to produce a son who would be the most powerful man upon the earth.

questions.question: How long after the birth of Yudhiṣṭhira did Pāṇḍu approach Kuntī to invoke Vāyu?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu approached Kuntī again a year after the birth of Yudhiṣṭhira.

questions.question: According to Pāṇḍu, what two qualities should a ruler possess?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu states that the wise have declared that a ruler should possess strength as well as righteousness.

questions.question: Which deity granted the boon that allowed Kuntī to obtain powerful sons from the gods?
questions.explanation: The paragraph notes that by Durvāsā's boon, Kuntī could obtain powerful sons born of the gods themselves.

1.2.45.paragraph_text: Kuntī folded her palms and bowed in assent. She again sat in meditation, this time calling for Vāyu. In moments the god appeared, riding a huge deer. In a voice that sounded like thunderclouds, the effulgent god said with a smile, “O Kuntī, what do you desire from me?”

questions.question: Upon appearing to Kuntī, what was Vāyu riding?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Vāyu appeared riding a huge deer.

questions.question: Which deity manifested in response to Kuntī’s meditation after she bowed in assent?
questions.explanation: Kuntī sat in meditation to call upon the god Vāyu.

questions.question: How is the voice of the god Vāyu described when he addresses Kuntī?
options: Like the ringing of temple bells
Like the roar of a lion
questions.explanation: The text describes Vāyu's voice as sounding like thunderclouds.

1.2.46.paragraph_text: Kuntī was bashful as she replied, “O best of the celestials, please give me a son who will possess immense bodily strength, and who will be capable of humbling the pride of everybody.”

questions.question: What two specific qualities did Kuntī request for the son she asked Vāyu to bless her with?
options: Immense bodily strength and the capacity to humble the pride of others
Unparalleled beauty and command over the winds
Regal authority and the ability to live forever
questions.correct_answer: Immense bodily strength and the capacity to humble the pride of others
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly asks for a son who possesses immense bodily strength and the ability to humble the pride of everyone.

1.2.47.paragraph_text: The god agreed, and by his yogic power he approached Kuntī and conceived a child within her womb. When Vāyu’s child took his birth a voice was again heard from the sky, saying, “This child will be the best of all those endowed with strength and power.”

questions.question: Which deity used his yogic power to conceive a child within Kuntī's womb?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Vāyu, by his yogic power, approached Kuntī and conceived a child within her womb.

questions.question: When Vāyu's child was born, what did the voice from the sky prophesy regarding the newborn?
options: That he would become a great king of the Kurus
That he would be the best of all those endowed with strength and power
That he would bring peace to the world
That he would surpass his father in wisdom
questions.correct_answer: That he would be the best of all those endowed with strength and power
questions.explanation: The text reports that a voice from the sky proclaimed that the child would be the best of all those endowed with strength and power.

1.2.48.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu and Kuntī were even more joyful to see their second son, whom they named Bhīma. Along with Mādrī, and in the company of the ṛṣis and Siddhas, they lovingly raised their children in the remote and beautiful woodlands on the mountainside.

questions.question: What was the name given to the second son of Pāṇḍu and Kuntī?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu and Kuntī named their second son Bhīma.

questions.question: In what type of environment did Pāṇḍu and Kuntī raise their children alongside Mādrī?
options: The banks of the Ganges River
A secluded forest ashram in the plains
questions.explanation: The passage describes the setting where Pāṇḍu, Kuntī, and Mādrī raised their children as remote and beautiful woodlands on the mountainside.

questions.question: Which groups of beings were present in the company of Pāṇḍu and his family while they raised their children?
questions.explanation: The text confirms that Pāṇḍu and his family raised their children in the company of ṛṣis and Siddhas.

1.2.49.paragraph_text: Some months after Bhīma’s birth, Kuntī was sitting near the edge of a mountain cliff with Bhīma asleep on her lap. Suddenly a tiger roared nearby. She jumped up in fear and the baby rolled from her lap over the cliff edge. Struck with horror, Pāṇḍu quickly descended the cliff. When he reached the bottom he saw the child lying unharmed amid a pile of rocky fragments. The baby had landed upon a large rock and had smashed it to pieces. In amazement Pāṇḍu gently lifted his child and took him back to Kuntī.

questions.question: What unexpected incident occurred while Kuntī was sitting near a mountain cliff with the infant Bhīma?
questions.explanation: Kuntī was startled by the roar of a tiger, causing her to jump and lose her hold on the infant.

questions.question: What unusual physical feat did the baby Bhīma accomplish upon falling down the mountain cliff?
options: He survived by landing on a patch of soft moss.
He caught onto a protruding tree branch.
He landed on a large rock and shattered it into pieces.
He floated down slowly due to divine intervention.
questions.correct_answer: He landed on a large rock and shattered it into pieces.
questions.explanation: Upon reaching the bottom of the cliff, Pāṇḍu discovered that the baby had remained unharmed after smashing a large rock into fragments under his weight.

questions.question: Who retrieved the infant Bhīma after he fell from the mountain cliff?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu descended the cliff and discovered the child, eventually lifting him up to return him to Kuntī.

1.2.50.paragraph_text: Even with two sons, Pāṇḍu continued to reflect on the future of his dynasty. He thought, “Success in this world depends upon exertion, but exertion is always subordinate to destiny. Yet even destiny is controlled by the will of the Supreme Lord. How then can I obtain a son who will be the best of all those devoted to the Lord?”

questions.question: What two factors does Pāṇḍu consider to be the primary drivers of success in the world?
options: Destiny and the will of the Supreme Lord
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu reflects that success in the world depends upon exertion, but notes that exertion is always subordinate to destiny.

questions.question: What entity does Pāṇḍu identify as ultimately controlling the force of destiny?
options: The gods of the celestial realm
The merit of one's own karma
The will of the Supreme Lord
The efforts of the kings of the dynasty
questions.correct_answer: The will of the Supreme Lord
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu explicitly states that even destiny is controlled by the will of the Supreme Lord.

questions.question: What specific quality does Pāṇḍu desire for his future son?
options: To be the greatest warrior among all kings
To be the best of all those devoted to the Lord
To be the heir who restores the Kuru dynasty
To be the most righteous ruler of the earth
questions.correct_answer: To be the best of all those devoted to the Lord
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu wonders how he can obtain a son who will be the best of all those devoted to the Lord.

1.2.51.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu thought of Indra, the king of all the gods and a famous devotee of the Lord. Indra had formerly performed a thousand sacrifices for Lord Viṣṇu’s pleasure, and he possessed immeasurable might, prowess and glory. From him would surely come a son who would be superior to all.

questions.question: Which deity did Pāṇḍu consider as the ideal candidate to father a son who would be superior to all others?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu reflected on Indra, the king of the gods, as the being from whom a son superior to all would come.

questions.question: How many sacrifices did Indra perform specifically for the pleasure of Lord Viṣṇu?
questions.explanation: The text states that Indra had formerly performed a thousand sacrifices for Lord Viṣṇu's pleasure.

questions.question: Besides being the king of the gods, what status does the text attribute to Indra regarding his relationship with the Lord?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly identifies Indra as the king of the gods and a famous devotee of the Lord.

1.2.52.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu consulted with the ṛṣis and was advised that he and Kuntī should practice an ascetic vow for one year to please Indra. At the end of that period Kuntī should summon the god with her mantra.

questions.question: For what duration were Pāṇḍu and Kuntī advised by the ṛṣis to perform their ascetic vow?
questions.explanation: The text states that Pāṇḍu was advised to practice an ascetic vow for one year to please Indra.

questions.question: Whom were Pāṇḍu and Kuntī seeking to please through their ascetic practice?
questions.explanation: The passage confirms that the ascetic vow was performed to please Indra.

questions.question: What specific action was Kuntī to perform at the conclusion of the year-long ascetic vow?
options: Offer a sacrifice to the sages
Summon the god with her mantra
Travel to the heavens to meet Indra
questions.correct_answer: Summon the god with her mantra
questions.explanation: The text specifies that at the end of the year-long period, Kuntī should summon the god using her mantra.

questions.question: With whom did Pāṇḍu consult before undertaking the ascetic vow?
questions.explanation: The paragraph notes that Pāṇḍu consulted with the ṛṣis regarding the ascetic vow.

1.2.53.paragraph_text: The monarch and his wife then accepted a vow of standing on one leg from sunrise till sunset, taking neither food nor water. They kept their minds absorbed in meditation and prayer for one complete year, with an aim to please Indra. At the end of the year Indra spoke to Pāṇḍu in his meditation. “I have become pleased with you, O King. I shall give you a son who will protect religion and chastise the wicked. He will delight his friends and relatives and slay his foes. Indeed, this boy will be the best of men.”

questions.question: What physical posture did King Pāṇḍu and his wife Kuntī adopt as part of their year-long ascetic vow?
options: Lying on a bed of thorns
Walking in a circle around the mountain
questions.explanation: The monarch and his wife practiced a vow of standing on one leg from sunrise till sunset during their year of meditation.

questions.question: During their ascetic practice, what duration of time did Pāṇḍu and Kuntī dedicate to meditation and prayer?
questions.explanation: They kept their minds absorbed in meditation and prayer for one complete year to please Indra.

questions.question: Which deity expressed satisfaction with Pāṇḍu's year-long ascetic practice?
questions.explanation: Indra spoke to Pāṇḍu at the end of the year, stating that he had become pleased with the King.

questions.question: What primary duty did Indra promise the son born to Pāṇḍu would fulfill?
options: To protect religion and chastise the wicked
To rule the entire world as a single monarch
To perform the great horse sacrifice
questions.correct_answer: To protect religion and chastise the wicked
questions.explanation: Indra promised to give Pāṇḍu a son who would protect religion and chastise the wicked.

1.2.54.paragraph_text: Following Pāṇḍu’s request, Kuntī again chanted her mantra. Indra appeared immediately, his bodily luster lighting up the whole region. By the power of the thousand-eyed Indra, Kuntī conceived, and in time a dark-complexioned child was born. For the third time the heavenly voice was heard, resounding over the mountainside: “O Kuntī, this child will be equal in strength to Indra and indeed Śiva himself. He shall be called Arjuna and he will spread your fame everywhere. He will subjugate many powerful kings and greatly increase the prosperity of your dynasty. Agni, Śiva and Indra will all be gratified by this boy’s service. He shall have no equal in prowess and will be famous throughout the three worlds.”

questions.question: Which deity manifested to Kuntī after she chanted her mantra to grant her a son?
questions.explanation: The text states that following Pāṇḍu’s request, Kuntī chanted her mantra and Indra appeared immediately.

questions.question: What name was bestowed upon the child born to Kuntī through the power of Indra?
questions.explanation: The heavenly voice declared that the child would be called Arjuna.

questions.question: To which two deities was the child Arjuna compared in terms of strength?
questions.explanation: The heavenly voice proclaimed that the child would be equal in strength to Indra and Śiva.

questions.question: Which three deities does the text specifically mention will be gratified by the service of the child Arjuna?
questions.explanation: The prophecy states that Agni, Śiva, and Indra will all be gratified by the boy's service.

1.2.55.paragraph_text: Overhearing this as well as other prophecies regarding Arjuna’s future greatness, the ṛṣis on that mountain were filled with joy. They uttered blessings while the celestial drums reverberated and showers of flowers fell from the sky. Many gods and heavenly sages appeared before Kuntī and offered the child benediction. Only the ascetic ṛṣis could see them as they appeared in their ethereal forms, standing in chariots and on mountain peaks. The ṛṣis were astonished by that wonderful sight and they stood with folded palms.

questions.question: Which specific group of individuals was uniquely capable of perceiving the gods and heavenly sages as they appeared in their ethereal forms?
options: The residents of the nearby mountain villages
questions.explanation: The text states that only the ascetic ṛṣis could see the gods and heavenly sages as they appeared in their ethereal forms.

questions.question: What reaction did the ascetic ṛṣis exhibit when observing the appearance of the gods and heavenly sages in their ethereal forms?
options: They fled in fear from the celestial light
They stood with folded palms and were astonished
They recited the Veda in unison
They knelt and touched the feet of the gods
questions.correct_answer: They stood with folded palms and were astonished
questions.explanation: The text describes the ṛṣis as being astonished by the sight and standing with folded palms.

questions.question: What celestial phenomena accompanied the prophecies regarding the future greatness of Arjuna?
options: Reverberating drums and showers of flowers
The appearance of a meteor shower
questions.correct_answer: Reverberating drums and showers of flowers
questions.explanation: The text mentions that celestial drums reverberated and showers of flowers fell from the sky upon the prophecies of Arjuna's future greatness.

1.2.56.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu was delighted. He now had three matchless sons. Seeing the wonderful result of Durvāsā’s boon, he decided to ask Kuntī to use the mantra a fourth time and summon yet another powerful god. When he approached her again, however, Kuntī refused. “O learned man, even in times of emergency it is never sanctioned by scripture for a woman to have intercourse with more than three men. Have you forgot the ordinance? I would become debased if I again conceived by another.”

questions.question: How many sons did Pāṇḍu possess after Kuntī utilized the boon granted by Durvāsā?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu was delighted because he now had three matchless sons.

questions.question: What limit did Kuntī cite regarding the number of men a woman is permitted to conceive children with, even during emergencies?
questions.explanation: Kuntī informs Pāṇḍu that according to scriptural ordinance, it is not sanctioned for a woman to have intercourse with more than three men, even in times of emergency.

questions.question: What specific consequence did Kuntī fear would happen to her if she conceived a child by a fourth man?
options: She would lose her divine boon
The child would be born cursed
She would be exiled from the forest
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly warns Pāṇḍu that she would become debased if she again conceived by another man after the limit of three.

1.2.57.paragraph_text: The king fell silent. Kuntī was right. He decided not to ask for any more sons and continued to live peacefully in the forest with his two wives. Their three sons grew luxuriantly in their care.

questions.question: How many wives lived with King Pāṇḍu in the forest while raising their sons?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that King Pāṇḍu continued to live peacefully in the forest with his two wives.

1.2.58.paragraph_text: Then one day Mādrī approached Pāṇḍu privately. “My lord, I do not complain that you treat me less favorably than Kuntī. Even though I am of a higher birth, I still have no complaint. My one grief is that you have no sons by me.”

questions.question: Which wife of Pāṇḍu approached him privately to express her grief over being childless?
questions.explanation: Mādrī approached Pāṇḍu privately to express her sorrow about not having sons.

questions.question: What claim regarding her own social status did Mādrī make to Pāṇḍu during their private conversation?
options: She was of a higher birth than Kuntī.
She was the elder queen of the Kuru dynasty.
She was of equal birth to Gāndhārī.
She was a princess from a rival kingdom.
questions.correct_answer: She was of a higher birth than Kuntī.
questions.explanation: Mādrī explicitly mentions to Pāṇḍu that she is of a higher birth than Kuntī.

1.2.59.paragraph_text: Mādrī found her position unbearable. No princesses in her line had ever gone without issue. Kuntī had three sons. Mādrī had heard that Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s wife, Gāndhārī, had given birth to a hundred sons in Hastināpura. Yet she, the lawful wife of a great hero, was childless. She asked Pāṇḍu to request Kuntī to teach her the mantra. Mādrī explained that she was too shy to ask her directly, since Kuntī was her rival.

questions.question: How many sons had Gāndhārī, the wife of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, given birth to in Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Gāndhārī had given birth to a hundred sons in Hastināpura.

questions.question: What specific request did Mādrī make to Pāṇḍu regarding Kuntī?
options: To banish Kuntī from the household
To adopt one of Kuntī's three sons
To ask Kuntī to teach her the mantra
To mediate a dispute regarding lineage rank
questions.correct_answer: To ask Kuntī to teach her the mantra
questions.explanation: Mādrī asked Pāṇḍu to request that Kuntī teach her the mantra so that she might also have children.

questions.question: Why was Mādrī reluctant to approach Kuntī personally to learn the mantra?
options: She feared Kuntī would refuse due to their rivalry
She was forbidden from speaking to Kuntī by Pāṇḍu
She did not believe Kuntī possessed the knowledge
She was too shy to ask her directly as a rival
questions.correct_answer: She was too shy to ask her directly as a rival
questions.explanation: Mādrī explained to Pāṇḍu that she was too shy to ask Kuntī for the mantra directly, given their status as rivals.

questions.question: How many sons does the text state that Kuntī possessed at the time Mādrī felt her position was unbearable?
questions.explanation: The text notes that Kuntī had three sons, contrasting this with Mādrī's childless status.

1.2.60.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu smiled. He felt compassion for his younger wife. “O Mādrī, I had already been considering this myself. I was reluctant to ask you because I was not sure how you might feel. Now that I know your mind, I shall certainly speak with Kuntī. I do not think she will refuse.”

questions.question: Whom did Pāṇḍu intend to approach regarding the concerns expressed by Mādrī?
