questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu explicitly states that he intends to speak with Kuntī to address the concerns raised by his wife, Mādrī.

questions.question: What reason did Pāṇḍu provide for not having previously asked Kuntī to assist Mādrī?
options: He feared Kuntī would refuse the request.
He was unsure of Mādrī's feelings on the matter.
He believed it was not his place to intervene.
He had already been blessed with enough sons.
questions.correct_answer: He was unsure of Mādrī's feelings on the matter.
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu states that he had been considering the request himself but was reluctant to act because he was not sure how Mādrī might feel.

1.2.61.paragraph_text: The monarch approached Kuntī immediately. He told her of Mādrī’s sorrow and asked her to be gracious to her co-wife. Pāṇḍu made it clear that he also desired more sons to secure more fully the good of his race, including that of himself and indeed Kuntī.

questions.question: Which co-wife's sorrow did Pāṇḍu ask Kuntī to be gracious toward?
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu approached Kuntī to ask her to be gracious toward the sorrow of her co-wife, Mādrī.

questions.question: Why did Pāṇḍu explicitly desire more sons?
options: To secure the good of his race and himself
To fulfill a prophecy given by a sage
To honor the memory of his ancestors
questions.correct_answer: To secure the good of his race and himself
questions.explanation: Pāṇḍu stated that he desired more sons to secure the good of his race, including the good of himself and Kuntī.

1.2.62.paragraph_text: Kuntī assented to her husband’s request and told Mādrī, “O gentle lady, I shall recite the mantra for your benefit. Please think of some deity from whom you desire offspring.”

questions.question: What role did Kuntī agree to perform in order to assist Mādrī in obtaining offspring?
options: She agreed to perform a sacrificial ritual for Mādrī
She agreed to recite a mantra for Mādrī
She agreed to travel to a holy forest to pray
She agreed to petition the gods on Mādrī's behalf
questions.correct_answer: She agreed to recite a mantra for Mādrī
questions.explanation: Kuntī tells Mādrī that she will recite the mantra for her benefit so that she may have children.

questions.question: What instructions did Kuntī give to Mādrī regarding the choice of a deity?
options: To select a deity based on the recommendation of the royal priests
To think of a deity from whom she desired offspring
To wait for a sign from the gods before making a choice
To choose only from the gods of the wind and fire
questions.correct_answer: To think of a deity from whom she desired offspring
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly instructs Mādrī to think of a deity from whom she desires offspring.

1.2.63.paragraph_text: Mādrī thought carefully. Most probably Kuntī would allow her to use the mantra only once. If she summoned the inseparable twin Aśvinī gods, she would get two sons at once. Even as she thought of the two gods they appeared before her, aglow with celestial splendor. Bashfully, she asked that they unite with her to conceive children and they immediately agreed. They begot upon Mādrī two boys of incomparable beauty who were named Nakula and Sahadeva. Upon their birth the divine voice said, “These virtuous and accomplished sons will transcend in energy and beauty even their celestial fathers.”

questions.question: Which celestial beings did Mādrī choose to summon to conceive her sons?
questions.explanation: Mādrī decided to summon the inseparable twin Aśvinī gods to gain two sons at once.

questions.question: What were the names given to the two sons born to Mādrī through the power of the mantra?
questions.explanation: The two boys begotten upon Mādrī by the Aśvinī gods were named Nakula and Sahadeva.

questions.question: What prophecy was delivered by the divine voice upon the birth of Nakula and Sahadeva?
options: They would be the greatest warriors in the world
They would rule the kingdom for a thousand years
They would transcend their celestial fathers in energy and beauty
They would be the protectors of the Vedic knowledge
questions.correct_answer: They would transcend their celestial fathers in energy and beauty
questions.explanation: A divine voice announced at their birth that Nakula and Sahadeva would exceed their celestial fathers in both energy and beauty.

questions.question: Why did Mādrī specifically choose to invoke the twin Aśvinī gods?
options: She hoped for a daughter instead of sons
She wanted to be granted eternal youth
She wanted two sons at once since the mantra was likely only available to her once
She was advised by the ṛṣis to choose them for longevity
questions.correct_answer: She wanted two sons at once since the mantra was likely only available to her once
questions.explanation: Mādrī reasoned that by summoning the twin Aśvinī gods, she could conceive two sons at once because she suspected Kuntī would limit her use of the mantra to a single time.

1.2.64.paragraph_text: The ṛṣis performed all the rites of passage for the five boys and they grew up swiftly. Even at age one, they appeared as if they were five years old. Pāṇḍu was overwhelmed with happiness at seeing his sons’ extraordinary strength, beauty, energy and wisdom. All the boys became favorites of the ṛṣis and they sported like five Gandharvas descended to earth.

questions.question: What was the physical appearance of the five boys at the age of one?
options: They appeared to be five years old
They had the stature of adolescent boys
They were indistinguishable from fully grown men
questions.correct_answer: They appeared to be five years old
questions.explanation: The text states that even at age one, the boys appeared as if they were five years old.

questions.question: To which celestial beings were the five boys compared as they sported?
questions.explanation: The text notes that all the boys became favorites of the ṛṣis and sported like five Gandharvas descended to earth.

questions.question: Who performed the rites of passage for the five boys?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the ṛṣis performed all the rites of passage for the five boys.

1.2.65.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu wondered if he might again have another son through Mādrī. But when he asked Kuntī if she would permit Mādrī to use the mantra again, Kuntī became angry and replied, “My lord, I was deceived by that wicked woman. Although I gave her the mantra only once, she obtained two sons. If I give it to her again I fear she may surpass me in the number of children. Pray do not ask me for this favor any more.”

questions.question: Why did Kuntī refuse to allow Mādrī to use the mantra again for a second time?
options: She feared that Mādrī would have more sons than her
She believed the mantra would lose its efficacy
She was instructed by the ṛṣis to limit the number of births
She felt that five sons were sufficient for the dynasty
questions.correct_answer: She feared that Mādrī would have more sons than her
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly states that if she allows Mādrī to use the mantra again, she fears that Mādrī will surpass her in the total number of children.

questions.question: How many sons did Mādrī obtain after Kuntī gave her the mantra a single time?
questions.explanation: Kuntī mentions that even though she gave Mādrī the mantra only once, the woman managed to obtain two sons.

questions.question: What accusation did Kuntī make against Mādrī regarding the use of the mantra?
options: That she had stolen the mantra
That she had tricked her into giving it away
That she was a wicked woman who had deceived her
That she was unfit to raise children
questions.correct_answer: That she was a wicked woman who had deceived her
questions.explanation: Kuntī refers to Mādrī as a wicked woman and claims that she was deceived by her when she gave her the mantra previously.

1.2.66.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu reconciled himself to the fact that he would have only five sons, but he was not disappointed. His boys grew up like gods. They were all as handsome as the moon-god, Soma, and as powerful as Indra. They all became fearless bowmen who were capable, even in their early youth, of facing furious mountain lions. The ṛṣis schooled them in every facet of Vedic knowledge. The ascetics were astonished to see the boys growing so rapidly, like lotuses blooming in a lake. Pāṇḍu and his wives offered prayers of thanks to Lord Viṣṇu for their great fortune, and they went on living in the Himālayan region, raising their children with affection.

questions.question: To which two deities were the five sons of Pāṇḍu compared in terms of their physical appearance and power?
questions.explanation: The text states the boys were as handsome as the moon-god, Soma, and as powerful as Indra.

questions.question: What specific animal were the young sons of Pāṇḍu capable of facing even during their early youth?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly notes the boys were capable of facing furious mountain lions in their early youth.

questions.question: Which deity did Pāṇḍu and his wives offer prayers of thanks to for their great fortune in raising their children?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Pāṇḍu and his wives offered prayers of thanks to Lord Viṣṇu.

questions.question: In what geographical region did Pāṇḍu and his wives reside while raising their five sons?
questions.explanation: The text states they went on living in the Himālayan region while raising their children.

questions.question: Who was responsible for schooling the sons of Pāṇḍu in every facet of Vedic knowledge?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that the ṛṣis schooled the boys in every facet of Vedic knowledge.

1.3.1.paragraph_text: In Hastināpura, Dhṛtarāṣṭra had performed five sacrifices with the wealth Pāṇḍu had bestowed upon him. He gave charity to hundreds of thousands of Brahmins and satisfied the gods with his offerings. The kingdom flourished and the citizens had everything they desired. They were devoted to virtue, sacrifice and truth. Bearing love and affection for one another, they grew in prosperity. Hastināpura was like the ocean filled with numerous palaces and mansions. There were golden arches and crystal fountains everywhere. Between broad, paved highways lay beautiful gardens and the air was filled with the sweet fragrance of blossoming trees. The clatter of horses’ hooves and chariot wheels mixed with the trumpeting of elephants and the blare of conchshells which mark the beginning of sacrificial performances. Holy chants uttered by numerous Brahmins emanated from tall and exquisitely carved temple buildings, which graced the city in their thousands. Hastināpura appeared like Indra’s celestial capital, and each day dozens of feudal kings would arrive bringing tribute.

questions.question: How many sacrifices did Dhṛtarāṣṭra perform using the wealth provided by Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra performed five sacrifices with the wealth Pāṇḍu had bestowed upon him.

questions.question: To which celestial city is the appearance of Hastināpura compared in the account of its prosperity?
questions.explanation: The text states that Hastināpura appeared like Indra’s celestial capital.

questions.question: What primary group received charity from Dhṛtarāṣṭra during his time of prosperity in Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Dhṛtarāṣṭra gave charity to hundreds of thousands of Brahmins.

1.3.2.paragraph_text: Assisted by Bhīṣma, Dhṛtarāṣṭra ruled over the kingdom on Pāṇḍu’s behalf. After hearing of Pāṇḍu cursed and his subsequent retirement, Dhṛtarāṣṭra, despite his blindness, was officially installed as monarch. He thought sadly of his brother and missed him greatly, but in his absence he competently managed all the affairs of state.

questions.question: Who provided assistance to Dhṛtarāṣṭra during his rule over the kingdom?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra was assisted by Bhīṣma while ruling the kingdom.

questions.question: On whose behalf was Dhṛtarāṣṭra managing the affairs of the kingdom?
questions.explanation: The text notes that Dhṛtarāṣṭra ruled over the kingdom on Pāṇḍu’s behalf.

questions.question: What primary reason led to Pāṇḍu's retirement according to the information provided?
questions.explanation: The text mentions Dhṛtarāṣṭra heard of Pāṇḍu being cursed and his subsequent retirement.

questions.question: What physical condition of Dhṛtarāṣṭra is specifically mentioned at the time of his installation as monarch?
questions.explanation: The text describes Dhṛtarāṣṭra as being officially installed as monarch despite his blindness.

1.3.3.paragraph_text: It once came to pass that Vyāsadeva arrived hungry and thirsty at Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s palace. Gāndhārī attended to him conscientiously. Vyāsadeva was pleased with the girl and blessed her, “You shall soon have one hundred sons as powerful as your husband.” In due course of time Gāndhārī conceived. For two years she bore the embryo within her womb, becoming increasingly anxious. Then one day news reached her that Kuntī had given birth in the forest to a boy as effulgent as the morning sun. Out of frustration and anger at her own excessively long gestation, she struck violently at her womb. She then brought forth a hard mass of flesh that resembled an iron ball. As her nurses informed her of the stillbirth, she was afflicted by grief and thought of Vyāsadeva and his boon.

questions.question: What blessing did the sage Vyāsadeva bestow upon Gāndhārī for her dedicated service?
options: That she would bear one hundred sons as powerful as her husband
That she would bear a single son who would rule the world
That her husband would regain his vision
That her children would be eternally immortal
questions.correct_answer: That she would bear one hundred sons as powerful as her husband
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva was pleased with Gāndhārī's service and blessed her that she would have one hundred sons as powerful as her husband.

questions.question: For what duration did Gāndhārī carry the embryo in her womb before resorting to a violent act?
questions.explanation: Gāndhārī bore the embryo within her womb for two years before becoming anxious and striking her womb.

questions.question: What specific news caused Gāndhārī to feel frustration and anger regarding her own pregnancy?
options: That Dhṛtarāṣṭra had taken another wife
That Kuntī had given birth to a son in the forest
That the kingdom was under attack
That the sage Vyāsadeva had revoked his blessing
questions.correct_answer: That Kuntī had given birth to a son in the forest
questions.explanation: Upon hearing the news that Kuntī had given birth to a boy in the forest, Gāndhārī felt frustration and anger at her own long gestation.

questions.question: What did Gāndhārī produce from her womb after striking it in frustration?
options: A perfectly healthy set of twins
A hard mass of flesh resembling an iron ball
questions.correct_answer: A hard mass of flesh resembling an iron ball
questions.explanation: After striking her womb, Gāndhārī brought forth a hard mass of flesh that resembled an iron ball.

1.3.4.paragraph_text: At once the sage appeared before her and said, “What have you done?”

1.3.5.paragraph_text: Gāndhārī told him how she had become overwhelmed with envy and frustration when she had heard of Kuntī giving birth to Yudhiṣṭhira. With tears in her eyes she said, “I struck my womb and this lump of flesh came out. What then was the meaning of your boon?”

questions.question: What specifically caused Gāndhārī to feel intense envy and frustration?
options: Hearing about the birth of Yudhiṣṭhira to Kuntī
The death of her unborn child
The departure of Vyāsadeva to his ashram
The failure of her husband to support her
questions.correct_answer: Hearing about the birth of Yudhiṣṭhira to Kuntī
questions.explanation: Gāndhārī explicitly states that she became overwhelmed with envy and frustration upon hearing of Kuntī giving birth to Yudhiṣṭhira.

questions.question: How did Gāndhārī physically react in her frustration leading to the emergence of a lump of flesh?
options: She prayed for a miracle from Vyāsadeva
She refused to eat for many days
She performed a penance in the forest
questions.explanation: Gāndhārī explains that she struck her womb, which resulted in the lump of flesh being expelled.

1.3.6.paragraph_text: Vyāsadeva replied that his words could never prove false. He asked the servants to bring one hundred one pots filled with ghee. He then sprinkled cool water on the lump of flesh and it gradually divided into one hundred and one parts, each the size of a thumb. These were placed in the pots which were then sealed and placed in a concealed spot. Vyāsadeva instructed that the pots should be opened only after two more years had passed. He then departed for his lonely mountain ashram.

questions.question: What quantity of ghee-filled pots did Vyāsadeva order his servants to prepare?
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva requested that the servants bring one hundred and one pots filled with ghee to facilitate the division of the lump of flesh.

questions.question: How did Vyāsadeva initiate the process of dividing the lump of flesh into individual parts?
options: He chanted sacred mantras over the lump
He used a sharp blade to cut it
He sprinkled cool water on the lump
He placed it in a bed of hot ashes
questions.correct_answer: He sprinkled cool water on the lump
questions.explanation: The text states that Vyāsadeva sprinkled cool water on the lump of flesh, which caused it to divide into one hundred and one parts.

questions.question: What was the size of each of the pieces into which the lump of flesh was divided?
options: The size of a mustard seed
The size of a newborn's palm
questions.explanation: After the application of water, the lump divided into one hundred and one parts, each specifically noted to be the size of a thumb.

questions.question: How long did Vyāsadeva instruct that the sealed pots remain unopened?
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva gave specific instructions that the sealed pots were to be opened only after two years had passed.

questions.question: Where did Vyāsadeva travel after completing the preparations for the pots?
options: To the royal palace in Hastināpura
To the banks of the Sarasvatī River
questions.explanation: The passage states that after providing his instructions, Vyāsadeva departed for his lonely mountain ashram.

1.3.7.paragraph_text: Exactly after the two years had elapsed the pots were opened one by one. From the first came a child who was named Duryodhana. At the moment he was brought out of the pot the sound of braying asses and screaming vultures was heard. Jackals howled and the wind blew fiercely. Without any apparent cause, fires sprang up all around the city and raged in all directions.

questions.question: How long after being sealed in pots were the contents opened to reveal the first child?
questions.explanation: The text states the pots were opened exactly after two years had elapsed.

questions.question: What was the name of the child who emerged from the first pot?
questions.explanation: The text identifies the child born from the first pot as Duryodhana.

questions.question: Which animals were heard braying and screaming, respectively, at the moment of Duryodhana's birth?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions the sound of braying asses (donkeys) and screaming vultures occurring at the moment of his birth.

questions.question: What environmental phenomena were observed spontaneously upon the emergence of the first child?
options: A solar eclipse and falling stars
questions.explanation: The text notes that fierce winds blew and fires sprang up all around the city.

1.3.8.paragraph_text: The frightened King Dhṛtarāṣṭra summoned the Brahmins, Bhīṣma, Vidura and other ministers and counselors. He asked them the meaning of the omens. “The eldest of the princes is undoubtedly Yudhiṣṭhira and he should inherit the kingdom. I do not dispute that. But will my own son become the king after him? O wise ones, please tell me what is right and lawful.”

questions.question: Which specific groups did King Dhṛtarāṣṭra summon to interpret the ominous signs surrounding his son's birth?
options: Brahmins, Bhīṣma, Vidura, and other ministers and counselors
Kshatriya warriors, palace guards, and the royal priests
The elders of the Kuru dynasty and the city merchants
The royal astrologers and the commanders of the army
questions.correct_answer: Brahmins, Bhīṣma, Vidura, and other ministers and counselors
questions.explanation: King Dhṛtarāṣṭra gathered these specific individuals to understand the meaning of the omens he witnessed.

questions.question: Whom does King Dhṛtarāṣṭra explicitly acknowledge as the rightful heir to the kingdom?
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra states that Yudhiṣṭhira is the eldest of the princes and should inherit the kingdom.

questions.question: What primary concern does King Dhṛtarāṣṭra express regarding the future of his own son?
options: Whether his son will be trained in the arts of warfare by Bhīṣma
Whether his son will lawfully become the king after Yudhiṣṭhira
Whether the omens predict the immediate death of his son
Whether his son will be accepted as a prince by the other ministers
questions.correct_answer: Whether his son will lawfully become the king after Yudhiṣṭhira
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra asks the council if his son will ascend the throne following Yudhiṣṭhira's reign.

1.3.9.paragraph_text: As Dhṛtarāṣṭra spoke the terrible sounds began again from all sides. Hearing this, Vidura replied to the king, “When these omens are seen at the birth of a child, it is evident that he will be the exterminator of his race. Our prosperity and future depend upon his being abandoned. Do not hesitate, O King. This child must be cast away at once.”

questions.question: What prediction did Vidura make regarding the child whose birth was accompanied by terrible omens?
options: He would bring eternal peace to the Kuru kingdom.
He would be the exterminator of his race.
He would become a great sage and renounce the throne.
He would be a wise and just king like his ancestors.
questions.correct_answer: He would be the exterminator of his race.
questions.explanation: Vidura explicitly stated that the omens seen at the birth of the child indicate that he will be the exterminator of his race.

questions.question: What action did Vidura advise King Dhṛtarāṣṭra to take regarding the newborn child?
options: To perform a purification ritual immediately.
To exile the child to the forest.
To cast the child away at once.
To raise the child in secret away from the palace.
questions.correct_answer: To cast the child away at once.
questions.explanation: Vidura advised the king that the child must be cast away at once because the family's prosperity and future depend on it.

questions.question: According to Vidura, what depends upon the immediate abandonment of the newborn child?
options: The expansion of the kingdom's borders.
The prosperity and future of the family.
The success of the upcoming harvest.
questions.correct_answer: The prosperity and future of the family.
questions.explanation: Vidura stated that the prosperity and future of their lineage depend upon the child being abandoned.

1.3.10.paragraph_text: Vidura told Dhṛtarāṣṭra that he would still have ninety-nine other sons. There would be no sin in abandoning this child, as the scriptures clearly state that an individual can be abandoned for the sake of a family. Indeed, a family can be abandoned for the sake of a village, a village for the sake of a city and the world itself can be abandoned for the sake of the soul.

questions.question: How many other sons did Vidura indicate Dhṛtarāṣṭra would still possess?
questions.explanation: Vidura explicitly stated that Dhṛtarāṣṭra would still have ninety-nine other sons despite the birth of the current child.

questions.question: According to the principles cited by Vidura, what is the proper order of prioritization for abandoning entities for the sake of higher interests?
questions.explanation: The text lists a hierarchy where an individual is abandoned for a family, a family for a village, a village for a city, and the world for the soul.

questions.question: What entity does the text state can be abandoned for the sake of the soul?
questions.explanation: The passage states that the world itself can be abandoned for the sake of the soul.

1.3.11.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra was unable to accept Vidura’s counsel. He could not allow his son to be cast away. He shook his head slowly and said nothing in reply. As the nurse stood by holding the baby, the king waved her toward Gāndhārī and the child was handed to her. Bhīṣma and Vidura looked at one another but said nothing. Dhṛtarāṣṭra was the monarch; his word was final.

questions.question: Who advised Dhṛtarāṣṭra that his child should be cast away?
questions.explanation: The text states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra was unable to accept Vidura's counsel regarding the casting away of his son.

questions.question: What was the final decision regarding the baby once Dhṛtarāṣṭra rejected Vidura's advice?
options: The baby was given to the nurse
The baby was abandoned in the forest
The baby was handed to Gāndhārī
The baby was sent to Bhīṣma
questions.correct_answer: The baby was handed to Gāndhārī
questions.explanation: After Dhṛtarāṣṭra waved the nurse toward Gāndhārī, the child was handed to her.

questions.question: Which two individuals observed the king's decision to keep the child without offering a verbal response?
questions.explanation: The text notes that Bhīṣma and Vidura looked at one another but said nothing after the king made his final decision.

1.3.12.paragraph_text: Over the course of the next month, all the pots were opened and one hundred boys and one girl were brought out. All the boys bore the signs of being great warriors and rulers. The king and queen rejoiced in their good fortune. Dismissing the evil omens, they dwelt contentedly in Hastināpura, attended by Bhīṣma and Vidura.

questions.question: How many children, in total, were born from the pots following the month-long period in Hastināpura?
options: One hundred boys and one girl
questions.correct_answer: One hundred boys and one girl
questions.explanation: The text states that after the pots were opened, one hundred boys and one girl were brought out.

questions.question: Who attended the king and queen in Hastināpura after the birth of their children?
questions.explanation: The paragraph explicitly mentions that the king and queen dwelt in Hastināpura, attended by Bhīṣma and Vidura.

questions.question: What traits were identified in the hundred boys born in Hastināpura?
options: They were destined to be scholars and sages
They were born as divine incarnations
They bore the signs of being great warriors and rulers
They were identified as masters of the mystic arts
questions.correct_answer: They bore the signs of being great warriors and rulers
questions.explanation: The text notes that all the boys bore the signs of being great warriors and rulers.

1.3.13.paragraph_text: On Saptasrota Mountain, spring had arrived. Pāṇḍu, surrounded by his five youthful sons, felt young again. He sported with the boys in the hills, scaling rock faces and challenging lions in their caves. They dived and played in mountain pools and climbed tall trees. Wrestling and laughing together, they rolled about the soft grassy slopes.

questions.question: On which mountain did Pāṇḍu reside with his five sons during the spring season?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu and his five sons were on Saptasrota Mountain when spring arrived.

questions.question: Which activities did Pāṇḍu engage in with his sons while on Saptasrota Mountain?
options: Scaling rock faces and challenging lions
Building huts and training with weapons
Hunting deer and gathering wild herbs
questions.correct_answer: Scaling rock faces and challenging lions
questions.explanation: The text describes Pāṇḍu and his sons sporting by scaling rock faces and challenging lions in their caves.

1.3.14.paragraph_text: One evening Pāṇḍu went into the woods with Mādrī to fetch roots and fruits for their evening meal. As they strolled, they saw countless varieties of blossoming trees and bushes. The air was heavy with scent and the sounds of cuckoos and other birds filled the air. Black bees swarmed about the many-colored flowers that surrounded lakes overgrown with lotuses. The celestial atmosphere awoke romance in Pāṇḍu’s heart. He gazed at the beautiful Mādrī, his mind influenced by Cupid. It was a hot day and Mādrī was clad in a long silk robe. In the bright sunshine, Pāṇḍu could see the outline of her exquisite form, which resembled that of a goddess. The soft breeze made her dress cling to her body, revealing her slender waist and firm, round breasts.

questions.question: What activity was Pāṇḍu engaged in with Mādrī in the woods when he was struck by romantic feelings?
options: Collecting roots and fruits for their evening meal
questions.correct_answer: Collecting roots and fruits for their evening meal
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu went into the woods with Mādrī to fetch roots and fruits for their evening meal.

questions.question: Which deity is identified as having influenced Pāṇḍu's mind when he looked upon Mādrī in the woods?
questions.explanation: The text states that Pāṇḍu gazed at the beautiful Mādrī with his mind influenced by Cupid.

questions.question: What specific item of clothing was Mādrī wearing when Pāṇḍu felt his romantic desire awakened?
questions.explanation: The text describes Mādrī as being clad in a long silk robe during the outing.

1.3.15.paragraph_text: Pāṇḍu could not suppress his desire. He had kept himself in check for twelve years and had not dared to contemplate embracing his wives. Kindama’s curse was always uppermost in his mind and he had resigned himself to practising celibacy with the determination of a ṛṣi. It seemed ages ago when he had been able to enjoy conjugal love with his wives. Now Pāṇḍu again felt those stirrings moving his heart. His mind was confounded and he moved closer to Mādrī, gently placing his hand on her arm.

questions.question: For how long had Pāṇḍu restrained himself from contemplating embracing his wives due to the fear of Kindama’s curse?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu had kept himself in check for twelve years before the current events.

questions.question: With what level of determination had Pāṇḍu been practicing celibacy?
questions.explanation: The text describes Pāṇḍu as practicing celibacy with the determination of a ṛṣi.

questions.question: Which specific figure’s curse was the primary reason Pāṇḍu maintained his celibacy?
questions.explanation: The text states that Kindama’s curse was always uppermost in Pāṇḍu's mind, preventing him from embracing his wives.

1.3.16.paragraph_text: Mādrī at once understood what was in Pāṇḍu’s heart. She was seized by conflicting emotions. How was her lord suddenly approaching her in this way? Out of fear of the curse he had carefully avoided any physical contact with either her or Kuntī for a long time. Although she herself had longed for his embrace, she had scrupulously avoided any situation that might entice her husband. She did not even apply cosmetics or perfumes. But as the powerful Pāṇḍu embraced her, she felt her resolve weaken. Delight and fear seized her heart at once. As her emotions clashed, her mind was petrified. What about the curse! If Pāṇḍu did not stop, he would doubtlessly be destroyed. She had to prevent him.

questions.question: What behavior had Pāṇḍu strictly avoided for a long time due to his fear of a curse?
questions.explanation: The text states that out of fear of the curse, Pāṇḍu had carefully avoided any physical contact with either Mādrī or Kuntī for a long time.

questions.question: To maintain her resolve and avoid enticing Pāṇḍu, what did Mādrī refrain from using?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly notes that Mādrī, in order to avoid enticing her husband, did not even apply cosmetics or perfumes.

questions.question: What was the immediate perceived consequence for Pāṇḍu if he continued his advances toward Mādrī despite the curse?
options: He would be exiled from the forest
He would lose his kingdom forever
He would lose his divine weapons
questions.explanation: The text mentions Mādrī's internal fear that if Pāṇḍu did not stop, he would doubtlessly be destroyed.

1.3.17.paragraph_text: Mādrī tried to push her husband away. Pāṇḍu smiled, incited further by her protests. His mighty arms, deeply tanned and marked with scars from his bowstring, closed around her like serpents. Overpowered by desire, he could not even hear her as she reminded him about the curse. He pressed her lips with his and dropped down to a grassy slope with the beautiful queen held tightly in his arms. Lifting her robes, he pushed himself firmly against her soft body. Mādrī’s struggles only inflamed his passion more.

questions.question: What physical feature marked King Pāṇḍu’s arms, indicative of his life as a warrior?
options: Deep tan and scars from a bowstring
Callouses from years of chariot racing
questions.correct_answer: Deep tan and scars from a bowstring
questions.explanation: The text explicitly describes Pāṇḍu’s arms as being deeply tanned and marked with scars from his bowstring.

questions.question: What was Mādrī’s primary reaction when Pāṇḍu initiated his embrace despite her protests?
options: She attempted to push him away
questions.correct_answer: She attempted to push him away
questions.explanation: The paragraph states that Mādrī tried to push her husband away while protesting.

questions.question: How did Pāṇḍu react to Mādrī’s attempts to remind him of the curse?
options: He became violent and struck her
He stopped immediately out of fear
He was unable to hear her due to overwhelming desire
He laughed at the prospect of the curse
questions.correct_answer: He was unable to hear her due to overwhelming desire
questions.explanation: The text notes that Pāṇḍu was overpowered by desire and could not even hear her as she reminded him of the curse.

1.3.18.paragraph_text: As if impelled by the lord of death himself, the monarch entirely lost his reason under the influence of lust. No thought of Kindama’s curse entered his mind as he lost himself in enjoyment with his fair-skinned wife. Even as he tried to consummate the act with Mādrī, the ṛṣi’s words took effect. Pāṇḍu’s chest was seized with a terrible pain and a shocked look crossed his face. His body stiffened and went slack.

questions.question: Whose curse caused Pāṇḍu to lose his life during his intimate moment with Mādrī?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu lost his reason and failed to recall Kindama’s curse, which took effect during his attempted union with Mādrī.

questions.question: What physical manifestation occurred to Pāṇḍu at the moment the ṛṣi’s curse took effect?
options: His skin turned black as coal
His chest was seized with a terrible pain
He became paralyzed in his lower limbs
questions.correct_answer: His chest was seized with a terrible pain
questions.explanation: The text describes that Pāṇḍu's chest was seized with a terrible pain and his body stiffened before going slack when the curse took effect.

1.3.19.paragraph_text: As Pāṇḍu’s body fell from hers, Mādrī let out a wail of sorrow. Her worst fears had been realized. Despite all her protests she had not been able to prevent her husband’s death. The distraught queen repeatedly embraced her dead husband and lamented loudly.

questions.question: Who was the wife present with King Pāṇḍu at the moment of his death?
questions.explanation: The text identifies that it was Mādrī whose body King Pāṇḍu fell from upon his death.

questions.question: What did Mādrī attempt to do prior to the death of King Pāṇḍu?
options: Seek a boon for the king
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that despite all her protests, Mādrī had not been able to prevent her husband's death.

1.3.20.paragraph_text: Kuntī heard Mādrī’s cries and ran over to where she lay. As Kuntī approached, the tearful Mādrī told her to come alone, without children. Kuntī quickly sent the boys back to the ashram, then rushed to Pāṇḍu’s side. She dropped to the ground. Kuntī realized at once what had happened, and she cried out, “My lord!” Tears welled into her eyes as she turned to Mādrī. “O noble one, how have you allowed this to happen? I was always so careful to protect our lord from this very danger. How did he embrace you, knowing of the ṛṣi’s curse? Why did you tempt him when you were alone together? He was always grave in our company as he thought of Kindama’s words. How did he become so careless?”

questions.question: What specifically did Mādrī request of Kuntī when she arrived at the scene of Pāṇḍu's death?
options: To bring the children to offer final prayers
To come alone, without the children
To call the sages from the ashram
To stay away from the body
questions.correct_answer: To come alone, without the children
questions.explanation: Upon Kuntī's arrival, Mādrī specifically instructed her to approach alone, without the children.

questions.question: Which specific sage is identified as having placed a curse on Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: Kuntī directly mentions Kindama's words when questioning why Pāṇḍu disregarded the ṛṣi’s curse.

questions.question: What did Kuntī believe Pāṇḍu was mindful of while in the company of his wives?
questions.explanation: Kuntī notes that Pāṇḍu was grave in their company because he was thinking of the words of the sage Kindama.

1.3.21.paragraph_text: Mādrī wept, unable to reply. Kuntī tossed about on the ground next to her. The two beautiful ladies embraced their husband’s dead body and shed profuse tears. Kuntī again addressed Mādrī. “O princess of Madra, you are surely more fortunate than I, for you saw our lord’s face light up with joy as he approached you in this lonely place.”

questions.question: Which kingdom is Mādrī associated with by title?
questions.explanation: Kuntī addresses Mādrī as the princess of Madra in the text.

1.3.22.paragraph_text: Gathering her senses, Mādrī replied, “O sister, with tears in my eyes I tried to resist him, yet he could not control himself. He seemed bent on fulfilling the ṛṣi’s curse.”

questions.question: What reason does Mādrī provide for the King's inability to control himself despite her resistance?
options: The fulfillment of a ṛṣi’s curse
A temporary loss of sanity due to grief
The influence of a powerful demon
questions.correct_answer: The fulfillment of a ṛṣi’s curse
questions.explanation: Mādrī explains that the King could not control himself because he was bent on fulfilling the ṛṣi’s curse.

1.3.23.paragraph_text: Kuntī prayed for strength. Somehow this was God’s arrangement. The ways of Providence were always mysterious. But what should be done now? What was her duty? She gently stroked the head of her younger co-wife and said, “O Mādrī, there is only one recourse for me. Please allow me to ascend to heaven with our lord. Rise up and raise our children. Give me the body and, tightly embracing it, I shall enter fire.”

questions.question: What choice does Kuntī propose to Mādrī as her only recourse following the death of their lord?
options: To remain in the palace and raise the children alone
To ascend to heaven with their lord
To travel to a forest to seek penance
To abdicate the throne in favor of the eldest son
questions.correct_answer: To ascend to heaven with their lord
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly states to Mādrī that there is only one recourse for her, which is to ascend to heaven with their lord.

questions.question: What specific duty does Kuntī assign to Mādrī regarding the children?
options: To send the children to the care of their elders
To teach the children the ways of the ṛṣis
To rise up and raise the children
To protect the children from the curse of the ṛṣi
questions.correct_answer: To rise up and raise the children
questions.explanation: Kuntī instructs Mādrī to rise up and fulfill the duty of raising their children.

questions.question: How does Kuntī intend to depart after receiving the body of their lord?
options: By walking into the deep forest
By entering fire while embracing the body
By performing a final sacrifice at the altar
questions.correct_answer: By entering fire while embracing the body
questions.explanation: Kuntī declares that after being given the body and tightly embracing it, she shall enter fire.

1.3.24.paragraph_text: Mādrī shook her head. She pleaded with Kuntī, “The monarch died as he approached me for intercourse. His desire was not satiated. Should it not be I who goes to the region of the dead to gratify him? I am still clasping him even now and will not allow him to go without me. O Kuntī, please let me go!”

questions.question: What reason did Mādrī provide for why she, rather than Kuntī, should accompany the deceased monarch to the region of the dead?
options: She was the elder queen by birthright
The monarch died while seeking intercourse with her, leaving his desire unfulfilled
Kuntī had already refused to ascend the funeral pyre
The gods had commanded her specifically to perform the sacrifice
questions.correct_answer: The monarch died while seeking intercourse with her, leaving his desire unfulfilled
questions.explanation: Mādrī argues that because the king died while approaching her, his desire remained unsatisfied, and she should go to the region of the dead to gratify him.

questions.question: What physical action did Mādrī describe to emphasize her bond with the deceased monarch at the moment of his death?
options: She was clasping him at the time of his passing
She had wrapped him in sacred silk cloths
She was holding his royal scepter
She was anointing his body with sacred oils
questions.correct_answer: She was clasping him at the time of his passing
questions.explanation: Mādrī states she is still clasping the king even now, using this as a justification for her desire to follow him into death.

1.3.25.paragraph_text: Mādrī felt incapable of bringing up the children by herself. Kuntī would be a far better mother. Mādrī had seen her gentle dealings with the boys. She would surely treat them all equally. Mādrī begged Kuntī’s permission to enter the fire with Pāṇḍu. “O Kuntī, the king sought me with desire. Grant me leave to fulfill that desire. You would be doing me the greatest good. I shall leave my sons in your care without any fear.”

questions.question: Why did Mādrī believe Kuntī would be a superior guardian for the children?
options: Kuntī had more financial resources to support the boys
Mādrī felt incapable of raising them alone and had observed Kuntī's gentle dealings with them
Kuntī had explicitly promised Pāṇḍu she would protect them
Mādrī was instructed by the elders to relinquish custody to Kuntī
questions.correct_answer: Mādrī felt incapable of raising them alone and had observed Kuntī's gentle dealings with them
questions.explanation: The text states Mādrī felt incapable of bringing up the children by herself and noted that she had seen Kuntī's gentle dealings with the boys.

questions.question: What primary reason did Mādrī provide to Kuntī for requesting permission to enter the funeral fire with Pāṇḍu?
options: She felt it was her duty as the younger wife to follow the king
She wished to escape the responsibility of raising the children
She felt a need to fulfill Pāṇḍu's desire, as he had sought her with desire when he died
She believed it was the only way to ensure the children would inherit the kingdom
questions.correct_answer: She felt a need to fulfill Pāṇḍu's desire, as he had sought her with desire when he died
questions.explanation: Mādrī begged Kuntī for permission, stating that the king had sought her with desire and she wished to fulfill that desire.

1.3.26.paragraph_text: Kuntī looked compassionately upon Mādrī as she lay with her arms around Pāṇḍu and bathing him with her tears. The noble Kuntī felt her heart torn. Although as the elder wife it was her privilege to choose to follow the king to the next world, how could she deny Mādrī? It was to her that Pāṇḍu had been attracted. If Mādrī were to live, she would live with the memory of that brief and terrible moment all her life. Guilt would consume her, along with the anguish of being unable to fulfill her lord’s last desire. Although she longed to follow her husband, Kuntī could not be so cruel to her co-wife. She touched Mādrī gently and said, “So be it.” Kuntī then went with a heavy heart toward the ashram.

questions.question: Which wife of Pāṇḍu held the position of the elder wife with the traditional privilege of following him in death?
questions.explanation: The text identifies Kuntī as the elder wife who possessed the privilege to choose to follow Pāṇḍu to the next world.

questions.question: What reason prompted Kuntī to grant Mādrī permission to follow Pāṇḍu in death?
options: Mādrī was the mother of the eldest prince
Kuntī feared the responsibility of raising all the boys alone
Pāṇḍu had been specifically attracted to Mādrī, and she would suffer guilt and anguish if she lived
Mādrī was the elder wife and held the primary claim
questions.correct_answer: Pāṇḍu had been specifically attracted to Mādrī, and she would suffer guilt and anguish if she lived
questions.explanation: Kuntī realized that Pāṇḍu had been attracted to Mādrī and that forcing her to live would leave her with the lifelong memory of her husband's death and a sense of guilt.

1.3.27.paragraph_text: When the boys heard of their father’s death they were struck with grief. They ran crying to where he lay and fell to the ground, like powerful lions rolling on the earth. Mādrī blessed them tearfully and told them that she would be ascending the pyre with her husband. She asked her two sons to remain with Kuntī and to serve her steadfastly. The boys were too shocked to reply. They watched as the ṛṣis built a pyre next to the king’s body, then, while reciting mantras, placed his body upon the pyre. They asked Yudhiṣṭhira to step forward. The prince, blinded by tears, set fire to the pyre and stood back. As the flames rose, Mādrī folded her palms and fell upon her lord’s body, holding tight as the fire consumed them both. Within minutes both she and Pāṇḍu were gone.

questions.question: Which son of Pāṇḍu was specifically asked by the ṛṣis to step forward and ignite the funeral pyre?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the ṛṣis asked Yudhiṣṭhira to step forward to set fire to the pyre.

questions.question: Whom did Mādrī instruct her two sons to remain with and serve steadfastly after her passing?
questions.explanation: Mādrī told her two sons to remain with Kuntī and to serve her after she ascended the pyre.

questions.question: What ritual action did the ṛṣis perform while placing Pāṇḍu's body upon the funeral pyre?
questions.explanation: The text states that the ṛṣis were reciting mantras while they placed Pāṇḍu's body upon the pyre.

1.3.28.paragraph_text: The griefstricken Kuntī then asked the ṛṣis what she could do. The ṛṣis advised Kuntī to return to Hastināpura with her boys as soon as possible. They also told her they would accompany her, carrying the remains of Pāṇḍu and Mādrī with them

questions.question: After the death of Pāṇḍu and Mādrī, which destination were the ṛṣis instructed to accompany Kuntī and the boys to?
questions.explanation: The ṛṣis advised Kuntī to return to Hastināpura with her boys.

questions.question: What items were the ṛṣis planning to carry with them during the journey to Hastināpura?
options: The remains of Pāṇḍu and Mādrī
The ritual implements of the sacrifice
questions.correct_answer: The remains of Pāṇḍu and Mādrī
questions.explanation: The ṛṣis told Kuntī they would accompany her, carrying the remains of Pāṇḍu and Mādrī with them.

1.3.29.paragraph_text: The large number of ṛṣis, Siddhas and Cāraṇas formed a procession, walking ahead of Kuntī and her sons. By their mystical powers, they all arrived at Hastināpura within a short time. Kuntī then presented herself at the northern gate and messengers ran swiftly to inform the king.

questions.question: Which specific groups of celestial or enlightened beings accompanied Kuntī and her sons on their journey to Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that a large number of ṛṣis, Siddhas, and Cāraṇas formed a procession to accompany Kuntī and her sons.

questions.question: How did the ṛṣis and other beings ensure they reached Hastināpura within a short amount of time?
options: By invoking the speed of Vāyu
By walking through a divine portal
questions.explanation: The text mentions that they arrived at Hastināpura within a short time by using their mystical powers.

questions.question: At which specific location in Hastināpura did Kuntī present herself upon arrival?
questions.explanation: The text specifies that Kuntī presented herself at the northern gate upon her arrival at Hastināpura.

1.3.30.paragraph_text: Upon hearing of her arrival, Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Satyavatī and all the other Kuru elders hurried to the gate, followed by a large number of citizens, who had heard that an assembly of celestials had arrived. Everyone was struck with wonder to see the brilliant ṛṣis surrounded by Siddhas and Cāraṇas. People came out in the thousands to see those divine beings.

questions.question: Which specific Kuru elders gathered at the gate to meet the approaching party?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly names Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, and Satyavatī as the Kuru elders who hurried to the gate upon hearing of the arrival.

questions.question: What groups accompanied the ṛṣis that caused the citizens of Hastināpura to gather in thousands?
questions.explanation: The text states that the brilliant ṛṣis were surrounded by Siddhas and Cāraṇas, which prompted the citizens to come out to see the divine beings.

1.3.31.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra, along with his one hundred sons, bowed before the ṛṣis. Bhīṣma and the others also offered their obeisances to the sages and, after offering them seats, they sat before them on silk mats spread out on the ground. When the crowd was finally settled, Bhīṣma went forward and worshipped the ṛṣis by bathing their feet and offering them arghya. He answered their enquiries about the kingdom’s welfare, saying that everything was well. Then one of the leading ṛṣis stood up and addressed the Kuru elders. “The virtuous monarch went to the Saptasrota mountain to observe the vow of celibacy. Somehow by the Lord’s inscrutable plan and the arrangement of the gods, his five sons were born.”

questions.question: Which specific location did the virtuous monarch visit to perform his vow of celibacy?
questions.explanation: The leading ṛṣi addresses the elders by stating that the virtuous monarch went to the Saptasrota mountain to observe the vow of celibacy.

questions.question: How many sons were said to be born to the virtuous monarch through the Lord's inscrutable plan and divine arrangement?
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi confirms that due to the Lord's inscrutable plan and the arrangement of the gods, the virtuous monarch had five sons born to him.

questions.question: How did Bhīṣma perform worship for the visiting ṛṣis?
options: By chanting Vedic hymns and offering garlands
By bathing their feet and offering arghya
By prostrating himself and offering gold
By presenting them with cattle and silk mats
questions.correct_answer: By bathing their feet and offering arghya
questions.explanation: The text states that Bhīṣma went forward and worshipped the ṛṣis specifically by bathing their feet and offering them arghya.

1.3.32.paragraph_text: The ṛṣi then introduced each of Pāṇḍu’s sons and revealed the identities of their divine fathers. He told them how the boys had been trained in Vedic knowledge and had grown to youthhood in the company of ṛṣis and Siddhas. Glancing around the large concourse of men who had assembled outside the city, the ṛṣi said, “Steadily adhering to virtue, and leaving behind him these children, Pāṇḍu has ascended to the higher worlds. The chaste Mādrī has gone with him. Now his sons should be accepted as the kingdom’s rightful heirs.”

questions.question: Which specific groups of celestial or holy beings were involved in the upbringing of Pāṇḍu's sons during their youth?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu's sons were trained in Vedic knowledge and grew to youthhood in the company of ṛṣis and Siddhas.

questions.question: What is the final status of Pāṇḍu and Mādrī according to the ṛṣi addressing the assembly?
options: They have entered a long period of penance in the Saptasrota mountains
They have both ascended to the higher worlds
They have returned to the city to reclaim their kingdom
They have been granted immortality by the gods
questions.correct_answer: They have both ascended to the higher worlds
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi states that Pāṇḍu has ascended to the higher worlds and that the chaste Mādrī has gone with him.

questions.question: What did the ṛṣi reveal to the assembled men regarding the origin of Pāṇḍu’s sons?
options: Their identities as incarnations of the Kuru ancestors
The identities of their divine fathers
That they were born from the blessings of the Saptasrota sages
Their status as sons of the gods and demons combined
questions.correct_answer: The identities of their divine fathers
questions.explanation: The text mentions that the ṛṣi introduced each of Pāṇḍu's sons and revealed the identities of their divine fathers.

questions.question: What final recommendation did the ṛṣi make to the Kuru assembly regarding Pāṇḍu's sons?
options: They should be trained by the court elders in warfare
They should be accepted as the kingdom’s rightful heirs
They should be sent back to the mountain forests
They should undergo a period of purification before taking power
questions.correct_answer: They should be accepted as the kingdom’s rightful heirs
questions.explanation: The ṛṣi instructed the gathered men that Pāṇḍu's sons should be accepted as the kingdom's rightful heirs.

1.3.33.paragraph_text: The ṛṣi pointed to a bier lying nearby, covered with a white cloth. “Here are the remains of Pāṇḍu and his wife. Perform the funeral rites and accept his sons as if they were your own. We shall now depart.”

questions.question: What items did the ṛṣi indicate contained the remains of Pāṇḍu and his wife?
options: A bundle of sacred kusha grass
questions.explanation: The text states the ṛṣi pointed to a bier lying nearby which held the remains of Pāṇḍu and his wife.

1.3.34.paragraph_text: When the ṛṣi stopped speaking he, along with all the other ṛṣis and celestial beings, vanished. In astonishment the citizens returned to their homes. Bhīṣma then led Kuntī and the boys into the city and arranged for their accommodation in a royal palace. Dhṛtarāṣṭra ordered the funeral rites to be performed and declared a state of mourning in the city for twelve full days.

questions.question: For how many days did Dhṛtarāṣṭra declare a state of mourning in the city following the arrival of the funeral bier?
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra ordered the funeral rites to be performed and declared a state of mourning in the city for twelve full days.

questions.question: Who was responsible for leading Kuntī and her sons into the city after the ṛṣis vanished?
questions.explanation: Bhīṣma led Kuntī and the boys into the city and arranged for their accommodation in a royal palace.

questions.question: Where did Bhīṣma arrange for Kuntī and the boys to stay upon their arrival in the city?
options: The house of the Kuru elders
questions.explanation: Bhīṣma arranged for the accommodation of Kuntī and the boys in a royal palace.

1.3.35.paragraph_text: The Kurus were distracted with grief over Pāṇḍu’s loss. Pāṇḍu had been dearly loved by all the citizens and they loudly lamented. Pāṇḍu’s five sons, known as the Pāṇḍavas, lay on the bare ground for twelve days, giving full vent to their grief. They did not accept royal comforts or beds, and they wept along with the Kuru elders.

questions.question: For how many days did the Pāṇḍavas lie on the bare ground mourning the death of Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu’s five sons lay on the bare ground for twelve days, giving full vent to their grief.

questions.question: What physical actions did the Pāṇḍavas take to express their grief following the death of their father?
options: They wandered through the forest in seclusion
They lay on the bare ground and rejected royal comforts
They held a feast for the city citizens
questions.correct_answer: They lay on the bare ground and rejected royal comforts
questions.explanation: The text notes that the five sons lay on the bare ground and did not accept royal comforts or beds during their period of mourning.

1.3.36.paragraph_text: At the end of the mourning period, the Kurus performed Pāṇḍu’s śraddhā. They distributed vast amounts of food and wealth to the Brahmins on behalf of the departed souls. Then life returned to normal in Hastināpura and Pāṇḍu’s sons took their place in the royal family.

questions.question: What religious ceremony did the Kurus perform for Pāṇḍu following the period of mourning?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that at the end of the mourning period, the Kurus performed Pāṇḍu’s śraddhā.

questions.question: To which social group was wealth and food distributed during the rites for Pāṇḍu?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that food and wealth were distributed to the Brahmins on behalf of the departed souls.

questions.question: In which city did the events following Pāṇḍu's mourning period take place?
questions.explanation: The passage states that life returned to normal in Hastināpura after the mourning rites.

1.3.37.paragraph_text: It was at that time that Vyasa chose to again appear in the city. He spoke privately to the bereaved and sorrowful Satyavatī. “O Mother, fearful and terrible times lie ahead. The dark age of Kali now approaches. Sin increases day by day. Soon, by the evil acts of the Kurus, your race will be destroyed and a great havoc will be wrought in the world.”

questions.question: Which figure visited the city of Hastināpura to speak with Satyavatī regarding the future of her race?
questions.explanation: Vyasa is the person who appeared in the city to speak privately with Satyavatī.

questions.question: What specific era did Vyasa warn was approaching in his conversation with Satyavatī?
questions.explanation: Vyasa explicitly identifies the approaching time as the dark age of Kali.

questions.question: According to Vyasa's warning, what will be the primary cause for the destruction of the Kuru race?
options: The evil acts of the Kurus themselves
questions.correct_answer: The evil acts of the Kurus themselves
questions.explanation: Vyasa states that the destruction of the race and the coming havoc will be wrought by the evil acts of the Kurus.

1.3.38.paragraph_text: Vyasa advised his mother to immediately retire to the forest rather than staying to witness the pain and horror that would soon follow. She should devote herself to yoga practice and meditation.

questions.question: What course of action did Vyasa strongly suggest to his mother, Satyavatī, in light of the approaching future?
options: To travel to the Himalayas and perform penance
To immediately retire to the forest
To assume the throne of Hastināpura
To seek counsel from the sages of the forest
questions.correct_answer: To immediately retire to the forest
questions.explanation: Vyasa advised his mother to immediately retire to the forest to avoid witnessing the future pain and horror.

questions.question: To what practices did Vyasa instruct his mother to dedicate her time while in the forest?
options: Service to the poor and pilgrimage
questions.explanation: Vyasa advised his mother that she should devote herself specifically to yoga practice and meditation.

questions.question: Why did Vyasa recommend that Satyavatī depart from her current surroundings?
options: To escape the influence of the Kurus
To avoid witnessing the impending pain and horror
To prepare for the arrival of the Kali Yuga
To prevent the destruction of her family line
questions.correct_answer: To avoid witnessing the impending pain and horror
questions.explanation: Vyasa told his mother to retire to the forest rather than staying to witness the pain and horror that would soon follow.

1.3.39.paragraph_text: After Vyasa left, Satyavatī reflected on his words. She decided to follow his advice. She then told her two daughters-in-law of her intentions, and asked Pāṇḍu’s distraught mother, Ambālikā, to accompany her. The two royal ladies soon left for the forest to dedicate themselves to asceticism. In time they gave up their bodies and went joyfully to the higher regions. Ambikā remained at Hastināpura with her son, Dhṛtarāṣṭra.

questions.question: Which family member did Satyavatī invite to accompany her to the forest to practice asceticism?
questions.explanation: Satyavatī requested that Ambālikā, the mother of Pāṇḍu, accompany her to the forest.

questions.question: Which royal figure chose to remain in Hastināpura rather than retiring to the forest with Satyavatī?
questions.explanation: Ambikā stayed at Hastināpura with her son, Dhṛtarāṣṭra, while the others left for the forest.

questions.question: Who is identified as the mother of Dhṛtarāṣṭra in the context of those remaining in Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The text states that Ambikā remained at Hastināpura with her son, Dhṛtarāṣṭra.

1.4.1.paragraph_text: The Pāṇḍavas began to enjoy life in Hastināpura. They sported with the hundred sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, who became known as the Kauravas. Pandu’s sons excelled the Kauravas in all areas: in strength, knowledge and prowess with weaponry. Bhīma was especially powerful and he took delight in defeating the Kauravas in sport. They could not equal him in anything. The exuberant Bhīma possessed the indefatigable power of his divine father. At wrestling and fighting he was unapproachable and could easily hold off the attacks of any number of Kauravas. Out of a boyish sense of fun he would often play practical jokes on them, laughing when they became angered and tried futilely to get back at him.

questions.question: Which specific traits allowed the Pāṇḍavas to excel over the Kauravas during their youth in Hastināpura?
options: Strength, knowledge, and prowess with weaponry
questions.correct_answer: Strength, knowledge, and prowess with weaponry
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the Pāṇḍavas excelled the Kauravas in these three specific areas: strength, knowledge, and weaponry.

questions.question: Which Pāṇḍava was noted for possessing the indefatigable power of his divine father?
questions.explanation: The paragraph highlights that the exuberant Bhīma possessed the indefatigable power inherited from his divine father.

questions.question: How did Bhīma typically interact with the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra during their competitive sports?
options: He remained reserved and avoided all physical contact
He constantly sought the guidance of the elder Kauravas
He played practical jokes on them and delighted in defeating them
He acted as their teacher in wrestling and weaponry
questions.correct_answer: He played practical jokes on them and delighted in defeating them
questions.explanation: The text describes Bhīma taking delight in defeating the Kauravas and playing practical jokes on them out of a boyish sense of fun.

questions.question: What reaction did the Kauravas exhibit when Bhīma played his practical jokes on them?
options: They admired his strength and requested his training
They would become angered and attempt to retaliate
They reported his actions to the elders of the Kuru house
questions.correct_answer: They would become angered and attempt to retaliate
questions.explanation: The paragraph notes that the Kauravas would become angered and try futilely to get back at Bhīma after his practical jokes.

1.4.2.paragraph_text: Duryodhana in particular found Bhīma’s antics and power intolerable. As the eldest son of the blind king, Duryodhana had enjoyed the most prestige in the Kuru house. The Kuru elders had carefully raised him and trained him in the kingly arts, thinking of him as the potential future world emperor. Mindful of the omens seen at his birth, the elders were especially careful to teach him moral codes. The prince was both powerful and capable in all areas of weaponry and politics, and was accustomed to being the center of attention in the royal palace since his birth. When the Pāṇḍavas arrived, however, all that changed. Pandu’s sons were gentle, modest and devoted to their elders. They soon became dear to Bhīṣma, Vidura and the other senior Kurus. Their behavior was a welcome change from that of Duryodhana and his brothers, who tended to be self-centered and proud, and often quite arrogant. Duryodhana quickly became envious of his five cousins. His envy grew up like an oil-fed fire when he saw Bhīma’s pranks.

questions.question: Which specific Kuru elders were tasked with the responsibility of training Duryodhana in moral codes and kingly arts?
options: Bhīṣma, Vidura, and other senior Kurus
questions.correct_answer: Bhīṣma, Vidura, and other senior Kurus
questions.explanation: The text notes that Bhīṣma, Vidura, and other senior Kurus raised and trained Duryodhana, and were responsible for teaching him moral codes.

questions.question: What primary factor influenced the Kuru elders to be particularly diligent in teaching Duryodhana moral codes from his birth?
options: The omens observed at his birth
His status as the only son of the king
A prophecy about the end of the Kuru dynasty
The request of his mother, Gandhari
questions.correct_answer: The omens observed at his birth
questions.explanation: The text states that the elders were mindful of the omens seen at Duryodhana's birth and were therefore especially careful to teach him moral codes.

questions.question: Which specific personality traits of the Pāṇḍavas made them endearing to the senior Kurus, such as Bhīṣma and Vidura?
options: Their martial prowess and strength in battle
Their tendency to be self-centered and proud
Their gentle nature, modesty, and devotion to their elders
Their ability to easily defeat the Kauravas in sports
questions.correct_answer: Their gentle nature, modesty, and devotion to their elders
questions.explanation: The text describes the Pāṇḍavas as gentle, modest, and devoted to their elders, which endeared them to Bhīṣma, Vidura, and other senior Kurus.

questions.question: How is the growth of Duryodhana’s envy toward the Pāṇḍavas metaphorically described in the text?
options: Like a seed planted in dry earth
Like a river overflowing its banks
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Duryodhana's envy of his five cousins grew up like an oil-fed fire.

questions.question: Who was regarded by the Kuru elders as a potential future world emperor?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that the Kuru elders had raised and trained Duryodhana thinking of him as the potential future world emperor.

1.4.3.paragraph_text: After another day of humiliation at Bhīma’s hands, Duryodhana felt he could take no more. He spoke with Dushashana, the next eldest of the hundred Kauravas. “Dear brother, this Bhīma is a constant thorn in our sides. He challenges all hundred of us at once and throws us about like pieces of straw. We cannot better him at anything. Why, even at eating he humbles us by consuming as much as twenty of us put together. Something has to be done to check his pride.”

questions.question: Which brother did Duryodhana consult after feeling humiliated by Bhīma?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana spoke with Dushashana, identified as the next eldest of the hundred Kauravas, regarding his frustration with Bhīma.

questions.question: How many Kauravas did Bhīma challenge simultaneously, according to Duryodhana’s complaint?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana explicitly states that Bhīma challenges all hundred of them at once.

questions.question: According to Duryodhana, how much food was Bhīma capable of consuming relative to the other princes?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana complains that Bhīma humbles the other princes by consuming as much food as twenty of them put together.

1.4.4.paragraph_text: Duryodhana’s eyes narrowed as he spoke. His intentions were vicious. The prince was inclined to wicked acts, and he had been spurred on by his uncle Shakuni, who had taken up residence in Hastināpura. The Gandhara prince had been offended by Bhīṣma’s decision to give his sister to the blind Dhṛtarāṣṭra rather than to Pandu. Now he wanted revenge. He involved himself in court intrigues in order to find a way to avenge himself against Bhīṣma. Hurting the Pāṇḍavas, who were obviously dear to Bhīṣma, was one good way. And, of course, he would simultaneously be advancing the cause of his sister’s sons. Duryodhana was a willing accomplice. The boy saw his scheming uncle as a mentor. When Duryodhana had come to him complaining about Bhīma it did not take much to convince him to do something terrible to the Pandava boy.

questions.question: Which relative of Duryodhana resided in Hastināpura and encouraged his wicked intentions?
questions.explanation: The text states that Duryodhana had been spurred on by his uncle Shakuni, who had taken up residence in Hastināpura.

questions.question: What primary motivation drove Shakuni to seek revenge against Bhīṣma?
options: Bhīṣma refused to grant him land in Gandhara
Bhīṣma insulted the Gandhara king in open court
Bhīṣma chose to give his sister to the blind Dhṛtarāṣṭra instead of to Pandu
Bhīṣma humiliated him during a diplomatic mission
questions.correct_answer: Bhīṣma chose to give his sister to the blind Dhṛtarāṣṭra instead of to Pandu
questions.explanation: The text explains that the Gandhara prince (Shakuni) was offended by Bhīṣma’s decision to give his sister to the blind Dhṛtarāṣṭra rather than to Pandu.

questions.question: Why did Shakuni determine that harming the Pāṇḍavas would be an effective strategy for his goals?
options: The Pāṇḍavas were the rightful heirs to the throne of Gandhara
Bhīṣma held the Pāṇḍavas very dear
The Pāṇḍavas had previously defeated his forces in battle
Bhīṣma had entrusted the Pāṇḍavas with his life
questions.correct_answer: Bhīṣma held the Pāṇḍavas very dear
questions.explanation: The text mentions that hurting the Pāṇḍavas was a good way to avenge himself against Bhīṣma, as they were obviously dear to him.

questions.question: In what capacity did Duryodhana view his uncle Shakuni?
options: As a rival for the throne
As a burden to the kingdom
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the boy (Duryodhana) saw his scheming uncle (Shakuni) as a mentor.

1.4.5.paragraph_text: Duryodhana revealed his heinous plan to Dushashana. “Tomorrow I shall feed Bhīma an enormous poisoned feast. When he falls unconscious after eating I shall bind his limbs and toss him into the Ganges. With Bhīma gone the other brothers are helpless. We can easily deal with them. Thus my claim to the throne will be unchallenged.”

questions.question: Whom did Duryodhana inform about his scheme to eliminate Bhīma through a poisoned feast?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana revealed his heinous plan to Dushashana.

questions.question: What method did Duryodhana propose to dispose of Bhīma after poisoning him?
options: Burning him in a wax palace
Binding his limbs and tossing him into the Ganges
Ordering his guards to execute him
questions.correct_answer: Binding his limbs and tossing him into the Ganges
questions.explanation: Duryodhana stated he would bind Bhīma's limbs and toss him into the Ganges after he fell unconscious from the poisoned food.

questions.question: Why did Duryodhana believe that removing Bhīma would ensure his claim to the throne?
options: Because Bhīma was the eldest brother
Because Bhīma was the only one who knew his secrets
Because the other brothers would be left helpless
Because Bhīma was the only one protected by Bhishma
questions.correct_answer: Because the other brothers would be left helpless
questions.explanation: Duryodhana asserted that with Bhīma gone, the other brothers would be helpless, making his claim to the throne unchallenged.

1.4.6.paragraph_text: Dushashana smiled in agreement. He too found Bhīma’s behavior intolerable and, like Duryodhana, had also found his own status in the Kuru house diminish since the Pāṇḍavas’ arrival. He put his arm around his brother and the two of them made their way back to their palace, laughing together as they walked the forest path.

questions.question: Why did Dushashana feel his status in the Kuru house had diminished?
options: Due to the arrival of the Pāṇḍavas
Following his father's loss of the throne
Because of the destruction of their palace
As a result of Bhīṣma's refusal to lead
questions.correct_answer: Due to the arrival of the Pāṇḍavas
questions.explanation: Dushashana felt his status had diminished as a direct result of the Pāṇḍavas' arrival in the Kuru house.

questions.question: Which member of the Pāṇḍavas did Dushashana find his behavior intolerable?
questions.explanation: Dushashana found the behavior of Bhīma to be intolerable.

1.4.7.paragraph_text: The next morning Duryodhana suggested that all the princes go to the river for some water sports. Soon they mounted their shining chariots, which resembled cities and had great wheels which rumbled like thunderclouds as they headed out, sending up clouds of dust. Upon arriving on the river bank, the mighty youths dismounted from their cars, laughing and joking, and entered the large pleasure house Dhṛtarāṣṭra had built for them.

questions.question: What activity did Duryodhana suggest to the princes on the morning they departed for the river?
options: A formal gathering to discuss state affairs
questions.explanation: Duryodhana suggested that all the princes go to the river for some water sports.

questions.question: Which Kuru king was responsible for building the pleasure house where the princes gathered by the river?
questions.explanation: The text identifies that the pleasure house the youths entered by the river bank was built by Dhṛtarāṣṭra.

1.4.8.paragraph_text: The elegant palace was built of white marble and it stood seven stories high. Many-colored pennants flew from tall golden flagstaffs on its roof. There were dozens of rooms offering every kind of luxury. Each room was tastefully decorated with tapestries, fine paintings, and ivory and coral furnishings, studded with gems and covered with golden cushions. Royal musicians and dancers stood by, ready to entertain the princes, and a hundred of the king’s select force of bodyguards stood ready to protect them.

questions.question: What primary building material was used to construct the palace visited by the princes?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states the palace was built of white marble.

questions.question: How many stories high was the palace built for the princes?
questions.explanation: The text describes the palace as standing seven stories high.

questions.question: What specific decorative materials were used for the furnishings within the palace rooms?
questions.explanation: The palace rooms are described as containing ivory and coral furnishings.

questions.question: How many of the king's bodyguards were assigned to protect the princes in the palace?
questions.explanation: The text specifies that a hundred of the king's select force of bodyguards were standing ready to protect the princes.

1.4.9.paragraph_text: Once inside Duryhodhana invited everyone to enjoy the great feast he had arranged for them. He led them through the mansion and out into the central gardens. The boys looked with pleasure upon the large ponds filled with red and blue lotuses and surrounded by soft, grassy banks. Crystal waterfalls made tinkling sounds that blended with the singing of brightly colored exotic birds. The heady scent of numerous blossoms filled the air. Fine cushions had been arranged in lines on the grass and many servants stood by, waiting to serve the feast.

1.4.10.paragraph_text: Duryodhana chose the seat next to Bhīma. Then he ordered the servants to bring the food. The dishes were exquisite. Duryodhana had personally mixed the poison with the food he had brought to him. He then offered the plate to Bhīma, feigning love and feeding him with his own hand. The guileless Bhīma suspected nothing and he cheerfully consumed his normal amount. Duryodhana rejoiced within as Bhīma hungrily swallowed the poisoned cakes, pies, creams, drinks and other preparations.

questions.question: Where did Duryodhana choose to sit during the feast to ensure he could interact with Bhīma?
options: At the head of the table
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Duryodhana chose the seat next to Bhīma.

questions.question: How did Duryodhana ensure that the food served to Bhīma was lethal?
options: He ordered the palace cooks to use a secret recipe
He had the food tainted by a master assassin
He personally mixed poison into the food
He requested that the food be imported from a distant land
questions.correct_answer: He personally mixed poison into the food
questions.explanation: Duryodhana had personally mixed the poison with the food that he had brought to Bhīma.

questions.question: What facade did Duryodhana maintain while serving the poisoned food to Bhīma?
options: He acted as a humble servant
He feigned love and fed Bhīma with his own hand
He pretended to be celebrating a great victory
He claimed to be following the orders of Dhṛtarāṣṭra
questions.correct_answer: He feigned love and fed Bhīma with his own hand
questions.explanation: The text notes that Duryodhana offered the plate to Bhīma while feigning love and feeding him with his own hand.

questions.question: How did Bhīma react to the poisoned meal provided by Duryodhana?
options: He suspected foul play and refused to eat
He noticed the strange taste but ate it anyway out of courtesy
He suspected nothing and cheerfully consumed his normal amount
He shared the food with others to test its safety
questions.correct_answer: He suspected nothing and cheerfully consumed his normal amount
questions.explanation: Bhīma is described as guileless, suspecting nothing and cheerfully consuming his normal amount of the poisoned food.

1.4.11.paragraph_text: When the feast was over, Duryodhana suggested they all go down to the river for sport. The boys raced to the river in great joy. They wrestled and rolled about on the ground, tossing each other into the clear blue water of the river. As usual, Bhīma was the most energetic. The poison did not appear to have affected him. The prince, who stood head and shoulders above his peers, was a peerless wrestler. Anyone who approached him quickly found themselves sailing through the air and landing in the water. Bhīma would then dive in and create huge waves by thrashing his arms. The other princes were then dunked under the waters by the playful Bhīma.
