options: The all-devouring fire at the end of an aeon
questions.correct_answer: The all-devouring fire at the end of an aeon
questions.explanation: The text explicitly compares Arjuna's intensity in battle to the all-devouring fire that appears at the end of an aeon.

questions.question: Which brothers were positioned to protect Arjuna while he engaged the Pañchāla army?
options: His two brothers who are not specified by name
questions.correct_answer: His two brothers who are not specified by name
questions.explanation: The text states that Arjuna was protected on either side by his two brothers, but it does not specify which two they were in this specific passage.

1.6.11.paragraph_text: Drupada raced to the head of his troops and they rallied with lion-like roars. Led by their king, they mounted a powerful counterattack against the Pāṇḍavas. Arrows, darts, spears and clubs rained down on the four brothers. Arjuna repelled all their missiles with his arrows. The Pañchālas enraged him with their furious attack and fought with redoubled energy. His foes could not mark any interval between his pulling an arrow from his quiver and bending his bow to fire it. All they saw was a constant stream of shafts speeding toward them. The mighty Pañchāla warriors shouted praises at Arjuna for his prowess.

questions.question: What reaction did the Pañchāla troops exhibit when Drupada rushed to the head of his forces?
options: They began singing hymns of peace
questions.explanation: Drupada raced to the head of his troops and they rallied with lion-like roars.

questions.question: Which weapons were rained down on the four brothers by the Pañchāla forces?
questions.explanation: Arrows, darts, spears and clubs rained down on the four brothers.

questions.question: What unique skill did the Pañchāla warriors observe regarding Arjuna's use of the bow?
options: He could fire arrows with both hands simultaneously
He never missed a target even in total darkness
There was no visible interval between pulling an arrow and firing it
He could shatter any shield with a single shot
questions.correct_answer: There was no visible interval between pulling an arrow and firing it
questions.explanation: His foes could not mark any interval between his pulling an arrow from his quiver and bending his bow to fire it.

questions.question: What was the final reaction of the Pañchāla warriors toward Arjuna's display of prowess?
options: They criticized his lack of honor
questions.explanation: The mighty Pañchāla warriors shouted praises at Arjuna for his prowess.

1.6.12.paragraph_text: Along with his commander-in-chief Satyajit, Drupada personally rushed toward Arjuna. Like the king, Satyajit was a warrior capable of contending with thousands of other warriors at once. He struck Arjuna with a hundred fierce arrows and sent up a great roar. Not tolerating the attack, Arjuna pierced Satyajit with ten arrows and simultaneously cut his bow to pieces with three more shafts. Seeing this wonderful feat, the other warriors cheered. Satyajit grasped another bow and immediately pierced Arjuna’s steeds as well as his charioteer. Arjuna again split Satyajit’s bow, then killed his horses and smashed his chariot to pieces.

questions.question: Who served as the commander-in-chief alongside King Drupada during the battle against Arjuna?
questions.explanation: The text identifies Satyajit as the commander-in-chief who accompanied Drupada in the attack against Arjuna.

questions.question: With how many arrows did Arjuna initially pierce the commander-in-chief Satyajit during their combat?
questions.explanation: Arjuna pierced Satyajit with ten arrows after Satyajit attacked him with a hundred fierce arrows.

questions.question: How did Arjuna neutralize Satyajit's capability to fight after their initial exchange of arrows?
options: By killing his charioteer and horses
By wounding him in the chest
By cutting his bow to pieces
By surrounding him with a cloud of arrows
questions.correct_answer: By cutting his bow to pieces
questions.explanation: The text states that Arjuna cut Satyajit's bow to pieces with three shafts, and then later split it again after Satyajit had retrieved a new one.

questions.question: Which specific components of Satyajit's chariot did he manage to strike before Arjuna destroyed his vehicle?
questions.explanation: After grasping a second bow, Satyajit successfully pierced Arjuna's steeds and his charioteer.

1.6.13.paragraph_text: Drupada came quickly to his commander’s assistance. A powerful exchange of arrows and other missiles followed between the king and Arjuna. Gradually Arjuna overpowered Drupada. He shattered the king’s bow, tore off his armor, felled his flagstaff and killed his horses. Seeing Drupada confounded, Arjuna threw down his own bow and took up a huge scimitar. He leapt down from his chariot and jumped onto Drupada’s, seizing him and holding the sword to his throat.

questions.question: After overpowering King Drupada in battle, what specific weapon did Arjuna choose to finish the engagement?
questions.explanation: After shattering Drupada's bow, Arjuna threw down his own bow and took up a huge scimitar to capture the king.

questions.question: What actions did Arjuna perform to completely dismantle Drupada's fighting capability before boarding his chariot?
options: He shattered the bow, tore off the armor, felled the flagstaff, and killed the horses.
He burned the chariot, broke the sword, and scattered the infantry.
He decapitated the charioteer and destroyed the royal banner.
He blinded the horses and disarmed the king of his shield and spear.
questions.correct_answer: He shattered the bow, tore off the armor, felled the flagstaff, and killed the horses.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly details these four specific actions—shattering the bow, tearing off armor, felling the flagstaff, and killing the horses—that Arjuna performed against Drupada.

questions.question: How did Arjuna physically restrain Drupada after leaping onto his chariot?
options: He bound him with rope and cast him onto the ground.
He seized him and held his sword to his throat.
He struck him unconscious with the hilt of his scimitar.
He grabbed him by the hair and threw him toward his own chariot.
questions.correct_answer: He seized him and held his sword to his throat.
questions.explanation: Upon jumping onto Drupada's chariot, Arjuna seized the king and held his sword to his throat.

1.6.14.paragraph_text: Bhīma had meanwhile been wreaking havoc among Drupada’s troops. Arjuna shouted to him to withdraw. They had achieved their aim and captured Drupada. The troops saw their king’s plight and fled in fear. Arjuna then dragged Drupada onto his own chariot and rode back toward Droṇa.

questions.question: Which warrior was engaged in wreaking havoc among King Drupada's troops while Arjuna was battling the king?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Bhīma had been wreaking havoc among Drupada's troops while Arjuna was elsewhere.

questions.question: After capturing Drupada, to whom did Arjuna take the captive king?
questions.explanation: The text notes that after dragging Drupada onto his chariot, Arjuna rode back toward Droṇa.

questions.question: What reaction did Drupada's troops have upon witnessing the king's capture?
questions.explanation: The passage states that the troops saw their king's plight and fled in fear.

1.6.15.paragraph_text: When Droṇa saw the captive king, he smiled. “So, O mighty king, do you now desire to revive our old friendship? It seems that your kingdom and wealth have become mine.”

questions.question: What reaction did Droṇa exhibit upon seeing the captive King Drupada?
questions.explanation: Droṇa's reaction to seeing the captive king was to smile.

questions.question: How did Droṇa characterize the ownership of Drupada’s kingdom and wealth after Drupada was captured?
options: He claimed they were transferred to his son
He stated they had become his own
He insisted they be returned to the citizens
He demanded they be split among the Kauravas
questions.correct_answer: He stated they had become his own
questions.explanation: Droṇa remarked to Drupada that his kingdom and wealth had become his.

questions.question: What question did Droṇa pose to Drupada regarding their past relationship after capturing him?
options: Whether he still regretted their shared childhood
Whether he now desired to revive their old friendship
Whether he acknowledged Droṇa as his master
Whether he wished to renounce his throne
questions.correct_answer: Whether he now desired to revive their old friendship
questions.explanation: Droṇa specifically asked Drupada if he now desired to revive their old friendship.

1.6.16.paragraph_text: Drupada squirmed and blushed. He looked down as Droṇa continued, “You need not fear for your life for I am a Brahmin and it is my duty to be ever forgiving. Indeed, I have always cherished an affection for you since we were children.”

questions.question: What primary duty does Droṇa cite as his motivation for being forgiving toward Drupada?
options: The duty of a kṣatriya to show mercy
The duty of a brāhmaṇa to be ever forgiving
The duty of a teacher to mentor former students
The duty of a king to maintain political stability
questions.correct_answer: The duty of a brāhmaṇa to be ever forgiving
questions.explanation: Droṇa explicitly states that because he is a brāhmaṇa, it is his duty to be ever forgiving.

questions.question: What does Droṇa claim to have held for Drupada since they were children?
questions.explanation: Droṇa mentions that he has always cherished an affection for Drupada dating back to their childhood.

1.6.17.paragraph_text: Droṇa then ordered Arjuna to release Drupada. The king listened in silence as Droṇa continued. “I still desire your friendship, Drupada, but how can one who is not a king be a king’s friend? Therefore I have decided to allow you to keep half your kingdom. I shall take the other half.”

questions.question: Whom did Droṇa instruct to release King Drupada?
questions.explanation: Droṇa ordered Arjuna to release Drupada.

questions.question: What portion of the kingdom did Droṇa decide to claim from Drupada?
questions.explanation: Droṇa stated he would take half of Drupada's kingdom.

questions.question: What reasoning did Droṇa provide for his decision to take half of Drupada's kingdom?
options: He wanted to punish Drupada for his pride
He believed a non-king cannot be a king's friend
He needed the land to support his students
He was fulfilling a vow made to his teacher
questions.correct_answer: He believed a non-king cannot be a king's friend
questions.explanation: Droṇa explained he desired friendship but questioned how one who is not a king can be a king's friend, thus leading to his decision to take half the kingdom.

1.6.18.paragraph_text: Drupada was in no position to argue. He knew that Droṇa’s martial power far exceeded his own--especially as he now had the mighty Kurus as disciples. There would be no question of defeating him in battle. Drupada nodded in assent. “You are a truly noble soul to act in this way, Droṇa,” he replied, summoning all his patience. “Great personalities like yourself are always magnanimous. I, too, desire your friendship. Let us live peacefully, each ruling his own half of the Pañchāla kingdom.”

questions.question: Why did Drupada accept Droṇa’s terms regarding the division of the Pañchāla kingdom?
options: He feared the divine wrath of the gods
He recognized that Droṇa’s martial power exceeded his own
He was promised a higher status in the Kuru assembly
He wished to retire to the forest to perform penance
questions.correct_answer: He recognized that Droṇa’s martial power exceeded his own
questions.explanation: Drupada acknowledged that Droṇa's martial power far exceeded his own and that he had the mighty Kurus as disciples, making defeat in battle inevitable.

questions.question: Which kingdom was partitioned between Droṇa and Drupada?
questions.explanation: Drupada explicitly mentions in his reply that they should live peacefully while each rules his own half of the Pañchāla kingdom.

questions.question: What factor contributed significantly to Droṇa's superior martial advantage according to Drupada's assessment?
options: Droṇa possessed celestial weapons granted by Indra
Droṇa commanded the entire Kuru army
Droṇa had the mighty Kurus as disciples
Droṇa was supported by the combined forces of the Pāṇḍavas
questions.correct_answer: Droṇa had the mighty Kurus as disciples
questions.explanation: Drupada's internal assessment of Droṇa's power was influenced heavily by the fact that Droṇa had the mighty Kurus as his disciples.

1.6.19.paragraph_text: Drupada had Brahmins perform appropriate rituals and bestowed the northern half of his kingdom upon Droṇa, who then left with the Pāṇḍavas for Hastināpura. Drupada burned with humiliation. Somehow he had to avenge his honor. Absorbed in thought, the king returned to his palace.

questions.question: Which portion of the Pañchāla kingdom did Drupada bestow upon Droṇa?
questions.explanation: Drupada bestowed the northern half of his kingdom upon Droṇa.

questions.question: After receiving half of the Pañchāla kingdom, where did Droṇa go with the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: Droṇa left the Pañchāla kingdom with the Pāṇḍavas for Hastināpura.

questions.question: Who did Drupada employ to perform the necessary rituals before ceding his land to Droṇa?
questions.explanation: Drupada had Brahmins perform the appropriate rituals before bestowing half of his kingdom upon Droṇa.

1.6.20.paragraph_text: As they returned to Hastināpura, Droṇa rode on Arjuna’s chariot and spoke to him affectionately. He loved this prince as dearly as his own son, and he knew there was nothing Arjuna would not do for him. Droṇa said, “O hero, you are now the best of all bowmen in this world. Although you have repaid me by defeating Drupada, I will ask one more thing from you as dakṣiṇa. You must fight with me when I come to fight with you.”

questions.question: Which specific student did Droṇa choose to ride with on a chariot during the return journey to Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The text states that as they returned to Hastināpura, Droṇa rode on Arjuna’s chariot.

questions.question: What reason did Droṇa give Arjuna for requesting a final dakṣiṇa?
options: To test Arjuna's obedience as a disciple
To ensure his own legacy as a teacher
To secure a future combat engagement between the two
To punish Arjuna for his pride in bowmanship
questions.correct_answer: To secure a future combat engagement between the two
questions.explanation: Droṇa tells Arjuna that the dakṣiṇa he requests is that Arjuna must fight with him when he comes to fight with him.

questions.question: How did Droṇa assess Arjuna’s standing among all the bowmen in the world?
options: As one of the most promising students
As the best of all bowmen
As a warrior nearing the skill level of his own son
As a hero who surpassed even his teacher
questions.correct_answer: As the best of all bowmen
questions.explanation: Droṇa directly addresses Arjuna as the best of all bowmen in the world.

questions.question: Which king had Arjuna defeated prior to Droṇa’s request for a final dakṣiṇa?
questions.explanation: Droṇa mentions that Arjuna has already repaid him by defeating Drupada.

1.6.21.paragraph_text: Arjuna was surprised. How could he ever fight with his teacher? Still, he replied without hesitating, “It shall be so. I am always your servant.”

questions.question: What reaction did Arjuna have when Droṇa requested that he fight with him as his final dakṣiṇa?
questions.explanation: The text states that Arjuna was surprised by the request to fight his teacher.

questions.question: How did Arjuna respond to the request from his teacher regarding their future combat?
options: He asked for time to consult with his brothers
He replied without hesitating that he would comply
He stated he could only fight if the war was just
He questioned whether such a fight was righteous
questions.correct_answer: He replied without hesitating that he would comply
questions.explanation: Arjuna answered 'It shall be so' and declared himself a servant without hesitating.

questions.question: What identity did Arjuna claim for himself in relation to his teacher, Droṇa?
questions.explanation: Arjuna explicitly told his teacher, 'I am always your servant.'

1.6.22.paragraph_text: The Kurus had heard of a wonderful occurrence in Mathurā, the city where Kuntī had been born. Kuntī had a brother named Vasudeva who had been imprisoned by the wicked King Kaṁsa. This powerful monarch had been viciously terrorizing Brahmins and other kings. Hearing a divine prophesy that Vasudeva’s eighth child would kill him, Kaṁsa imprisoned both Vasudeva and his wife Devakī. He then killed their first six children at birth. But somehow, despite Kaṁsa’s vigilance, the seventh and eighth children, Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa, had escaped death. By some mystical arrangement Kṛṣṇa had been carried away from Mathurā and raised in Vṛndāvana, a small village of cowherds, by its leader, Nanda. The great ṛṣis said that Kṛṣṇa was in fact the Supreme Lord and that it had been by his own divine arrangement he had gone to Vṛndāvana. The Kurus were amazed to hear from the sages that God himself had appeared in the neighboring kingdom of the Yadus.

questions.question: Which city is identified as the birthplace of Kuntī?
questions.explanation: The text states that Kuntī had been born in the city of Mathurā.

questions.question: Who was imprisoned by King Kaṁsa because of a divine prophecy regarding his eighth child?
questions.explanation: The text explains that Vasudeva was imprisoned by Kaṁsa after a divine prophecy stated his eighth child would kill the king.

questions.question: How many of Vasudeva and Devakī's children did Kaṁsa kill at birth?
questions.explanation: The text states that Kaṁsa killed the first six children of Vasudeva and Devakī at birth.

questions.question: Who raised Kṛṣṇa in the village of Vṛndāvana?
questions.explanation: The text identifies Nanda as the leader of Vṛndāvana who raised Kṛṣṇa after he was carried away from Mathurā.

questions.question: What reason did the great ṛṣis give for the appearance of Kṛṣṇa in the kingdom of the Yadus?
options: To reclaim the throne of Mathurā
To protect the Brahmins from Kaṁsa
That he was the Supreme Lord appearing by his own divine arrangement
To unite the Kuru and Yadu dynasties
questions.correct_answer: That he was the Supreme Lord appearing by his own divine arrangement
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states the great ṛṣis identified Kṛṣṇa as the Supreme Lord, who came to Vṛndāvana by his own divine arrangement.

1.6.23.paragraph_text: When he became a youth, Kṛṣṇa had returned to Mathurā and killed Kaṁsa with his bare hands. This tremendous deed astonished all those who saw it and confirmed for them Kṛṣṇa’s divine identity. Kaṁsa had struck fear even into the gods’ hearts. There had been no earthly king capable of standing against him. But the young Kṛṣṇa, still only a boy, and His brother, Balarāma, had overpowered Kaṁsa’s troops and generals, finally killing the king along with his evil ministers. The two brothers then became chiefs of the Yadu dynasty. They maintained a friendship with the Kurus in Hastināpura, taking a special interest in the welfare of their Aunt Kuntī and her five fatherless sons.

questions.question: How did Kṛṣṇa terminate the life of King Kaṁsa in Mathurā?
options: By using his divine disc weapon
By striking him with a mace
Through an arrow shot from a distance
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that when Kṛṣṇa reached youth, he returned to Mathurā and killed Kaṁsa with his bare hands.

questions.question: Besides Kaṁsa, who else did Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma eliminate during the conflict in Mathurā?
options: The evil ministers of the king
The leaders of the Vṛndāvana cowherds
questions.correct_answer: The evil ministers of the king
questions.explanation: The text confirms that Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma killed King Kaṁsa along with his evil ministers.

questions.question: What position did Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma assume after the downfall of Kaṁsa in Mathurā?
options: They became commanders of the Kuru army
They became priests of the Yadu dynasty
They became chiefs of the Yadu dynasty
They became advisors to the Kurus in Hastināpura
questions.correct_answer: They became chiefs of the Yadu dynasty
questions.explanation: After killing the king and his ministers, the two brothers became chiefs of the Yadu dynasty.

questions.question: Which specific family members did Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma maintain a special interest in following their rise to power?
options: Kuntī and her five fatherless sons
The ministers of the former Yadu king
The sages of the Kuru kingdom
questions.correct_answer: Kuntī and her five fatherless sons
questions.explanation: The text states that after becoming Yadu chiefs, they maintained a friendship with the Kurus, specifically taking an interest in the welfare of their aunt Kuntī and her five fatherless sons.

1.6.24.paragraph_text: Balarāma, also said by the ṛṣis to be a manifestation of the Supreme Godhead, became famous as a peerless mace fighter. At Droṇa’s request, he agreed to train Bhīma and Duryodhana in the skills of handling a mace. Thus Balarāma spent time in Hastināpura. While there, he learned of the fierce rivalry and envy the Kauravas felt toward their cousins. When Kṛṣṇa heard this, He became concerned for Kuntī and her sons. Therefore He asked one of his advisors, Akrūra, to visit Hastināpura and assess the situation, and to see if he could be of any help.

questions.question: Which specific weapon style is Balarāma celebrated for mastering?
questions.explanation: Balarāma is described as a peerless mace fighter.

questions.question: Which two royal figures received combat training from Balarāma?
questions.explanation: Balarāma agreed to train Bhīma and Duryodhana in the skills of handling a mace at the request of Droṇa.

questions.question: Who requested that Balarāma provide instruction to the Kaurava and Pāṇḍava princes?
questions.explanation: The text states that Balarāma trained the students specifically at the request of Droṇa.

questions.question: Whom did Kṛṣṇa send to Hastināpura to evaluate the safety of the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: Kṛṣṇa sent his advisor Akrūra to Hastināpura to assess the situation regarding the rivalry toward Kuntī and her sons.

questions.question: What observation did Balarāma make regarding the relationship between the Kauravas and their cousins while staying in Hastināpura?
options: They maintained a strong, unbreakable fraternal bond.
They were indifferent toward one another.
They harbored fierce rivalry and envy.
They were engaged in a secret alliance against Kṛṣṇa.
questions.correct_answer: They harbored fierce rivalry and envy.
questions.explanation: While in Hastināpura, Balarāma learned of the fierce rivalry and envy the Kauravas felt toward their cousins.

1.6.25.paragraph_text: The Kurus received Akrūra in friendship. Kuntī was overjoyed to see him. She inquired about her kinsmen and friends in Mathurā. Kuntī especially wanted to know everything about Kṛṣṇa, whom she accepted as the Supreme Lord. “Does my nephew Kṛṣṇa think of me and my sons?” she asked. “Does he know how I am suffering in the midst of my enemies, like a doe in the midst of wolves?”

questions.question: Whom did Kuntī acknowledge as the Supreme Lord?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Kuntī accepted Kṛṣṇa as the Supreme Lord.

questions.question: What comparison did Kuntī use to describe her situation among the Kurus?
options: A doe in the midst of wolves
A lone traveler in a dark forest
A small boat in a great storm
questions.correct_answer: A doe in the midst of wolves
questions.explanation: Kuntī describes her suffering and vulnerability among her enemies by comparing herself to a doe in the midst of wolves.

questions.question: Regarding which city did Kuntī inquire about her kinsmen and friends?
questions.explanation: When greeting Akrūra, Kuntī inquired about her friends and relatives specifically in Mathurā.

1.6.26.paragraph_text: Akrūra asked her to tell him more about the situation in the city and she began explaining everything. Kuntī knew that Duryodhana and his brothers were always intriguing against her sons. The Kauravas could not tolerate the Pāṇḍavas’ superior prowess. The humiliation they had recently received at Kāmpilya had made them even more keen to dispose of Pāṇḍu’s sons. Kuntī spoke to Akrūra with tears in her eyes, “Will Kṛṣṇa come here to console me? I always pray to that all-powerful protector of the universe. Indeed, I see no other shelter for myself and my sons.”

questions.question: Which specific location was the site of the recent humiliation suffered by the Kauravas, further fueling their desire to harm the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that the humiliation the Kauravas recently received at Kāmpilya made them more determined to dispose of Pāṇḍu’s sons.

questions.question: Why did the Kauravas harbor such intense animosity toward the Pāṇḍavas?
options: Because of a disputed inheritance of the throne
Because of the Pāṇḍavas' superior prowess
Due to a disagreement over ancestral lands
Because the Pāṇḍavas refused to pay taxes
questions.correct_answer: Because of the Pāṇḍavas' superior prowess
questions.explanation: The text states that the Kauravas could not tolerate the Pāṇḍavas’ superior prowess.

questions.question: Whom does Kuntī identify as the all-powerful protector of the universe to whom she prays for shelter?
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly refers to Kṛṣṇa as the all-powerful protector of the universe and her only shelter.

1.6.27.paragraph_text: Kuntī cried out to Kṣṇa in front of Akrūra. He gently reassured her that Kṛṣṇa was often speaking about her and had sent him to analyze the situation. Both Akrūra and Vidura comforted Kuntī and reminded her about her sons’ extraordinary birth. The Pāṇḍava boys were expansions of the gods. There was no way that the evil Kaurava princes could overcome them.

questions.question: Whom did Akrūra and Vidura comfort while speaking of the extraordinary origins of the Pāṇḍava brothers?
questions.explanation: The text states that Akrūra and Vidura comforted Kuntī and reminded her of her sons' extraordinary birth.

questions.question: What reason did Akrūra give Kuntī for his presence in the city?
options: He was sent by Kṛṣṇa to analyze the situation.
He was sent by Bhīṣma to protect the Pāṇḍavas.
He came to invite the Pāṇḍavas to Mathurā.
He was sent by Dhṛtarāṣṭra to discuss the throne.
questions.correct_answer: He was sent by Kṛṣṇa to analyze the situation.
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Akrūra reassured Kuntī that Kṛṣṇa had sent him to analyze the situation.

questions.question: How did Akrūra and Vidura describe the nature of the Pāṇḍava boys?
options: As warriors destined to conquer the world.
As protectors of the Vedic tradition.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly describes the Pāṇḍava boys as expansions of the gods.

1.6.28.paragraph_text: Akrūra remained in Hastināpura for several months. Then, when he felt he had understood the situation fully, he decided it was time to return to Mathurā. Before leaving, however, he met with Dhṛtarāṣṭra to offer some advice. Ultimately the blind king was responsible for his sons’ acts and he could certainly check their behavior if he chose. Akrūra said, “My dear King, you have obtained the throne only because your brother Pāṇḍu passed away prematurely. Therefore Pāṇḍu’s sons have first claim on the throne. You should not discriminate against them in favor of your own sons.”

questions.question: For how long did Akrūra stay in Hastināpura before deciding to return to Mathurā?
questions.explanation: The text states that Akrūra remained in Hastināpura for several months before deciding it was time to return to Mathurā.

questions.question: Why did Akrūra remind Dhṛtarāṣṭra that Pāṇḍu had passed away prematurely?
options: To warn him of a looming curse
To justify his presence in Hastināpura
To argue that Pāṇḍu’s sons had the first claim to the throne
To explain why the kingdom was currently unstable
questions.correct_answer: To argue that Pāṇḍu’s sons had the first claim to the throne
questions.explanation: Akrūra informed Dhṛtarāṣṭra that he obtained the throne only because Pāṇḍu died prematurely, and therefore Pāṇḍu’s sons possessed the rightful first claim.

questions.question: What responsibility did Akrūra emphasize that Dhṛtarāṣṭra held regarding the actions of his sons?
options: Dhṛtarāṣṭra was responsible for their education
Dhṛtarāṣṭra was responsible for their marriage arrangements
Dhṛtarāṣṭra was responsible for their acts and could check their behavior
Dhṛtarāṣṭra was responsible for their military training
questions.correct_answer: Dhṛtarāṣṭra was responsible for their acts and could check their behavior
questions.explanation: Akrūra explicitly told the blind king that he was responsible for his sons’ acts and had the power to check their behavior if he so chose.

1.6.29.paragraph_text: Akrūra also advised Dhṛtarāṣṭra to rule the kingdom strictly according to moral principles. He should treat all his subjects equally, what to speak of the Pāṇḍavas, his own nephews and heirs to the throne. Over-attachment for one’s close relatives is simply born of ignorance. Every creature in the world is born alone and dies alone. He experiences the results of his own good and evil deeds and in the end leaves the present body to accept another. The belief that one person is the relation of another is nothing more than illusion.

questions.question: What primary reason does Akrūra give for why Dhṛtarāṣṭra should treat the Pāṇḍavas equally with his own sons?
options: The Pāṇḍavas are his nephews and legal heirs to the throne.
The Pāṇḍavas possess greater military skill than his sons.
The gods have commanded the Pāṇḍavas to be protected.
The Pāṇḍavas have provided him with significant wealth.
questions.correct_answer: The Pāṇḍavas are his nephews and legal heirs to the throne.
questions.explanation: Akrūra specifically tells Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat all subjects equally, especially the Pāṇḍavas, who are identified as his nephews and heirs to the throne.

questions.question: According to Akrūra's advice to Dhṛtarāṣṭra, what is the ultimate source of over-attachment to one's close relatives?
questions.explanation: The text states that over-attachment for one's close relatives is simply born of ignorance.

questions.question: What fundamental reality of human existence does Akrūra cite to highlight the illusory nature of relationships?
options: Humans are inherently connected through divine grace.
Every creature is born alone and dies alone.
All beings are governed by the same planetary movements.
Relationships are the only lasting legacy one leaves behind.
questions.correct_answer: Every creature is born alone and dies alone.
questions.explanation: Akrūra emphasizes that every creature is born and dies alone to illustrate that the belief in being related to another is an illusion.

questions.question: In the context of the journey of the soul, what determines what a person experiences after leaving their present body?
options: The prayers offered by their living relatives.
The wealth they accumulated during their lifetime.
The results of their own good and evil deeds.
The social status they held while living.
questions.correct_answer: The results of their own good and evil deeds.
questions.explanation: Akrūra explains that a person experiences the results of their own good and evil deeds before leaving their body to accept another.

1.6.30.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra listened in silence as Akrūra spoke. He understood the implications of his words. Akrūra had made it clear that the Pāṇḍavas’ cause was righteous and that by opposing them he would reap only grief. Dhṛtarāṣṭra bowed his head as Akrūra concluded, “By favoring your own sons, O lord of the earth, you are acting out of ignorance. How then can you hope for any good result? Ignorance always leads to sorrow. Therefore, act virtuously and deal with Pāṇḍu’s sons as they rightfully deserve.”

questions.question: What primary consequence did Akrūra predict would result from Dhṛtarāṣṭra's opposition to the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: Akrūra stated that by opposing the Pāṇḍavas, Dhṛtarāṣṭra would reap only grief.

questions.question: According to Akrūra, what is the ultimate outcome of acting out of ignorance by favoring one's own sons?
questions.explanation: Akrūra explicitly warns that acting out of ignorance by favoring one's sons leads to sorrow.

questions.question: How did Dhṛtarāṣṭra physically react after listening to Akrūra's final admonition?
options: He turned his back on Akrūra
questions.explanation: The text notes that Dhṛtarāṣṭra bowed his head as Akrūra concluded his speech.

1.6.31.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra sighed, “O wise one, your words are like the immortal nectar of the gods. I could go on hearing them forever. You have surely spoken the truth. But just as a person on the verge of death cannot be saved by nectar, so your instructions do not stay in my heart.”

questions.question: To what does Dhṛtarāṣṭra compare the words spoken by Akrūra?
options: The cooling waters of the Ganges
The immortal nectar of the gods
The sharp sting of an arrow
The wisdom of the ancient sages
questions.correct_answer: The immortal nectar of the gods
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra explicitly compares Akrūra's words to the immortal nectar of the gods.

questions.question: Why does Dhṛtarāṣṭra claim that Akrūra's instructions fail to remain in his heart?
options: Because the words are too complex to understand
Because he believes Akrūra is lying
Because he is like a person on the verge of death whom nectar cannot save
Because his heart is already filled with the wisdom of the Pāṇḍavas
questions.correct_answer: Because he is like a person on the verge of death whom nectar cannot save
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra uses the analogy that just as nectar cannot save one on the verge of death, the instructions do not stay in his heart.

1.6.32.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra admitted that he was prejudiced by affection for his sons. He told Akrūra that he felt helpless to overcome that affection. Like Kuntī, the king also understood Kṛṣṇa’s position. “Surely everything moves according to the will of the Supreme Lord. No man can influence the Lord’s will. Now he has appeared to relieve the earth’s burden and that will surely happen. What then can I do? Destiny is surely all-powerful.”

questions.question: What internal factor does Dhṛtarāṣṭra admit is the cause of his prejudice?
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra explicitly admits that his prejudice is caused by his affection for his sons.

questions.question: To whom does Dhṛtarāṣṭra confess his inability to overcome his partiality?
questions.explanation: The text states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra told Akrūra that he felt helpless to overcome the affection he held for his sons.

questions.question: According to Dhṛtarāṣṭra, which individual shares his understanding of Kṛṣṇa’s position?
questions.explanation: The paragraph notes that like Kuntī, the king understood Kṛṣṇa’s position regarding his appearance on earth.

questions.question: What reason does Dhṛtarāṣṭra give for Kṛṣṇa’s appearance on earth?
options: To teach the Vedas to humanity
To grant liberation to the kings
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra declares that Kṛṣṇa has appeared to relieve the earth’s burden.

questions.question: How does Dhṛtarāṣṭra characterize the power of destiny in relation to the Supreme Lord’s will?
options: As easily influenced by mortal kings
As irrelevant to the Lord's plan
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra states, 'Destiny is surely all-powerful,' while acknowledging that nothing moves outside the will of the Supreme Lord.

1.6.33.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra had heard the sages describe how Kṛṣṇa’s mission was to destroy the large number of demonic kings who had begun to populate the earth. Pāṇḍu had managed to check them, but since his retirement they had formed alliances and built huge armies, posing a constant threat to world security.

questions.question: What primary objective of Kṛṣṇa did Dhṛtarāṣṭra learn about from the sages?
options: To establish a new capital in Mathurā
To destroy demonic kings populating the earth
To personally rule over the Kuru kingdom
To expand the borders of the Hastināpura empire
questions.correct_answer: To destroy demonic kings populating the earth
questions.explanation: The text states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra heard from sages that Kṛṣṇa's mission was to destroy the large number of demonic kings populating the earth.

questions.question: Who was responsible for initially checking the power of the demonic kings before retiring?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that Pāṇḍu had managed to check the demonic kings until his retirement.

questions.question: What actions did the demonic kings take following Pāṇḍu's retirement to increase their power?
options: They petitioned the sages for blessings
They formed alliances and built huge armies
They migrated to the kingdom of Mathurā
They surrendered their lands to Dhṛtarāṣṭra
questions.correct_answer: They formed alliances and built huge armies
questions.explanation: The text notes that after Pāṇḍu's retirement, the demonic kings formed alliances and built huge armies to threaten world security.

1.6.34.paragraph_text: Akrūra could understand that Dhṛtarāṣṭra was set upon a course that would lead to his ruin. By favoring his own sons over the Pāṇḍavas, the king would ultimately ignite a conflict between them which would result in the destruction of the Kuru race. Akrūra felt there was nothing more he could do. Dhṛtarāṣṭra refused to accept responsibility for his acts. Taking his leave from the king, Akrūra made his way back to Mathurā.

questions.question: What outcome did Akrūra foresee as a result of Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s decision to favor his own sons over the Pāṇḍavas?
options: The expansion of the Kuru kingdom
The destruction of the Kuru race
The exile of the Pāṇḍavas to the forest
questions.correct_answer: The destruction of the Kuru race
questions.explanation: Akrūra understood that Dhṛtarāṣṭra's favoritism would ignite a conflict leading to the destruction of the Kuru race.

questions.question: To which city did Akrūra return after failing to convince Dhṛtarāṣṭra to accept responsibility for his actions?
questions.explanation: The text states that after taking his leave from the king, Akrūra made his way back to Mathurā.

questions.question: What specific behavior by Dhṛtarāṣṭra led Akrūra to believe that the king was headed for his own ruin?
options: His refusal to accept responsibility for his acts
His decision to attack the neighboring kingdoms
His rejection of the advice from his ministers
His reliance on demonic kings for counsel
questions.correct_answer: His refusal to accept responsibility for his acts
questions.explanation: Akrūra realized the king's impending ruin because Dhṛtarāṣṭra refused to accept responsibility for his acts while favoring his own sons.

1.6.35.paragraph_text: When Akrūra was gone, Dhṛtarāṣṭra pondered on his words. It was true that Pāṇḍu’s sons were the rightful heirs to the kingdom. That could not be denied. It was especially clear that the eldest of them, Yudhiṣṭhira, was qualified to be the king. The king had seen how the prince was noted for his honesty, patience, kindness and unswerving adherence to duty. Along with his brothers, he was a firm favorite of the people. The citizens had loved Pāṇḍu and it seemed to them that he returned to live among them as his sons. Everywhere people were speaking of their desire to see Yudhiṣṭhira installed as the king. Dhṛtarāṣṭra had heard of their talks: “Now we have a qualified prince. Why should the blind Dhṛtarāṣṭra remain king? Let us place the pious Yudhiṣṭhira on the throne. He will surely be a righteous and benevolent ruler.”

questions.question: Which specific traits of Yudhiṣṭhira are explicitly recognized by Dhṛtarāṣṭra as qualities of an ideal king?
options: Intelligence, wealth, physical strength, and wisdom
Honesty, patience, kindness, and unswerving adherence to duty
Courage, diplomatic skill, military prowess, and piety
Charisma, wealth, lineage, and physical stature
questions.correct_answer: Honesty, patience, kindness, and unswerving adherence to duty
questions.explanation: The paragraph states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra noted these four specific virtues in Yudhiṣṭhira while considering his suitability for the throne.

questions.question: Why did the citizens of the kingdom believe that the era of Pāṇḍu had effectively returned?
options: Because Pāṇḍu had secretly returned from the forest
Because the citizens saw Pāṇḍu's sons as living embodiments of Pāṇḍu's presence
Because Dhṛtarāṣṭra stepped down voluntarily to honor Pāṇḍu
Because the kingdom had suddenly become prosperous after Pāṇḍu's death
questions.correct_answer: Because the citizens saw Pāṇḍu's sons as living embodiments of Pāṇḍu's presence
questions.explanation: The text notes that the citizens loved Pāṇḍu and felt that he had returned to live among them in the form of his sons.

questions.question: What primary argument did the citizens use to justify placing Yudhiṣṭhira on the throne instead of Dhṛtarāṣṭra?
options: That Dhṛtarāṣṭra had become too old to rule
That Yudhiṣṭhira was a qualified prince and a righteous, benevolent ruler
That the gods had commanded the change in leadership
That the treasury was being mismanaged by the current king
questions.correct_answer: That Yudhiṣṭhira was a qualified prince and a righteous, benevolent ruler
questions.explanation: The citizens argued that since a qualified prince like the pious Yudhiṣṭhira was available, he should be installed as the righteous and benevolent ruler instead of the blind Dhṛtarāṣṭra.

questions.question: What internal acknowledgment did Dhṛtarāṣṭra make regarding the inheritance rights of the kingdom?
options: He admitted that his own sons were the only legitimate heirs
He acknowledged that Pāṇḍu's sons were the rightful heirs
He decided to divide the kingdom equally among all nephews
He claimed the kingdom belonged to him by right of seniority
questions.correct_answer: He acknowledged that Pāṇḍu's sons were the rightful heirs
questions.explanation: The paragraph states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra pondered the words of Akrūra and could not deny that Pāṇḍu's sons were the rightful heirs to the kingdom.

1.6.36.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra consulted with Bhīṣma, Vidura and the Brahmins. They all decided that Yudhiṣṭhira should be installed as prince regent. The ceremony was soon performed and the people rejoiced.

questions.question: Which three parties did Dhṛtarāṣṭra consult before making a decision regarding the prince regent?
options: The citizens, the ministers, and the warriors
The Pāṇḍavas, the Kauravas, and the elders
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Dhṛtarāṣṭra consulted with Bhīṣma, Vidura, and the Brahmins.

questions.question: What official position was Yudhiṣṭhira installed into following the council's decision?
questions.explanation: The text states that the council decided Yudhiṣṭhira should be installed as prince regent.

1.6.37.paragraph_text: Duryodhana, however, was seething. How had his father bypassed him to make Yudhiṣṭhira prince regent? When Bhīma sneered at his distress, making it even more unbearable, he went with Karṇa and Dushashana to discuss with Śakuni a way to eradicate the Pāṇḍavas.

questions.question: Which specific individual was installed as prince regent in place of Duryodhana?
questions.explanation: The text states that Duryodhana was upset because his father bypassed him to make Yudhiṣṭhira prince regent.

questions.question: Which characters accompanied Duryodhana to consult with Śakuni regarding the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana went with Karṇa and Dushashana to discuss a way to eradicate the Pāṇḍavas with Śakuni.

questions.question: Whose mockery made Duryodhana's distress over the succession ceremony even more unbearable?
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Bhīma sneered at Duryodhana's distress, intensifying his frustration.

1.6.38.paragraph_text: Śakuni’s eyes narrowed as Duryodhana and the others vented their rage. He pressed his fingertips together to think. “The only answer,” he said finally, “is to get the Pāṇḍavas out of Hastināpura to a place where they can be killed without interference. We should somehow contrive to have them burned to death, making it seem like an accident.”

questions.question: What method did Śakuni suggest to Duryodhana for disposing of the Pāṇḍavas?
options: Exiling them to the deep forest
Burning them to death in an apparent accident
Poisoning their food at a royal feast
Having them killed during a hunting expedition
questions.correct_answer: Burning them to death in an apparent accident
questions.explanation: Śakuni proposed that they should contrive to have the Pāṇḍavas burned to death, framing the event as an accident.

questions.question: Where did Śakuni advise the Pāṇḍavas should be sent to ensure they could be killed without interference?
options: To the borders of the kingdom
questions.explanation: Śakuni suggested getting the Pāṇḍavas out of Hastināpura to a location where they could be killed without interference.

1.6.39.paragraph_text: Duryodhana smiled, but Karṇa was not so sure. He did not like Śakuni’s devious ways. “Only cowards resort to deceit and underhanded methods. Powerful men favor open combat. If the Pāṇḍavas are our enemies, then let us march out to the battlefield and settle this dispute.”

questions.question: What reason did Karṇa provide for opposing the use of deceitful methods against the Pāṇḍavas?
options: He feared the divine wrath of the gods.
He believed only cowards rely on underhanded methods.
He preferred to follow the advice of Bhīṣma.
He believed the Pāṇḍavas were invincible in any form of combat.
questions.correct_answer: He believed only cowards rely on underhanded methods.
questions.explanation: Karṇa explicitly states that only cowards resort to deceit and underhanded methods when expressing his disapproval of Śakuni's plan.

questions.question: What method did Karṇa advocate for resolving the dispute between the Kauravas and the Pāṇḍavas?
options: Exiling them to a distant forest.
Engaging in open combat on the battlefield.
Negotiating a peaceful division of the kingdom.
Using political influence to isolate them from the elders.
questions.correct_answer: Engaging in open combat on the battlefield.
questions.explanation: Karṇa argues that powerful men favor open combat and suggests marching out to the battlefield to settle the dispute.

questions.question: Which character reacted to Śakuni’s proposal with skepticism and moral disapproval regarding the use of devious methods?
questions.explanation: The text identifies Karṇa as the individual who was not sure about the plan and expressed dislike for Śakuni’s devious ways.

1.6.40.paragraph_text: Śakuni’s lips tightened. Then he smiled slightly. “My child, you are powerful but foolish. It seems you have forgotten Bhīma’s superhuman strength. And do you not remember the incident at Kāmpilya? All of the Kaurava princes, with you by their side, could not overpower Drupada. But with only four fighting, the Pāṇḍavas were successful. It is unlikely that we will win in a confrontation with those five brothers. Take heed of my words.”

questions.question: Which specific location did Śakuni reference to remind the Kauravas of their failure to defeat King Drupada?
questions.explanation: Śakuni explicitly references the incident at Kāmpilya where the Kaurava princes were unable to overpower Drupada.

questions.question: According to Śakuni, what quality did the Pāṇḍavas possess that made them superior to the Kaurava princes during the conflict at Kāmpilya?
options: Successful coordination among only four fighters
questions.correct_answer: Successful coordination among only four fighters
questions.explanation: Śakuni points out that while the entire Kaurava force failed, the Pāṇḍavas were successful with only four fighting.

questions.question: Which individual's superhuman strength does Śakuni specifically warn the Kauravas not to forget?
questions.explanation: Śakuni warns the princes that they have forgotten Bhīma's superhuman strength.

questions.question: How many brothers did Śakuni identify as being part of the group that defeated Drupada, thereby making an open confrontation unlikely to succeed for the Kauravas?
questions.explanation: Śakuni concludes that it is unlikely the Kauravas would win in a confrontation with the five Pāṇḍava brothers.

1.6.41.paragraph_text: Reminded of Kāmpilya, Karṇa was embarrassed. He let out an angry shout. They had been taken by surprise there. Drupada had been stronger than they expected. The Pāṇḍavas had the advantage, confronting Drupada after having witnessed his actual power. Next time, if the Pāṇḍavas confronted the Kauravas directly, things would be different. Karṇa shook his head and left the room. “Do what you will, but I cannot be a party to such cowardice.”

questions.question: Why did Karṇa feel embarrassed during his discussion with Śakuni?
options: Because he was reminded of the events at Kāmpilya
Because he was insulted by Duryodhana
Because he was defeated by the Pāṇḍavas in a duel
Because he was accused of stealing wealth
questions.correct_answer: Because he was reminded of the events at Kāmpilya
questions.explanation: Karṇa was embarrassed specifically because he was reminded of the incident at Kāmpilya.

questions.question: What explanation did Karṇa give for the Kauravas' failure against Drupada at Kāmpilya?
options: They were outnumbered by Drupada's massive army
They were taken by surprise and underestimated Drupada's strength
They were betrayed by a spy within their ranks
The Pāṇḍavas refused to support their efforts
questions.correct_answer: They were taken by surprise and underestimated Drupada's strength
questions.explanation: Karṇa reasoned that they were taken by surprise and that Drupada possessed more strength than they had anticipated.

questions.question: What specific reason did Karṇa provide for the Pāṇḍavas' success against Drupada?
options: The Pāṇḍavas had a divine weapon from Indra
The Pāṇḍavas confronted Drupada only after witnessing his true power
The Pāṇḍavas had bribed Drupada's generals
The Pāṇḍavas were aided by an earthquake that weakened Drupada's fort
questions.correct_answer: The Pāṇḍavas confronted Drupada only after witnessing his true power
questions.explanation: Karṇa attributed the Pāṇḍavas' advantage to the fact that they attacked Drupada having already seen his actual strength.

questions.question: Why did Karṇa leave the room at the conclusion of his conversation with Śakuni?
options: He wanted to prepare for a secret mission
He refused to participate in what he characterized as cowardice
He was exiled for his insolence
questions.correct_answer: He refused to participate in what he characterized as cowardice
questions.explanation: Karṇa left the room declaring he could not be a party to the cowardice proposed by Śakuni.

1.6.42.paragraph_text: The cunning Śakuni had carefully assessed the situation. He pressed Duryodhana to approach his father and ask for the Pāṇḍavas to be sent away. He knew that Dhṛtarāṣṭra would not refuse his son anything. Duryodhana nodded slowly. He trusted Śakuni’s judgment. Together they worked out the details of their plan. Then Duryodhana went to see the king.

questions.question: Whose judgment did Duryodhana trust when planning to have the Pāṇḍavas sent away?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Duryodhana trusted Śakuni’s judgment when they worked out the details of the plan to approach the king.

questions.question: What did Śakuni suggest Duryodhana request from his father, King Dhṛtarāṣṭra?
options: The resignation of Yudhiṣṭhira as heir-apparent
That the Pāṇḍavas be sent away
A formal declaration of war against Drupada
questions.correct_answer: That the Pāṇḍavas be sent away
questions.explanation: Śakuni pressed Duryodhana to approach his father and ask for the Pāṇḍavas to be sent away.

questions.question: What assumption did Śakuni make regarding King Dhṛtarāṣṭra's relationship with his son?
options: That the king would be easily manipulated by external ministers
That the king would not refuse Duryodhana anything
That the king feared the power of the Pāṇḍavas
That the king wished to retire from political life
questions.correct_answer: That the king would not refuse Duryodhana anything
questions.explanation: The text notes that Śakuni knew Dhṛtarāṣṭra would not refuse his son anything, which formed the basis of his strategy.

1.6.43.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra knew well of his sons’ hatred for the Pāṇḍavas. He knew that Yudhiṣṭhira’s installation as the heir-apparent had been a bitter blow to Duryodhana. The king thought about what he could do for his sons. He had spoken with Śakuni. His brother-in-law, knowing that Dhṛtarāṣṭra never acted without counsel, had suggested that he seek advice from Śakuni’s Brahmin friend, Kaṇika. This Brahmin, Śakuni had said, was an expert in statecraft and politics. Śakuni then personally brought Kaṇika to see Dhṛtarāṣṭra.

questions.question: Whose installation as heir-apparent was considered a bitter blow to Duryodhana?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Yudhiṣṭhira’s installation as the heir-apparent was a bitter blow to Duryodhana.

questions.question: Who was the Brahmin that Śakuni recommended to Dhṛtarāṣṭra for advice on statecraft and politics?
questions.explanation: The text identifies Kaṇika as the Brahmin friend of Śakuni who was an expert in statecraft and politics.

questions.question: What was the familial relationship between Śakuni and King Dhṛtarāṣṭra?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly refers to Śakuni as Dhṛtarāṣṭra's brother-in-law.

1.6.44.paragraph_text: Kaṇika told Dhṛtarāṣṭra that he should feel no compunction about rooting out his enemies before they harmed him. If he saw the Pāṇḍavas as his enemies, then he should destroy them without hesitation by any means possible. Because they were stronger than his own sons, a direct confrontation would probably fail. Better to employ some devious means. In the meantime the king should continue to appear as the Pāṇḍavas’ well-wisher. Then as soon as an opportunity arose, he should strike.

questions.question: What advice did Kaṇika give to King Dhṛtarāṣṭra regarding the Pāṇḍavas?
options: To abdicate the throne in favor of Yudhiṣṭhira
To destroy them using devious means
To engage them in a direct confrontation on the battlefield
To offer them half of the kingdom to avoid war
questions.correct_answer: To destroy them using devious means
questions.explanation: Kaṇika specifically advised that because the Pāṇḍavas were stronger than his own sons, a direct confrontation would fail and it would be better to employ devious means.

questions.question: Why did Kaṇika advise against a direct confrontation between the Pāṇḍavas and the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra?
options: The Pāṇḍavas were stronger than his own sons
The Pāṇḍavas were protected by divine intervention
The Pāṇḍavas held more land than the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra
The Pāṇḍavas had the support of the elderly Bhīṣma
questions.correct_answer: The Pāṇḍavas were stronger than his own sons
questions.explanation: Kaṇika argued that a direct confrontation would likely fail because the Pāṇḍavas were physically or strategically stronger than the king's own sons.

questions.question: What behavior did Kaṇika recommend King Dhṛtarāṣṭra adopt toward the Pāṇḍavas while waiting for an opportunity to strike?
options: Banish them from the kingdom immediately
Continue to appear as their well-wisher
Challenge them to a public debate
questions.correct_answer: Continue to appear as their well-wisher
questions.explanation: Kaṇika advised that while waiting for an opportunity to strike, the king should maintain the appearance of being a well-wisher to the Pāṇḍavas.

questions.question: How did Kaṇika justify the need to feel no compunction when removing enemies?
options: Because they were a threat before they could cause harm
Because the law of the jungle allowed it
Because they had offended the Brahmins
Because they were not true princes
questions.correct_answer: Because they were a threat before they could cause harm
questions.explanation: Kaṇika told the king he should feel no compunction about rooting out his enemies before they had the chance to harm him.

1.6.45.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra thanked the Brahmin for his advice and dismissed him. He sat alone in his chamber for some time. The thought of killing Pāṇḍu’s sons distressed him. Perhaps there was some way they could be removed from Hastināpura. While they were present his own sons would never be happy. Duryodhana complained constantly about his cousins. Now the monarchy was about to pass back to Pāṇḍu’s line. Dhṛtarāṣṭra had enjoyed his opportunity to occupy Hastināpura’s throne as the world emperor. Although the firstborn son, and thus the first in line for the throne, he had thought his blindness had forever denied him the chance to be king. But in the years since Pāṇḍu’s departure he had become accustomed to holding the reins of power. It would not be easy to let go.

questions.question: What primary reason initially caused Dhṛtarāṣṭra to believe he would be denied his position as king of Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The text notes that although Dhṛtarāṣṭra was the firstborn son, he had believed his blindness had permanently prevented him from ascending to the throne.

questions.question: Which individual's sons did Dhṛtarāṣṭra feel would never be happy as long as their cousins remained in Hastināpura?
questions.explanation: The passage identifies that Duryodhana complained constantly about his cousins and that Dhṛtarāṣṭra's own sons would not be happy while Pāṇḍu's sons were present.

questions.question: What concern did Dhṛtarāṣṭra hold regarding the impending transfer of the monarchy?
options: The Pāṇḍavas would reject the kingdom
The monarchy was returning to Pāṇḍu's line
Duryodhana would refuse to serve the king
The citizens of Hastināpura would revolt
questions.correct_answer: The monarchy was returning to Pāṇḍu's line
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra reflects on the fact that the monarchy was about to pass back to the line of Pāṇḍu, which he found difficult to accept.

1.6.46.paragraph_text: As Dhṛtarāṣṭra sat in his darkened room, Duryodhana came to see him. He heard his son’s heavy steps approaching and his sighs as he sat before him. Dhṛtarāṣṭra gently greeted the prince and asked what ailed him.

1.6.47.paragraph_text: “I am hearing ill news of the kingdom, Father. The citizens are growing restless. They want you to soon hand over the throne to the Pāṇḍavas. ‘Why should we have the blind king now that Yudhiṣṭhira is grown?’ are the words they utter. They care not for you or for myself.”

questions.question: What reason does Duryodhana cite for the citizens' growing restlessness?
options: The lack of protection from external invaders
The desire for the throne to be transferred to the Pāṇḍavas
The absence of regular festivals and sacrifices
The economic hardship brought on by the famine
questions.correct_answer: The desire for the throne to be transferred to the Pāṇḍavas
questions.explanation: Duryodhana informs his father that the citizens want him to soon hand over the throne to the Pāṇḍavas.

questions.question: Which specific figure does Duryodhana mention as having grown to adulthood in the eyes of the citizens?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana claims the citizens are asking why they should have a blind king now that Yudhiṣṭhira is grown.

questions.question: How do the citizens perceive the current monarch according to Duryodhana's report?
options: As a wise and impartial judge
As an aging leader in need of rest
As a blind king no longer needed
As a protector of the Vedic dharma
questions.correct_answer: As a blind king no longer needed
questions.explanation: Duryodhana quotes the citizens asking, 'Why should we have the blind king now that Yudhiṣṭhira is grown?'

1.6.48.paragraph_text: Duryodhana stood up sharply and began to pace, his gold ornaments jangling. As he strode about, he punched his hand with his clenched fist.

1.6.49.paragraph_text: “Soon we shall become dependent upon the Pāṇḍavas. They will be the kings and after them their sons will inherit the throne. Thus our line will be plunged into misfortune. We shall become powerless and lose the honor we have long enjoyed. What could be more painful for us? I have been considering how to deliver us. Listen as I explain my idea.”

questions.question: What primary concern does Duryodhana express regarding the future of the royal lineage and the Pāṇḍavas?
options: The Pāṇḍavas will initiate a great war for the throne.
The royal line will become dependent upon the Pāṇḍavas as future kings.
The Pāṇḍavas will refuse to accept their inheritance.
The gods will withdraw their protection from the kingdom.
questions.correct_answer: The royal line will become dependent upon the Pāṇḍavas as future kings.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana explicitly states that they will soon become dependent upon the Pāṇḍavas, who will be the kings and whose sons will inherit the throne.

questions.question: According to Duryodhana's perspective, what is the ultimate fate of their line if the Pāṇḍavas become kings?
options: The line will be plunged into misfortune.
The line will gain greater honor and prosperity.
The line will be exiled to the forest.
The line will be forced to serve the gods.
questions.correct_answer: The line will be plunged into misfortune.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana warns that their line will be plunged into misfortune if the Pāṇḍavas inherit the throne.

questions.question: Besides the loss of power, what does Duryodhana fear losing as a consequence of the Pāṇḍavas' ascendancy?
options: The wealth accumulated in the treasury.
The honor they have long enjoyed.
The control of the royal military forces.
questions.correct_answer: The honor they have long enjoyed.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana specifically mentions that they will become powerless and lose the honor they have long enjoyed.

1.6.50.paragraph_text: Duryodhana first suggested the Pāṇḍavas be sent to a distant city. On the pretext of sending them on holiday, they could be sent to Vāraṇāvata, where a splendid festival honoring Śiva would soon be celebrated. The town was noted for its attractions. The Pāṇḍavas would surely be pleased to visit it. Once they left Hastināpura, perhaps they would never return.

questions.question: Which distant city did Duryodhana propose the Pāṇḍavas should visit?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana suggested that the Pāṇḍavas be sent to the city of Vāraṇāvata on the pretext of a holiday.

questions.question: What public event was scheduled to occur in Vāraṇāvata that served as the excuse for sending the Pāṇḍavas there?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana planned to use the pretext of a splendid festival honoring Śiva, which was soon to be celebrated in Vāraṇāvata, to lure the Pāṇḍavas away from Hastināpura.

questions.question: What was the underlying strategic hope Duryodhana held regarding the Pāṇḍavas' trip to Vāraṇāvata?
options: That they would be converted to his political party
That they would never return to Hastināpura
That they would marry into local noble families
That they would renounce their royal titles
questions.correct_answer: That they would never return to Hastināpura
questions.explanation: Duryodhana believed that once the Pāṇḍavas left Hastināpura for Vāraṇāvata, they might never return.

1.6.51.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra immediately understood what was on his son’s mind. How could he possibly agree? “Pāṇḍu was always devoted to virtue,” he said. “He did not care for wealth. My pious brother was devoted to me. He gave me everything, including this wide and prosperous kingdom. How could I hurt his sons?”

questions.question: How did Dhṛtarāṣṭra characterize Pāṇḍu’s attitude toward wealth?
options: Pāṇḍu was obsessed with hoarding wealth.
Pāṇḍu used wealth to buy the loyalty of his subjects.
Pāṇḍu felt that wealth was the only sign of a king's success.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Pāṇḍu did not care for wealth.

questions.question: What primary virtue did Dhṛtarāṣṭra attribute to his brother Pāṇḍu?
options: A desire to live as a hermit in the forest.
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra refers to Pāṇḍu as being always devoted to virtue.

questions.question: What did Dhṛtarāṣṭra acknowledge Pāṇḍu had given him?
options: A divine weapon to protect Hastināpura.
A promise to never claim the throne.
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra states that Pāṇḍu gave him everything, including the wide and prosperous kingdom.

1.6.52.paragraph_text: Besides that consideration, said Dhṛtarāṣṭra, the people love the Pāṇḍavas. They would be angered if the Kurus sent them away. Perhaps they would rise up against their leaders and remove them by force. And certainly Bhīṣma and the other senior Kurus would favor the Pāṇḍavas.

questions.question: What reason does Dhṛtarāṣṭra provide for why the Kurus should not banish the Pāṇḍavas?
options: The Pāṇḍavas possess too much wealth
The kingdom is too small for division
The gods have forbidden their exile
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra expresses that the citizens hold affection for the Pāṇḍavas and would be angered by their forced departure.

questions.question: What risk does Dhṛtarāṣṭra anticipate if the Kurus attempt to send the Pāṇḍavas away?
options: A civil war between the princes
The people might rise up and remove their leaders by force
The senior Kurus would abandon the throne
Duryodhana would lose his claim to the throne
questions.correct_answer: The people might rise up and remove their leaders by force
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra fears that public anger over the removal of the Pāṇḍavas could lead to an uprising against the current leaders.

questions.question: Whose support does Dhṛtarāṣṭra believe the Pāṇḍavas would receive among the senior Kurus?
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra explicitly mentions that Bhīṣma and other senior Kurus would favor the Pāṇḍavas.

1.6.53.paragraph_text: Duryodhana was ready for this objection. Before sending the Pāṇḍavas away, the Kurus should try to win over the people by various means. By bestowing wealth and honors on them they would gain their love and trust. Then, in the Pāṇḍavas’ absence, it would be possible for Duryodhana to become king. The prince went on, “Once my position is established in Hastināpura, Kuntī and her sons could even return. Do not fear for their welfare.”

questions.question: What method did Duryodhana propose to win the people's trust before sending the Pāṇḍavas away from Hastināpura?
options: By bestowing wealth and honors upon them
By declaring a tax amnesty for the citizens
By holding a public tournament of martial skills
questions.correct_answer: By bestowing wealth and honors upon them
questions.explanation: Duryodhana suggested that by bestowing wealth and honors on the people, the Kurus would gain their love and trust.

questions.question: What condition did Duryodhana set regarding Kuntī and her sons once his position as king was established in Hastināpura?
options: They would be exiled to the forest indefinitely
They would be required to serve as ministers
They could be permitted to return
They would be integrated into the royal army
questions.correct_answer: They could be permitted to return
questions.explanation: Duryodhana stated that once his position in Hastināpura was established, Kuntī and her sons could even return.

1.6.54.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra sat up and leaned toward his son. “This very thought has been on my mind, but I have not spoken it because it is a sinful thought. I am still doubtful. How do you propose that we deal with Bhīṣma, Vidura, Droṇa and Kṛpa? They love the Pāṇḍavas as if they were their own sons.”

questions.question: Which four figures does Dhṛtarāṣṭra identify as being particularly fond of the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra explicitly names Bhīṣma, Vidura, Droṇa, and Kṛpa as the individuals who love the Pāṇḍavas like their own sons.

questions.question: How does Dhṛtarāṣṭra characterize the nature of his own thoughts regarding the Pāṇḍavas?
options: As a test of divine will
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits to his son that his thoughts regarding the Pāṇḍavas are sinful.

1.6.55.paragraph_text: Duryodhana smiled. “Bhīṣma will remain neutral, as he always does. Droṇa’s son is my staunch supporter. His father will never go against him. Droṇa has married Kṛpa’s sister. Therefore Kṛpa’s support is assured. Vidura is the only one we cannot trust--he will certainly side with the Pāṇḍavas--but what harm can he alone do to us?”

questions.question: What is Duryodhana's assessment of Bhīṣma's stance in the impending conflict?
options: He will actively fight for the Pāṇḍavas.
He will remain neutral as he always does.
He will retire to the forest in protest.
questions.correct_answer: He will remain neutral as he always does.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana explicitly states that Bhīṣma will remain neutral, following his typical behavior.

questions.question: Why does Duryodhana feel assured that Droṇa will support him?
options: Droṇa is bound by an ancient vow to the Kuru throne.
Droṇa's son is a staunch supporter of Duryodhana.
Droṇa has received vast wealth from Duryodhana.
Droṇa is afraid of Duryodhana's military strength.
questions.correct_answer: Droṇa's son is a staunch supporter of Duryodhana.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana reasons that because Droṇa's son is his staunch supporter, Droṇa will not go against him.

questions.question: What marital connection does Duryodhana cite as a reason for Kṛpa's support?
options: Kṛpa married the sister of Droṇa.
Droṇa married the sister of Kṛpa.
Kṛpa is the brother-in-law of Bhīṣma.
Droṇa is the brother-in-law of Duryodhana.
questions.correct_answer: Droṇa married the sister of Kṛpa.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana mentions that Droṇa has married Kṛpa's sister to explain why Kṛpa's support is assured.

questions.question: Which advisor does Duryodhana consider untrustworthy?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana specifically identifies Vidura as the only one they cannot trust and predicts he will side with the Pāṇḍavas.

1.6.56.paragraph_text: Duryodhana implored his father to agree. If the Pāṇḍavas were allowed to remain in Hastināpura, he would not be able to live. His heart was burning and he lived in constant anxiety.

questions.question: What reason did Duryodhana give his father for why the Pāṇḍavas should not be allowed to remain in Hastināpura?
options: Their presence prevented him from being able to live.
They were secretly plotting to overthrow the king.
He feared their military strength would cause a civil war.
They refused to acknowledge him as the future ruler.
questions.correct_answer: Their presence prevented him from being able to live.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana told his father that if the Pāṇḍavas were allowed to stay in Hastināpura, he would not be able to live.

questions.question: How did Duryodhana describe his emotional state regarding the Pāṇḍavas' presence in Hastināpura?
options: He felt deep resentment and betrayed.
His heart was burning and he lived in constant anxiety.
He was filled with fury and a desire for immediate battle.
He felt humiliated and unworthy of the throne.
questions.correct_answer: His heart was burning and he lived in constant anxiety.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana explicitly stated that his heart was burning and he lived in constant anxiety due to the presence of the Pāṇḍavas.

1.6.57.paragraph_text: Dhṛtarāṣṭra was torn. He sat sighing for some moments. Finally, he gave his assent. Everything lay in the hands of Providence. What could he, a mere mortal filled with desire and fears, do against destiny? The king called his servants and retired for the night.

questions.question: What was the final decision made by King Dhṛtarāṣṭra regarding the presence of the Pāṇḍavas in Hastināpura?
options: He demanded they undergo a trial by combat.
He refused to allow them to remain.
questions.explanation: After sighing and feeling torn, Dhṛtarāṣṭra ultimately gave his assent regarding the situation.

questions.question: How did Dhṛtarāṣṭra characterize his own capacity to influence outcomes against the force of destiny?
options: He believed he was a powerful ruler capable of changing his fate.
He felt he was a mere mortal filled with desire and fears.
He viewed himself as an instrument of the gods with ultimate authority.
He considered himself indifferent to worldly events.
questions.correct_answer: He felt he was a mere mortal filled with desire and fears.
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra reflected on his own limitations, describing himself as a mere mortal filled with desires and fears who could do little against destiny.

1.7.1.paragraph_text: During the weeks that followed, Duryodhana and his brothers slowly began to win over the people. They distributed wealth and honors liberally and provided the citizens with all kinds of amenities and pleasures. At the same time, Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s ministers spoke continuously in the court about Vāraṇāvata, as they had been instructed by the king. The Pāṇḍavas heard them describe the city’s attractions. “The festival of Pāśupāta is starting soon. The procession has no equal anywhere in the world. The decorations, gems, jewels and entertainments delight the heart.”

questions.question: What methods did Duryodhana and his brothers use to gain the favor of the people following the events in Hastināpura?
options: They enforced strict taxation to build new fortifications.
They distributed wealth and honors while providing amenities and pleasures.
They organized military parades to showcase their strength.
They appointed new regional governors loyal to their cause.
questions.correct_answer: They distributed wealth and honors while providing amenities and pleasures.
questions.explanation: Duryodhana and his brothers won over the people by liberally distributing wealth and honors and providing various amenities and pleasures.

questions.question: Which city did Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s ministers consistently promote within the court to the Pāṇḍavas?
questions.explanation: Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s ministers spoke continuously in the court about Vāraṇāvata as instructed by the king.

questions.question: What specific festival did the ministers describe to the Pāṇḍavas to entice them regarding Vāraṇāvata?
questions.explanation: The ministers explicitly mentioned the festival of Pāśupāta to the Pāṇḍavas as a reason to visit Vāraṇāvata.

1.7.2.paragraph_text: The young princes were attracted. When Dhṛtarāṣṭra saw that their curiosity had been aroused, he said, “I have been hearing a lot about Vāraṇāvata of late. It occurred to me that you boys would derive much pleasure from visiting that city. Why not make a state visit? Bestow charity on the people and take your leisure. After enjoying yourselves there, you may return here whenever you please.”

questions.question: Which specific city did King Dhṛtarāṣṭra suggest the young princes visit for a state tour?
questions.explanation: King Dhṛtarāṣṭra explicitly suggests that the princes visit the city of Vāraṇāvata for a state visit.

questions.question: What primary activities did King Dhṛtarāṣṭra propose the princes engage in during their stay in Vāraṇāvata?
options: Perform religious sacrifices and military training
Collect taxes and oversee local administration
Bestow charity on the people and take their leisure
Construct a new palace and invite neighboring kings
questions.correct_answer: Bestow charity on the people and take their leisure
questions.explanation: The text states that King Dhṛtarāṣṭra advised the princes to bestow charity on the people and take their leisure while visiting the city.

1.7.3.paragraph_text: Yudhiṣṭhira, however, was intelligent; he understood that this was simply a ploy to remove them from the city. Why did the king not ask his own sons to go to Vāraṇāvata? Yudhiṣṭhira had seen the unusual kindness that Duryodhana had been displaying toward the people lately, and he knew that he was hatching a plot. But he felt helpless. He decided it would be better to do as the king suggested for the time being. Their position was not strong. The Pāṇḍavas had few friends or supporters and the king’s sons were constantly inclined to harm them in any way they could. Perhaps a time away from Hastināpura would help.

questions.question: Why did Yudhiṣṭhira suspect the king’s suggestion to visit Vāraṇāvata?
options: He feared the kingdom would be attacked by foreign invaders.
He believed it was a strategic move to remove the Pāṇḍavas from the city.
He knew the people of Vāraṇāvata were hostile toward his family.
He was ordered by his elders to never leave the capital.
questions.correct_answer: He believed it was a strategic move to remove the Pāṇḍavas from the city.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira perceived the king's suggestion as a calculated ploy to relocate the Pāṇḍavas away from Hastināpura.

questions.question: What specific observation led Yudhiṣṭhira to believe that Duryodhana was planning a plot against them?
options: Duryodhana had been showing unusual kindness toward the citizens.
Duryodhana was seen consulting with secret advisors.
Duryodhana had publicly declared himself the heir to the throne.
Duryodhana had attempted to seize their ancestral lands.
questions.correct_answer: Duryodhana had been showing unusual kindness toward the citizens.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Yudhiṣṭhira observed the unusual kindness Duryodhana had been displaying toward the people and concluded he was hatching a plot.

questions.question: What primary reason did Yudhiṣṭhira cite for his inability to resist the king's command?
options: He was bound by a sacred vow of obedience.
He believed it would benefit the kingdom's expansion.
The Pāṇḍavas lacked sufficient friends and supporters to challenge the king.
He was advised by his counselors to trust the king's intentions.
questions.correct_answer: The Pāṇḍavas lacked sufficient friends and supporters to challenge the king.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira felt helpless and complied because the Pāṇḍavas' position was weak due to a lack of friends and supporters.

questions.question: What was Yudhiṣṭhira's assessment of the king's sons regarding their intentions toward the Pāṇḍavas?
options: They were indifferent and focused only on their own wealth.
They were constantly looking for ways to harm the Pāṇḍavas.
They were planning to share the kingdom equally with them.
They were fearful of the Pāṇḍavas' growing influence.
questions.correct_answer: They were constantly looking for ways to harm the Pāṇḍavas.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira recognized that the king's sons were constantly inclined to harm the Pāṇḍavas in any way they could.

1.7.4.paragraph_text: A date was set for the Pāṇḍava’s departure. Duryodhana was overjoyed that the Pāṇḍavas had agreed to go, and immediately began to make plans.
