He opened his crimson eyes in surprise as he saw his sister standing in a human form next to Bhīma. Seeing her so beautifully bedecked with celestial ornaments, Hiḍimba immediately understood that she had become lustful toward the human. He spoke angrily.

questions.question: Which specific physical features characterized the appearance of the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba?
options: Dark as a rain cloud with arrow-shaped ears and red hair
Golden-skinned with sharp tusks and emerald eyes
Transparent skin with long white beard and silver horns
Obsidian-skinned with avian wings and blue hair
questions.correct_answer: Dark as a rain cloud with arrow-shaped ears and red hair
questions.explanation: The text explicitly describes Hiḍimba as being as dark as a rain cloud, having ears shaped like arrows, and possessing a shock of erect red hair.

questions.question: What physical trait indicated the immense strength and stature of the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba?
options: He had wings that spanned the width of a forest
His arms were thick like tree trunks and reached down to his knees
He carried a weapon made of pure celestial diamond
His skin was harder than tempered iron
questions.correct_answer: His arms were thick like tree trunks and reached down to his knees
questions.explanation: The text states that Hiḍimba's arms were thick like tree trunks and reached down to his knees.

questions.question: What specifically caused Hiḍimba to become angry upon encountering his sister?
options: She had betrayed their tribe to the gods
She had stolen his celestial ornaments
She was standing with a human and appeared bedecked with ornaments due to lust
She had failed to provide the humans he demanded for food
questions.correct_answer: She was standing with a human and appeared bedecked with ornaments due to lust
questions.explanation: Hiḍimba understood that his sister had become lustful toward the human because he saw her standing next to Bhīma and bedecked with celestial ornaments.

1.7.59.paragraph_text: “Who is so foolish as to create obstacles for me when I am hungry? O sister, have you become so senseless that you do not fear my anger? Fie upon you, O unchaste woman! Simply out of lust you are ready to do me an injury and sacrifice the very honor of our race. I shall kill you along with these humans.”

questions.question: What primary reason did Hiḍimba cite for his anger toward his sister while he was experiencing hunger?
options: She refused to provide him with food
She attempted to steal his territory
She failed to provide the necessary offerings
questions.explanation: Hiḍimba expresses anger because his sister is associating with humans, which he views as a betrayal of their race and a result of lust.

questions.question: How did Hiḍimba characterize his sister's actions regarding the honor of their race?
options: He claimed she was bringing glory to their family
He accused her of sacrificing the honor of their race for lust
He stated she was defending the honor of their race by aligning with humans
He claimed her actions were irrelevant to their ancestral honor
questions.correct_answer: He accused her of sacrificing the honor of their race for lust
questions.explanation: Hiḍimba explicitly accuses his sister of sacrificing the honor of their race due to her lustful feelings toward the humans.

questions.question: What threat did Hiḍimba issue to his sister and the humans present?
options: To banish them from the forest
To enslave the humans and forgive his sister
To kill both his sister and the humans
To challenge the humans to a duel while sparing his sister
questions.correct_answer: To kill both his sister and the humans
questions.explanation: Hiḍimba explicitly states his intention to kill his sister along with the humans.

1.7.60.paragraph_text: Pressing his teeth together, Hiḍimba ran at his sister with his arms raised. Bhīma stood up at once and stepped forward. His voice boomed out. “Stop! How dare you awaken my peacefully sleeping brothers. Nor should you attack this innocent woman. O wicked-minded one, your sister is not in control of herself. She has been brought under Cupid’s control. Therefore, she should not be punished.”

questions.question: Why does Bhīma insist that Hiḍimbā should not be punished by her brother?
options: She is protected by a vow of celibacy
She is under the influence of Cupid
She is an innocent traveler of the forest
She has already repented for her actions
questions.correct_answer: She is under the influence of Cupid
questions.explanation: Bhīma justifies his defense of the sister by stating she has been brought under Cupid's control and therefore is not in control of herself.

questions.question: What reason does Bhīma give for stopping Hiḍimba from attacking his brothers?
options: They are protected by the gods
The brothers are in the middle of a sacred ritual
Hiḍimba has already lost the right to fight
questions.explanation: Bhīma commands Hiḍimba to stop because he is threatening his brothers while they are peacefully sleeping.

1.7.61.paragraph_text: Bhīma smiled scornfully at the cannibal. Challenging him to a fight, the prince said, “Today you shall reach the land of the dead. I will pound your head to pieces. Your sister shall see me drag your mountain-like frame even as a lion drags an elephant. Hawks, vultures and jackals will then gleefully tear apart your corpse. Today this forest shall be rendered safe for all travelers.”

questions.question: What fate did Bhīma promise for Hiḍimba's corpse after the fight?
options: It would be buried in the forest
It would be burned on a pyre
Hawks, vultures and jackals would tear it apart
It would be offered as a sacrifice to the gods
questions.correct_answer: Hawks, vultures and jackals would tear it apart
questions.explanation: Bhīma specifically states that hawks, vultures, and jackals will gleefully tear apart Hiḍimba's corpse.

questions.question: To what animal did Bhīma compare himself when describing how he would drag Hiḍimba's body?
questions.explanation: Bhīma tells Hiḍimba that his sister will see him drag the cannibal's frame as a lion drags an elephant.

questions.question: What was the intended outcome of Bhīma's fight regarding the forest environment?
options: To reclaim the forest for the Pandavas
To render the forest safe for all travelers
To prove the superiority of human warriors over cannibals
questions.correct_answer: To render the forest safe for all travelers
questions.explanation: Bhīma declares that by killing Hiḍimba, the forest shall be rendered safe for all travelers.

1.7.62.paragraph_text: Hiḍimba flared up. He laughed at the human’s impudence. Advancing toward Bhīma, the cannibal screamed, “What use is this boasting? First accomplish all this and then speak. You think yourself strong but you shall learn the truth today. For now your brothers may sleep peacefully. First I shall kill you, O foul-mouthed one. After drinking your blood I will then slay the others.”

questions.question: Which specific action did Hiḍimba intend to perform after consuming the blood of Bhīma?
options: Offer a sacrifice to his ancestors
questions.explanation: Hiḍimba explicitly states his intention to slay the others after drinking Bhīma's blood.

questions.question: How did Hiḍimba react to the verbal challenges issued by Bhīma?
options: He feared the warrior and retreated
He laughed at the human's impudence
He fell into a meditative trance
questions.correct_answer: He laughed at the human's impudence
questions.explanation: The text notes that Hiḍimba flared up and laughed at the human's impudence.

1.7.63.paragraph_text: The Rākṣasa rushed at Bhīma with his arms outstretched. Bhīma immediately seized the giant’s arms and, not wanting to disturb his sleeping brothers, dragged him away a full thirty-two cubits, even as a lion might drag a small deer.

questions.question: By what specific distance did Bhīma drag the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba away from his sleeping brothers?
questions.explanation: Bhīma dragged the Rākṣasa away a full thirty-two cubits to avoid disturbing his sleeping brothers.

questions.question: What metaphor is used to describe the ease with which Bhīma dragged the Rākṣasa away?
options: Like a wolf dragging a stray lamb
Like a lion dragging a small deer
Like a bear dragging a trapped bird
Like an elephant dragging a fallen tree
questions.correct_answer: Like a lion dragging a small deer
questions.explanation: The text compares Bhīma dragging the giant Rākṣasa to a lion dragging a small deer.

questions.question: What was the primary reason Bhīma dragged the Rākṣasa away from the camp?
options: To tire him out through exertion
To reach a more favorable terrain for combat
To avoid disturbing his sleeping brothers
To showcase his immense physical strength to his family
questions.correct_answer: To avoid disturbing his sleeping brothers
questions.explanation: Bhīma dragged the Rākṣasa away specifically because he did not want to disturb his sleeping brothers.

1.7.64.paragraph_text: Hiḍimba broke free from Bhīma’s hold and wrapped his arms around him. He tightened his grasp and tried to crush him to death. Bhīma was unharmed. Even while held in the Rākṣasa’s powerful grip, Bhīma pulled him still further away so that his terrible yells might not disturb the others. He then burst free from Hiḍimba and clasped him in turn. The two fighters displayed their might as they lifted and hurled each other violently. They crashed about like two great elephants fighting for supremacy. Huge trees were smashed and splintered. The noise awoke the other princes and their mother. They sat up and looked around. They were astonished to see the extraordinarily beautiful Hiḍimbī. Kuntī addressed her with gentle words.

questions.question: When Bhīma and Hiḍimba engaged in their struggle, what comparison was used to describe the nature of their violent clash?
options: Two lions fighting over a kill
Two great elephants fighting for supremacy
Two mountain peaks colliding in a storm
Two powerful Rākṣasas vying for the forest throne
questions.correct_answer: Two great elephants fighting for supremacy
questions.explanation: The text explicitly describes the two fighters as crashing about like two great elephants fighting for supremacy.

questions.question: What was Bhīma's primary motivation for pulling Hiḍimba further away during the struggle?
options: To lure him into a trap set by the other brothers
To find more room for his physical maneuverings
To prevent his terrible yells from disturbing the others
To move the fight toward the river bank
questions.correct_answer: To prevent his terrible yells from disturbing the others
questions.explanation: The text notes that Bhīma pulled Hiḍimba away while in his grip so that his yells might not disturb the others.

questions.question: What immediate consequence did the violent fighting between Bhīma and Hiḍimba have on the surroundings?
options: The ground opened up beneath them
The forest fire was extinguished by their speed
Huge trees were smashed and splintered
The river nearby ceased its flow
questions.correct_answer: Huge trees were smashed and splintered
questions.explanation: The narrative states that as they fought, huge trees were smashed and splintered.

questions.question: Who was present at the scene and awoke as a result of the noise caused by the fight?
options: The other princes and their mother
The sages residing in the nearby hermitage
The inhabitants of the local village
questions.correct_answer: The other princes and their mother
questions.explanation: The text mentions that the noise of the fight awoke the other princes and their mother.

1.7.65.paragraph_text: “O celestial maiden, who are you? To whom do you belong? Why have you come to this forest? Are you the forest deity or an Apsarā? Please tell me everything.”

questions.question: When addressing the mysterious woman in the forest, which specific roles or identities does Kuntī inquire if she possesses?
options: A forest deity or an Apsarā
questions.correct_answer: A forest deity or an Apsarā
questions.explanation: Kuntī asks the woman if she is a forest deity or an Apsarā.

1.7.66.paragraph_text: Hiḍimbī explained who she was and how she had come to be standing there. Pointing to the combatants, she said, “I have chosen your golden-hued and immensely powerful son as my husband. Greatly angered by this, my brother has attacked him. See now how they struggle together, man and Rākṣasa, filling the forest with their roars.”

questions.question: Whom did Hiḍimbī identify as her chosen husband in the forest?
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī refers to the immensely powerful and golden-hued son, who is Bhīma, as the one she has chosen for her husband.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the attacker of the Pāṇḍava and Hiḍimbī?
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī explicitly states that her brother was greatly angered by her choice and attacked the Pandava warrior.

questions.question: How did Hiḍimbī describe the physical appearance of the son she intended to marry?
options: Glowed like the moon and very gentle
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī describes the object of her affection as golden-hued and immensely powerful.

1.7.67.paragraph_text: Yudhiṣṭhira and the other Pāṇḍavas stood up hastily and looked across at Bhīma. He was pounding Hiḍimba with his fists, making a sound like thunder claps. The Rākṣasa pressed forward and reached for Bhīma’s neck with his large hands. As they grappled, a dust cloud rose and covered them. They appeared like two cliffs enveloped in mist.

questions.question: What sound was produced by Bhīma as he struck the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba during their confrontation?
options: The roaring of a mountain lion
questions.explanation: The text explicitly compares the sound made by Bhīma pounding Hiḍimba with his fists to thunder claps.

questions.question: To what were Bhīma and Hiḍimba compared while they grappled and raised a dust cloud?
options: Two storm clouds colliding in the sky
Two mighty rivers meeting in a gorge
questions.explanation: The paragraph describes the two combatants as appearing like two cliffs enveloped in mist as the dust rose during their fight.

1.7.68.paragraph_text: Arjuna ran over and, with a smile on his lips, said to his brother, “O mighty-armed one, why did you not wake me? I can see that you are growing tired fighting this terrible Rākṣasa. Rest now and I shall kill him. Nakula and Sahadeva will protect our mother.”

questions.question: Which brother offered to take over the fight against the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba from Bhīma?
questions.explanation: Arjuna approaches the fighting brothers and tells Bhīma that he should rest while he finishes the task of killing the Rākṣasa.

questions.question: Which two brothers were assigned by Arjuna to guard their mother?
questions.explanation: Arjuna specifies that Nakula and Sahadeva are to remain with and protect their mother while he fights the Rākṣasa.

1.7.69.paragraph_text: Arjuna was taunting his brother only to incite his anger, and his words had the desired effect. Bhīma blazed up with fury and replied, “You need only be a spectator, dear brother. Have no fear. Now that this evil cannibal has entered my clutches he shall not escape with his life.”

questions.question: What was the purpose of Arjuna taunting Bhīma during the combat?
options: To mock his lack of skill
To warn him of the enemy's strength
questions.explanation: Arjuna taunted his brother specifically to incite Bhīma's anger.

questions.question: How did Bhīma characterize the Rākṣasa in his response to Arjuna?
questions.explanation: Bhīma refers to his opponent as an evil cannibal who has entered his clutches.

questions.question: What role did Bhīma assign to Arjuna during the confrontation with the cannibal?
questions.explanation: Bhīma explicitly tells Arjuna to be a spectator during the fight.

1.7.70.paragraph_text: Arjuna urged Bhīma to make haste. Twilight was approaching and the Rākṣasa’s strength would be doubled. Bhīma should kill him at once before he was able to use mystic powers and illusions to fight.

questions.question: Why did Arjuna urge Bhīma to hasten his attack against the Rākṣasa?
options: The Rākṣasa was preparing to flee the forest.
Twilight was approaching, which would double the creature's strength.
The Rākṣasa held innocent captives that needed rescue.
A storm was gathering that would hinder combat.
questions.correct_answer: Twilight was approaching, which would double the creature's strength.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Arjuna urged Bhīma to make haste because twilight was approaching and the Rākṣasa's strength would be doubled.

questions.question: What specific advantage did the Rākṣasa possess that concerned Arjuna during the battle?
options: The ability to summon celestial weapons.
Command over the elements of nature.
questions.explanation: The text mentions that Bhīma should kill the Rākṣasa before he was able to use his mystic powers and illusions in the fight.

1.7.71.paragraph_text: Bhīma summoned his father Vāyu’s latent power. With a roar he lifted the Rākṣasa above his head. Whirling him around, Bhīma said, “O cannibal, you have led a life of sin. Your existence has been in vain. Therefore you deserve an unholy death. Now I shall slay you like the beast you are, thereby freeing this forest from its thorny plant.”

questions.question: Whose latent power did Bhīma invoke during his confrontation with the Rākṣasa?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Bhīma summoned the latent power of his father Vāyu.

questions.question: What metaphor did Bhīma use to describe the impact of slaying the Rākṣasa on the forest environment?
options: Like clearing a path through a dense thicket
questions.explanation: Bhīma stated he would slay the Rākṣasa to free the forest from its 'thorny plant'.

questions.question: What specific action did Bhīma perform immediately after lifting the Rākṣasa above his head?
options: He threw him into the forest fire
He chanted a mantra of destruction
He bound him with mystical ropes
questions.explanation: The text describes that after lifting the Rākṣasa, Bhīma whirled him around before addressing him.

1.7.72.paragraph_text: Bhīma whirled the Rākṣasa one hundred times and dashed him to the ground. Hiḍimba let out a terrible roar which reverberated around the forest like a massive drum. Now Hiḍimba was only semi-conscious. Bhīma lifted him again and, by smashing him onto the trunk of a large sal tree, broke his back in two. He then stood up and smiled at his brothers, who by then had gathered around him. They embraced him and, looking at the lifeless form of the giant Rākṣasa, congratulated him for his incredible feat.

questions.question: How many times did Bhīma whirl the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba before dashing him to the ground?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Bhīma whirled the Rākṣasa one hundred times before dashing him to the ground.

questions.question: What object did Bhīma use to break the back of the Rākṣasa Hiḍimba?
options: The trunk of a large sal tree
questions.correct_answer: The trunk of a large sal tree
questions.explanation: Bhīma smashed the Rākṣasa onto the trunk of a large sal tree to break his back.

1.8.1.paragraph_text: Soon after Bhīma had killed Hiḍimba, the sun rose and the brothers could see paths through the forest. They decided to continue south. They were bound to come to a town at some point.

questions.question: Which Pandava brother was responsible for the death of the Rakshasa Hidimba?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Bhima killed Hidimba.

questions.question: In which direction did the brothers decide to travel after leaving the forest where Hidimba was killed?
questions.explanation: The text mentions the brothers decided to continue south after the sun rose.

1.8.2.paragraph_text: As they walked off with Kuntī between them, Hiḍimbī followed behind. Bhīma became concerned and said, “The Rākṣasas are known to avenge themselves on their enemies. They use deceptions and illusions. Therefore, O Hiḍimbī, you shall go the way your brother has gone.”

questions.question: What reason did Bhīma provide for wanting to eliminate Hiḍimbī after Hiḍimba's death?
options: She was attempting to steal their food supplies.
She was leading them into an ambush of more Rākṣasas.
Rākṣasas are known to avenge themselves on enemies using illusions and deception.
She was mocking the death of her brother.
questions.correct_answer: Rākṣasas are known to avenge themselves on enemies using illusions and deception.
questions.explanation: Bhīma explicitly cites the deceptive and vengeful nature of Rākṣasas as the reason for his concern regarding Hiḍimbī's presence.

questions.question: Who accompanied Bhīma and the other brothers as they walked through the forest following the encounter with Hiḍimba?
questions.explanation: The text states that the brothers walked off with Kuntī between them.

1.8.3.paragraph_text: Bhīma turned menacingly toward Hiḍimbī. He did not fear her in the least, but wished only to scare her away before she tried any trickery. But Yudhiṣṭhira stopped his younger brother. “O Bhīma, you should never kill a woman even in anger. The attainment of virtue is always a higher duty than the protection of one’s body. Besides this, what harm can this woman do to us? You have already slain her more powerful brother.”

questions.question: What reason does Yudhiṣṭhira provide to stop Bhīma from killing Hiḍimbī?
options: He believes she is innocent of all wrongdoing.
He considers the killing of a woman under any circumstances to be a violation of duty.
He fears the wrath of the gods if a woman is harmed.
He thinks she can be useful for their future plans.
questions.correct_answer: He considers the killing of a woman under any circumstances to be a violation of duty.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira explicitly states that one should never kill a woman, even in anger, as the attainment of virtue is a higher duty.

questions.question: How does Yudhiṣṭhira evaluate the threat posed by Hiḍimbī after the death of her brother?
options: He believes she possesses dangerous illusions and deceptive magic.
He sees her as a formidable warrior who still requires elimination.
He argues she is harmless because her more powerful brother is already dead.
He thinks she will eventually lead them into an ambush.
questions.correct_answer: He argues she is harmless because her more powerful brother is already dead.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira dismisses the threat posed by Hiḍimbī by pointing out that Bhīma has already slain her more powerful brother.

questions.question: What priority does Yudhiṣṭhira place on the protection of one's own body compared to the attainment of virtue?
options: The protection of the body is more important than virtue.
They are of equal importance in the face of danger.
The attainment of virtue is a higher duty.
Virtue is only important if the body is already safe.
questions.correct_answer: The attainment of virtue is a higher duty.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira asserts that the attainment of virtue is always a higher duty than the protection of one's own body.

1.8.4.paragraph_text: Hiḍimbī folded her palms to Yudhiṣṭhira and thanked him. She approached Kuntī and said with tears in her eyes, “Noble lady, you know well the suffering of a woman afflicted by desire. The god of love has pierced me with his shafts and I am consumed by desire for your son Bhīma. If he does not accept me as his wife, I will not be able to live. Do not doubt this.”

questions.question: Whom did Hiḍimbī approach to express her desire to become Bhīma's wife?
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī approached Kuntī to explain her suffering and request to be accepted as the wife of her son, Bhīma.

questions.question: By what force does Hiḍimbī claim she is afflicted when speaking to Kuntī?
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī states that the god of love has pierced her with his shafts, consuming her with desire for Bhīma.

1.8.5.paragraph_text: Hiḍimbī begged Kuntī to be merciful and allow her to marry Bhīma. She would carry all of them to a celestial region where they could rest for some time. There she could sport alone with Bhīma. Hiḍimbī promised Kuntī that she would always be available to serve the Pāṇḍavas. They had only to think of her and she would appear before them at once. Kneeling before Kuntī, Hiḍimbī said, “Please do not kill me by saying no. My request is in accord with virtue and indeed saving one’s life by any means is always considered virtuous by the wise. Virtue itself protects and sustains life; therefore grant me my desire, for it is not sinful.”

questions.question: What specific ability did Hiḍimbī offer to the Pāṇḍavas to assist them in their travels?
options: She could grant them eternal youth while in the forest.
She could carry them to a celestial region for rest.
She could lead them to the hidden kingdom of the Rākṣasas.
She could provide them with indestructible weapons.
questions.correct_answer: She could carry them to a celestial region for rest.
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī offered to carry the Pāṇḍavas to a celestial region where they could rest for some time.

questions.question: What assurance did Hiḍimbī provide regarding her availability to serve the Pāṇḍavas?
options: She would live in their camp permanently.
She would visit them once every lunar cycle.
She would appear at once whenever they thought of her.
She would send her servants to fulfill their needs.
questions.correct_answer: She would appear at once whenever they thought of her.
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī promised that she would be available to serve the Pāṇḍavas whenever they thought of her, appearing before them at once.

questions.question: What reasoning did Hiḍimbī use to justify her marriage request as virtuous?
options: She argued that she was a devotee of the same gods as the Pāṇḍavas.
She claimed that saving one's life by any means is virtuous.
She stated that she was destined to marry a kṣatriya warrior.
She declared that virtue is found in the union of two different species.
questions.correct_answer: She claimed that saving one's life by any means is virtuous.
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī argued that her request was virtuous because saving one's own life by any means is always considered virtuous by the wise.

1.8.6.paragraph_text: Yudhiṣṭhira smiled. He was impressed by Hiḍimbī’s knowledge of religion. She would be a good wife for Bhīma, whom he had noted was not above her sidelong glances. Yudhiṣṭhira said, “You have spoken well. O highly attractive lady, it must be as you say. You shall become Bhīma’s wife. Remaining with him by day, you may sport with him as you please. At night, however, he should always be returned to our presence.”

questions.question: Which Pandava brother gave the final approval for the union between Bhima and Hidimbi?
questions.explanation: The text states that Yudhisthira smiled and addressed Hidimbi, confirming she would become Bhima's wife.

questions.question: What specific limitation did Yudhisthira place on the time Bhima could spend with Hidimbi?
options: They could only be together during the morning hours
They could only be together during the night
They could only be together during the day
They could only be together during religious festivals
questions.correct_answer: They could only be together during the day
questions.explanation: Yudhisthira instructed that Hidimbi could sport with Bhima by day, but he must be returned to their presence at night.

questions.question: What reason did Yudhisthira provide for granting Hidimbi's request to marry Bhima?
options: He was impressed by her knowledge of religion
He wanted to secure an alliance with the forest dwellers
He felt sorry for her because she was lonely
questions.correct_answer: He was impressed by her knowledge of religion
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that Yudhisthira was impressed by Hidimbi's knowledge of religion before agreeing to the union.

1.8.7.paragraph_text: Hiḍimbī’s face blossomed with happiness. She looked at Bhīma with eyes full of love. The prince smiled at her and said, “I agree to this union, but I shall make one condition. As soon as you obtain a son I shall depart and leave you alone. My brothers and I have much to achieve in order to win back our father’s kingdom.”

questions.question: What specific condition did Bhīma stipulate for his union with Hiḍimbī?
options: He would leave as soon as she bore him a son.
He would only stay with her until the kingdom was reclaimed.
He would return to his brothers every evening.
He would never leave her side during the day.
questions.correct_answer: He would leave as soon as she bore him a son.
questions.explanation: Bhīma explicitly states to Hiḍimbī that he agrees to the union on the condition that he will depart and leave her alone as soon as she obtains a son.

questions.question: What primary motivation did Bhīma cite for his eventual departure from Hiḍimbī?
options: A desire to return to his mother Kuntī.
The need to fulfill his dharma as a warrior.
The necessity of achieving goals to reclaim his father's kingdom.
A command from his elder brother Yudhiṣṭhira.
questions.correct_answer: The necessity of achieving goals to reclaim his father's kingdom.
questions.explanation: Bhīma explains that he and his brothers have much to achieve in order to win back their father's kingdom.

1.8.8.paragraph_text: Hiḍimbī agreed. Then, assuming a large form, she carried them all to a high mountain lake called Salivahana. In that beautiful woodland region the brothers constructed a wooden hut on the lake shore. There they lived peacefully. Hiḍimbī took Bhīma with her during the daytime. She soared through the sky to celestial places and showed Bhīma the numerous exquisite gardens frequented by Siddhas and Gandharvas. There they lay together on the sandy banks of crystal streams covered with blue and red lotuses. Hiḍimbī also took him to the land of the Guhakas, which was situated on the shore of the divine Mānasa lake. Bhīma saw beautiful towns full of shining mansions and palaces and groves of blossoming trees and heavenly flowers, whose fragrance completely enchanted the mind.

questions.question: What is the name of the high mountain lake to which Hiḍimbī carried the Pāṇḍava brothers?
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī carried the brothers to a high mountain lake called Salivahana.

questions.question: Which celestial beings frequented the exquisite gardens that Hiḍimbī showed to Bhīma?
questions.explanation: The gardens Hiḍimbī showed to Bhīma were frequented by Siddhas and Gandharvas.

questions.question: Where is the land of the Guhakas situated according to the account of Bhīma's travels with Hiḍimbī?
options: On the banks of the Sarasvatī River
In the middle of the Salivahana lake
On the shore of the divine Mānasa lake
questions.correct_answer: On the shore of the divine Mānasa lake
questions.explanation: Hiḍimbī took Bhīma to the land of the Guhakas, which was situated on the shore of the divine Mānasa lake.

1.8.9.paragraph_text: Hiḍimbī was as dazzling as a goddess. She adorned herself with fine gems and gold ornaments and she constantly poured forth sweet song. Bhīma was captivated by her and the seven months they enjoyed together seemed to pass as quickly as if it were seven days. At the end of the seven months she gave birth to a son named Ghaṭotkaca. Within days of his birth the boy grew to youthhood and he took on the terrible form of a Rākṣasa. His huge body was fearsome, with knotted muscles, a head as bald as a pot, terrible red eyes, a long pointed nose and ears like sharp arrows. His chest was broad and he stood as tall as a palm tree.

questions.question: How long did Hiḍimbī and Bhīma live together before their time together concluded?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Bhīma and Hiḍimbī enjoyed seven months together.

questions.question: What is the name of the son born to Bhīma and Hiḍimbī?
questions.explanation: The text identifies the son born to Hiḍimbī after seven months as Ghaṭotkaca.

questions.question: Which of the following physical characteristics is mentioned as belonging to Ghaṭotkaca after he reached youthhood?
options: A head covered in thick, flowing hair
A head as bald as a pot
A slender frame like a deer
questions.correct_answer: A head as bald as a pot
questions.explanation: The text describes Ghaṭotkaca as having a head as bald as a pot upon reaching youthhood.

questions.question: How did the growth rate of Ghaṭotkaca progress following his birth?
options: He grew at the normal rate of a human child
He reached adulthood after seven years
He remained an infant for several months
questions.explanation: The text states that within days of his birth, the boy grew to youthhood.

1.8.10.paragraph_text: Although a Rākṣasa by nature, Ghaṭotkaca was inclined to virtue and he became a great favorite of the Pāṇḍavas. He was devoted to their service and they looked on him as a younger brother. Taught by the Pāṇḍavas, the boy quickly became proficient at weaponry and fighting. After a couple of months he asked permission from his parents to leave for the northern regions where Yakṣas and Rākṣasas dwell. He promised the Pāṇḍavas that they need only think of him and he would return to render them any service they required. After touching the feet of his mother and of all the Pāṇḍavas, he rose up to the sky and departed.

questions.question: Which region did Ghaṭotkaca intend to visit after seeking permission from his parents?
questions.explanation: Ghaṭotkaca asked his parents for permission to depart for the northern regions, which were inhabited by Yakṣas and Rākṣasas.

questions.question: What promise did Ghaṭotkaca make to the Pāṇḍavas regarding his future assistance?
options: He would return every year to offer tribute
He would return whenever they thought of him
He would defend them only during times of war
He would build a fortress in the north for their protection
questions.correct_answer: He would return whenever they thought of him
questions.explanation: Ghaṭotkaca promised that the Pāṇḍavas only needed to think of him and he would immediately return to render any service they required.

questions.question: How did the Pāṇḍavas view their relationship with Ghaṭotkaca?
questions.explanation: The Pāṇḍavas were devoted to his service and looked upon Ghaṭotkaca as a younger brother.

questions.question: What specific skill did the Pāṇḍavas teach to Ghaṭotkaca?
questions.explanation: Taught by the Pāṇḍavas, Ghaṭotkaca quickly became proficient in weaponry and fighting.

questions.question: What primary trait characterized Ghaṭotkaca's nature despite his Rākṣasa origin?
questions.explanation: Although he was a Rākṣasa by nature, Ghaṭotkaca was inclined toward virtue.

1.8.11.paragraph_text: The time had come for Bhīma to leave Hiḍimbī. She embraced the Pāṇḍava tearfully and asked if she would ever again see him. Bhīma assured her that in the future, after he and his brothers had overcome their obstacles and were free from danger, they could be reunited.

questions.question: Upon his departure from Hiḍimbī, what assurance did Bhīma provide regarding a potential future reunion?
options: He would return once he had regained the Kuru throne.
They would be reunited after he and his brothers overcame their obstacles and were free from danger.
They would meet again as soon as he had fulfilled his vow to his mother.
He would seek her out after the completion of his exile in the forest.
questions.correct_answer: They would be reunited after he and his brothers overcame their obstacles and were free from danger.
questions.explanation: Bhīma told Hiḍimbī that they could be reunited in the future once he and his brothers had overcome their obstacles and were free from danger.

1.8.12.paragraph_text: Leaving Hiḍimbī in their mountain dwelling, the Pāṇḍavas and Kuntī resumed their travels. They disguised themselves as ascetics by matting their hair and wearing tree-bark garments. Bhīma carried his mother as they traveled through the many different lands. Going from forest to forest they passed through the countries of the Matsyas, the Trigartas and the Pañchālas. No one recognised them. They proceeded slowly, unsure of what to expect and awaiting the Lord’s indication as to what they should do next. While travelling they studied the Vedic scriptures together and all five brothers imbibed the science of morality and many other subjects described in the Vedas.

questions.question: What disguise did the Pāṇḍavas and Kuntī adopt while traveling through different lands?
questions.explanation: The Pāṇḍavas and Kuntī disguised themselves as ascetics by matting their hair and wearing tree-bark garments.

questions.question: Which of the Pāṇḍava brothers carried his mother, Kuntī, during their travels?
questions.explanation: Bhīma is explicitly mentioned as carrying his mother as they traveled through various lands.

questions.question: Which three specific countries did the Pāṇḍavas pass through during their journey?
questions.explanation: The text states they passed through the countries of the Matsyas, the Trigartas, and the Pañchālas.

questions.question: What activity did the five brothers engage in together while they were traveling?
questions.explanation: While traveling, the five brothers studied the Vedic scriptures together and learned the science of morality.

1.8.13.paragraph_text: One day as they sat in the forest by their sacred fire, Vyāsadeva came to see them. After being received with due worship, the ṛṣi sat down and said, “I have been aware of the Kauravas’ unjust behavior toward you. Although I see both the Kauravas and yourselves equally, I feel a greater affection for you brothers due to your misfortune. I have therefore come here wishing to do you some good.”

questions.question: Which prominent rishi visited the Pāṇḍavas while they were sitting in the forest by their sacred fire?
questions.explanation: The text identifies the rishi who came to see the Pāṇḍavas in the forest as Vyāsadeva.

questions.question: Why did Vyāsadeva express a greater affection for the Pāṇḍava brothers compared to the Kauravas?
options: Because of their superior knowledge of the Vedas
Because they were his direct students
Due to their adherence to ascetic rituals
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva states that while he sees the Kauravas and the Pāṇḍavas equally, he feels greater affection for the Pāṇḍavas due to their misfortune.

questions.question: What reason did Vyāsadeva give for his arrival to visit the Pāṇḍavas in the forest?
options: To test their resolve in ascetic life
To perform a sacrificial ritual for their protection
To warn them of an impending attack by the Kauravas
To offer them some form of good or benefit
questions.correct_answer: To offer them some form of good or benefit
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva explicitly states that he has come wishing to do the Pāṇḍavas some good.

1.8.14.paragraph_text: Vyāsadeva informed them of a nearby village called Ekacakra. He instructed them to live there for some time, saying that he would come again to give them further directions. The ṛṣi then reassured the sorrowing Kuntī. Her sons would in time rule the world. The virtuous Yudhiṣṭhira, protected by his powerful brothers, would certainly become king. Soon he would perform the great Rājasūya sacrifice, establishing himself as the emperor of the entire globe.

questions.question: Which specific village did Vyāsadeva direct the brothers to reside in?
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva informed the brothers of a nearby village called Ekacakra and instructed them to live there.

questions.question: What major ritual does Vyāsadeva predict Yudhiṣṭhira will perform to establish himself as emperor?
questions.explanation: Vyāsadeva stated that Yudhiṣṭhira would perform the great Rājasūya sacrifice to become the emperor of the globe.

questions.question: How does Vyāsadeva characterize the future status of Yudhiṣṭhira in his conversation with Kuntī?
options: He will become a forest hermit
He will serve as a royal advisor
He will become the emperor of the entire globe
He will reign only over a small district
questions.correct_answer: He will become the emperor of the entire globe
questions.explanation: The paragraph states that Yudhiṣṭhira would perform the Rājasūya sacrifice and establish himself as the emperor of the entire globe.

1.8.15.paragraph_text: The sage personally led them to Ekacakra. He brought them to a Brahmin’s house where they were received as guests. Vyāsadeva then took his leave, telling them again that he would return to them before long.

questions.question: Which specific location did the sage Vyāsadeva personally escort the Pāṇḍavas to?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly identifies Ekacakra as the destination to which the sage led the group.

questions.question: In whose residence were the Pāṇḍavas hosted upon arriving in Ekacakra?
questions.explanation: The text states that Vyāsadeva brought the group to a Brahmin’s house to be received as guests.

1.8.16.paragraph_text: The Pāṇḍavas surveyed their new abode. The little village of Ekacakra was situated amid beautiful woodlands. The Brahmin had kindly given the brothers two rooms in his house for their residence. He had taken a vow that he would always receive any travelers who came to his door. By day they went about the village begging for food and, due to their gentleness and humility, they soon became dear to the people there. Everyone accepted them as wandering ascetics staying briefly in the village and they gladly gave them alms.

questions.question: What was the specific vow taken by the Brahmin who hosted the Pāṇḍavas in Ekacakra?
options: To never turn away a traveler who arrived at his door
To provide a permanent home for wandering ascetics
To fast until his guests were fully satisfied
To remain silent until the Pāṇḍavas left his house
questions.correct_answer: To never turn away a traveler who arrived at his door
questions.explanation: The text states the Brahmin had taken a vow to always receive any travelers who came to his door.

questions.question: How did the local villagers perceive the Pāṇḍavas while they were residing in Ekacakra?
options: As merchants seeking new trade routes
questions.explanation: The villagers accepted the Pāṇḍavas as wandering ascetics who were staying briefly in the village.

questions.question: What method did the Pāṇḍavas utilize to obtain food during their stay in Ekacakra?
options: They grew crops in the nearby woodlands
They traded goods with the local merchants
They begged for alms throughout the village
They were supported by the Brahmin's personal wealth
questions.correct_answer: They begged for alms throughout the village
questions.explanation: The text explains that the brothers went about the village begging for food during the day.

questions.question: Why did the people of Ekacakra become fond of the Pāṇḍavas?
options: Because of their impressive display of martial skill
Because of their gentleness and humility
Because they belonged to a high-ranking Brahmin family
Because they offered to help with agricultural labor
questions.correct_answer: Because of their gentleness and humility
questions.explanation: The text specifies that due to their gentleness and humility, the Pāṇḍavas soon became dear to the local people.

1.8.17.paragraph_text: Every night the brothers offered their mother whatever alms had been collected. She would then prepare their meal. Half of the food was given to Bhīma, and the rest was divided among the other four brothers and Kuntī.

questions.question: How did the brothers distribute the daily alms collected among themselves and Kuntī?
options: Bhīma received half, while the remaining half was shared among the other four brothers and Kuntī
Each of the five brothers received an equal portion, and Kuntī took a smaller share
The food was divided into six equal parts for each person
Kuntī took half for herself and the brothers shared the other half equally
questions.correct_answer: Bhīma received half, while the remaining half was shared among the other four brothers and Kuntī
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that half of the food was given to Bhīma, and the rest was divided among the other four brothers and Kuntī.

questions.question: At what time of day did the brothers present their collected alms to their mother?
questions.explanation: The text states that every night the brothers offered their mother the alms they had collected.

1.8.18.paragraph_text: When the brothers went out begging, a different one of them would remain behind each day to protect Kuntī. One particular day it was Bhīma’s turn to stay back. He was sitting with his mother when they heard loud crying in the house. Hearing the piteous lamentations, Kuntī’s heart was moved and she spoke to Bhīma. “O son, due to this Brahmin’s kindness we are living here peacefully with no fear of Duryodhana and his brothers. I am always thinking how we might repay this gentle Brahmin. A virtuous man should always return the good done to him with an even greater good. Perhaps now our chance has come.”

questions.question: What system did the brothers follow to ensure their mother Kuntī was protected while they were out begging?
options: One brother would stay behind each day on a rotational basis
They alternated staying behind every week
They took turns guarding her only on alternating days
Only the eldest brother was tasked with her protection
questions.correct_answer: One brother would stay behind each day on a rotational basis
questions.explanation: The text states that when the brothers went out begging, a different one of them would remain behind each day to protect Kuntī.

questions.question: Which of the brothers was tasked with staying back to guard Kuntī on the specific day they heard the crying in the Brahmin's house?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly identifies that on the day they heard the lamentations, it was Bhīma’s turn to stay back with his mother.

questions.question: What moral philosophy does Kuntī express regarding the repayment of kindness received from the Brahmin?
options: A man should repay equal value for the good he has received
A virtuous man should repay a good deed with an even greater good
Kindness to a Brahmin is its own reward and requires no repayment
One should repay kindness only when the benefactor is in distress
questions.correct_answer: A virtuous man should repay a good deed with an even greater good
questions.explanation: Kuntī remarks that a virtuous man should always return the good done to him with an even greater good.

questions.question: What primary reason does Kuntī provide for feeling obligated to help the Brahmin?
options: He is a relative of the Pāṇḍavas
He has provided them with peace and shelter, protecting them from Duryodhana and his brothers
He is an elderly man who has no children to help him
The Brahmin had asked them specifically for assistance in exchange for food
questions.correct_answer: He has provided them with peace and shelter, protecting them from Duryodhana and his brothers
questions.explanation: Kuntī mentions they are living there peacefully because of the Brahmin's kindness and protection from Duryodhana and his brothers.

1.8.19.paragraph_text: Kuntī could understand that the Brahmin had fallen into some great distress. Bhīma told her to try to ascertain the cause. He would then try to remove it, no matter how difficult a task it may prove to be.

1.8.20.paragraph_text: Kuntī slipped quietly into the inner apartments where the Brahmin lived with his family. She stood unnoticed by the door while the Brahmin and his wife and children sat with downcast faces. As Kuntī watched, the Brahmin said to his wife, “Fie upon this wretched life which affords one only misery. To live is to experience nothing but disease and pain. Pursuing in turn religion, wealth and pleasure, one endeavors much but receives little happiness. While everything leads to salvation, that is an impossible goal to achieve. Those who desire riches suffer, while those who have riches suffer even more. Alas, why do I live?”

questions.question: What three areas of human pursuit does the Brahmin argue bring little happiness?
questions.explanation: The Brahmin explicitly laments the pursuit of religion, wealth, and pleasure as yielding little happiness.

questions.question: How does the Brahmin characterize the state of life in his expression of despair?
options: As a journey toward inevitable enlightenment
As a cycle of inevitable disease and pain
As a test of endurance for the soul
As a burden that only the wealthy can escape
questions.correct_answer: As a cycle of inevitable disease and pain
questions.explanation: The Brahmin states that to live is to experience nothing but disease and pain.

questions.question: What conclusion does the Brahmin reach regarding the pursuit of salvation?
options: It is the only path that guarantees freedom from suffering
It is a goal that is impossible to achieve
It is easily reached by those who renounce wealth
It is a duty that he has successfully fulfilled
questions.correct_answer: It is a goal that is impossible to achieve
questions.explanation: The Brahmin laments that while everything leads to salvation, it remains an impossible goal to achieve.

questions.question: According to the Brahmin, what is the experience of those who possess riches?
options: They enjoy the ultimate freedom from misery
They are immune to disease and pain
They suffer even more than those who desire them
They are the only ones capable of reaching salvation
questions.correct_answer: They suffer even more than those who desire them
questions.explanation: The Brahmin states that those who desire riches suffer, but those who actually possess them suffer even more.

1.8.21.paragraph_text: Kuntī listened in silence as the Brahmin continued to condemn himself and his misfortune. He spoke of a terrible danger that had befallen them. His wife wept as he censured her, blaming their present predicament upon her wish to remain in the village due to affection for her now dead relatives. The Brahmin held his head in anguish. “How can I abandon you to save my own life? You were given to me by your parents. It is my duty to protect you. You have always served me and borne my children. I can in no way let you go. Nor can I abandon my only son or daughter. I shall go. Or maybe we should all die together.”

questions.question: To what does the Brahmin attribute the cause of his family's current dire predicament?
options: His wife's desire to stay in the village for the sake of her deceased relatives
A curse placed upon him by an offended sage
The loss of his wealth and royal status
An impending invasion of their village by a demon
questions.correct_answer: His wife's desire to stay in the village for the sake of her deceased relatives
questions.explanation: The Brahmin explicitly censures his wife, blaming their present predicament on her wish to remain in the village due to affection for her dead relatives.

questions.question: What primary reasons does the Brahmin cite for his inability to abandon his wife?
options: She was given to him by her parents and has dutifully served him and borne his children
She is the daughter of a powerful king who would seek retribution
Social law strictly forbids a man from ever living without a spouse
He is unable to survive physically without her daily assistance
questions.correct_answer: She was given to him by her parents and has dutifully served him and borne his children
questions.explanation: The Brahmin states that his wife was given to him by her parents, and he notes her role in serving him and bearing their children as reasons he cannot abandon her.

questions.question: What three options does the Brahmin consider when contemplating how to handle the danger facing his family?
options: He will go, his wife will go, or they will find a new village
He will go, they will hide, or they will fight
He will go, they should all die together, or they will flee
He will go, they will seek royal aid, or they will repent
questions.correct_answer: He will go, they should all die together, or they will flee
questions.explanation: The Brahmin contemplates three distinct courses of action: he shall go himself, or they should all die together (with the implication that he also considers the possibility of them collectively dying, though the text states 'I shall go. Or maybe we should all die together.').

1.8.22.paragraph_text: The Brahmin fell to the ground sobbing. His wife lifted him gently and said, “This lamentation does not befit a learned man like yourself. No one should lament inevitable death. Nor shall you or our children die. I shall go. Indeed, a woman’s highest duty lies in sacrificing her life to serve her husband. Undoubtedly such an act will confer upon me regions of eternal bliss.”

questions.question: What reason does the Brahmin's wife provide for why the Brahmin should not lament his circumstances?
options: The gods have promised them victory
The village will offer them protection
questions.explanation: The wife states that a learned man should not lament because death is inevitable.

questions.question: What does the wife of the Brahmin identify as the highest duty of a woman?
options: Raising children in the path of truth
Sacrificing her life to serve her husband
Seeking refuge with the local Brahmin community
questions.correct_answer: Sacrificing her life to serve her husband
questions.explanation: The wife declares that a woman's highest duty is to sacrifice her life in service to her husband.

questions.question: What spiritual reward does the wife believe she will attain by sacrificing her life for her husband?
options: The protection of the village gods
questions.explanation: She states that sacrificing her life to serve her husband will confer upon her regions of eternal bliss.

1.8.23.paragraph_text: Kuntī was curious. The Brahmin’s wife continued to implore her husband to allow her to die. She said that neither she nor her children could possibly survive in his absence. If she were left a widow, she would become a prey to dishonorable men, who would seek her just as crows descend upon a piece of meat left on the ground. How then could she protect their two young children and keep them on the path of truth and virtue? She folded her hands and begged her husband for permission to leave. He could then accept another wife, while she would earn undying religious merits by her final service.

questions.question: What reason did the Brahmin's wife give for why she and her children could not survive without her husband?
options: She feared they would be unable to perform the necessary ritual sacrifices
She believed they would be prey to dishonorable men who would seek them out
She knew they would be cast out of their village by local leadership
She claimed they lacked the physical strength to sustain themselves in the forest
questions.correct_answer: She believed they would be prey to dishonorable men who would seek them out
questions.explanation: The wife explicitly states that without her husband, she and her children would become prey to dishonorable men who would seek her out like crows descending on meat.

questions.question: What metaphor did the Brahmin's wife use to describe how dishonorable men would treat her if she were left a widow?
options: Like wolves encircling a lonely lamb
Like hawks hunting a small bird
Like crows descending upon a piece of meat left on the ground
Like vultures waiting for a wounded creature to expire
questions.correct_answer: Like crows descending upon a piece of meat left on the ground
questions.explanation: The wife specifically compares the anticipated harassment by dishonorable men to crows descending upon meat left on the ground.

questions.question: What did the Brahmin's wife propose her husband should do after she sacrificed her life?
options: Retire to the forest to live as a hermit
Seek protection for their children from the local King
Abandon his religious duties to focus on mourning
questions.explanation: The wife suggests that after she performs her final service and sacrifices her life, her husband would be free to accept another wife.

questions.question: Besides saving her family, what spiritual benefit did the wife believe she would gain by her final service?
options: She would earn undying religious merits
She would attain direct rebirth as a celestial being
She would absolve her children of all future sins
She would immediately join her ancestors in heaven
questions.correct_answer: She would earn undying religious merits
questions.explanation: The wife argues that by sacrificing her life in her husband's service, she would earn undying religious merits.

1.8.24.paragraph_text: The Brahmin sat with his head in his hands and made no reply. Then his daughter began to speak. “O father, why are you sorrowful when you have me? Allow me to go and thus save yourself. It is a child’s duty to save the parent. This is why the wise have called one’s child putra, one who delivers the parents from hell. My duty to my forefathers is to bear a son to offer them the sacred śraddhā, but by the grace of Providence I may now serve my forefathers by saving my father. O Father, you will one day have to abandon me. Therefore do not hesitate to do so now.”

questions.question: What is the etymological definition of the word 'putra' according to the Brahmin's daughter?
options: One who delivers the parents from hell
One who preserves the ancestral lineage
One who protects the family wealth
questions.correct_answer: One who delivers the parents from hell
questions.explanation: The daughter explains that the wise have named one's child 'putra' because they deliver their parents from hell.

questions.question: What primary duty toward her forefathers does the Brahmin's daughter mention needing to fulfill?
options: Constructing a temple for the ancestors
Bearing a son to offer them the sacred śraddhā
Living a life of complete celibacy
questions.correct_answer: Bearing a son to offer them the sacred śraddhā
questions.explanation: The daughter states her duty to her forefathers is to bear a son to offer them the sacred śraddhā.

questions.question: How does the Brahmin's daughter justify her father's choice to abandon her to the danger?
options: By arguing that it is a child's duty to save their parent
By claiming that her father is destined for immortality
By suggesting that her mother is better suited to stay
By stating that she has already fulfilled her life's purpose
questions.correct_answer: By arguing that it is a child's duty to save their parent
questions.explanation: The daughter tells her father that it is a child's duty to save the parent and asks him to allow her to go in his place.

1.8.25.paragraph_text: The girl wept along with her parents. The Brahmin’s small son then said in broken speech, “None of you should cry. Send me and I shall kill the cannibal Rākṣasa in a moment.” The boy smiled and brandished a piece of long grass as if it were a weapon.

questions.question: What object did the young boy brandish as a make-shift weapon while declaring his intent to kill the Rākṣasa?
questions.explanation: The boy brandished a piece of long grass while declaring he would kill the cannibal Rākṣasa.

1.8.26.paragraph_text: Although they were grief-stricken, they all laughed at the young boy’s words. Kuntī took the sudden change of mood as an opportunity to inquire about the cause of their distress. Could she do anything to help? The Brahmin replied that their grief could not be removed by any human being. The country where they lived was protected from enemies by a powerful Rākṣasa named Baka. He had long terrorized the people, who found no protection from their weak king. The Rākṣasa used to come whenever he wanted and kill them for his food. Finally the people went to Baka and proposed that if he would stop attacking them at will, then each week one of them would go to him with a large cartload of food. In turn he should protect them from attackers. The cannibal agreed, but he demanded that he also eat the man who delivered his food.

questions.question: What is the name of the powerful Rākṣasa who terrorized the country inhabited by the Brahmin and his family?
questions.explanation: The paragraph identifies the powerful Rākṣasa terrorizing the people as Baka.

questions.question: What agreement was established between the people and the Rākṣasa Baka to stop his random attacks?
options: The people would pay a tax of gold
The people would perform daily sacrifices to appease him
The people would send one person each week with a cartload of food
The people would build a temple in his honor
questions.correct_answer: The people would send one person each week with a cartload of food
questions.explanation: The text states that the people proposed to send one of them each week with a large cartload of food in exchange for Baka stopping his attacks.

questions.question: Besides receiving a cartload of food, what final demand did Baka make regarding the delivery of the food?
options: The delivery person must be a king
The delivery person must be a priest
The person who delivered the food must also be eaten
The person who delivered the food must stay as a servant
questions.correct_answer: The person who delivered the food must also be eaten
questions.explanation: The paragraph explains that Baka agreed to the deal on the condition that he also eat the man who delivered the food.

questions.question: Why were the people of the country unable to find protection against the Rākṣasa Baka?
options: Their soldiers were afraid of magic
The Rākṣasa lived in an impenetrable forest
The king was absent in a distant land
questions.explanation: The text mentions that the people found no protection from their weak king.

1.8.27.paragraph_text: The Brahmin told Kuntī that the turn of each man in their country came only after many years. Tomorrow it was his turn. He did not know what to do. He could not leave his young family alone, nor could he send them to their deaths. Therefore they would all go to meet the demon. Perhaps Baka would show compassion and spare them. Or they would all be devoured at once.

questions.question: What specifically was the Brahmin's predicament regarding the Rākṣasa Baka's demand?
options: He had been chosen by the king to be eaten next
It was his family's turn to deliver the tribute and be consumed
He had failed to deliver the required cartload of food on time
The Rākṣasa demanded his entire village be vacated
questions.correct_answer: It was his family's turn to deliver the tribute and be consumed
questions.explanation: The Brahmin explains to Kuntī that it was his turn to take the tribute to Baka and feared for his young family's fate.

questions.question: How often did the turn for a person to be delivered to the demon Baka arrive for the citizens?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the turn of each man in the country came only after many years.

questions.question: What options did the Brahmin consider when facing his turn to confront Baka?
options: Fleeing to a different kingdom or appealing to the king
Hiding his family or asking for a reprieve
Seeking compassion from Baka or having his entire family consumed together
Offering the Rākṣasa wealth instead of human life
questions.correct_answer: Seeking compassion from Baka or having his entire family consumed together
questions.explanation: The Brahmin contemplated either hoping Baka would show compassion and spare them, or having his entire family devoured at once.

1.8.28.paragraph_text: Kuntī said, “I see a means by which you may be delivered from this fear. Although you have but two children, I have five sons. Therefore let one of them go with the Rākṣasa’s tribute.”

questions.question: How many sons did Kuntī offer to send to the Rākṣasa as part of the tribute?
questions.explanation: Kuntī proposed that one of her five sons should go with the Rākṣasa's tribute to help the Brahmin.

questions.question: How many children did the Brahmin have, according to Kuntī's assessment of his situation?
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly states to the Brahmin that he has but two children.

questions.question: How many sons did Kuntī state that she herself had?
questions.explanation: Kuntī mentions that she has five sons when offering to send one of them to face the Rākṣasa.

1.8.29.paragraph_text: The Brahmin was shocked. “I can never cause the death of a guest and a Brahmin to save my own life. Even the most sinful man would not do this. Rather, one should sacrifice himself and his children for the sake of a Brahmin.”

questions.question: What ethical stance does the Brahmin take when presented with the proposal to send one of his children to the Rākṣasa?
options: He agrees to the plan to ensure his family's survival
He refuses, declaring he will never sacrifice a guest or a fellow Brahmin to save himself
He offers to fight the Rākṣasa alone instead of sending his children
He suggests that the Kuru princes should deal with the Rākṣasa instead
questions.correct_answer: He refuses, declaring he will never sacrifice a guest or a fellow Brahmin to save himself
questions.explanation: The Brahmin expresses shock and states he can never cause the death of a guest and a Brahmin to preserve his own life.

questions.question: According to the Brahmin, what is the proper order of sacrifice for the sake of a Brahmin?
options: A man should sacrifice his neighbor's children before his own
A man should sacrifice his servants first to ensure his children live
A man should be willing to sacrifice himself and his children
A man should sacrifice his own life only after all his children have perished
questions.correct_answer: A man should be willing to sacrifice himself and his children
questions.explanation: The Brahmin states that one should sacrifice himself and his children for the sake of a Brahmin.

1.8.30.paragraph_text: Kuntī was grave. “I am of the same opinion that Brahmins should always be protected, but you need not fear for my son. The Rākṣasa will not be able to kill him. He is powerful and knows the science of mantras.”

questions.question: What reason does Kuntī provide for why her son will not be killed by the Rākṣasa?
options: He is protected by the Pandavas
He is powerful and knows the science of mantras
He has been blessed by Lord Krishna
questions.correct_answer: He is powerful and knows the science of mantras
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly states that her son will not be killed because he is powerful and has knowledge of the science of mantras.

1.8.31.paragraph_text: Kuntī told the Brahmin that she had already seen her son kill a powerful Rākṣasa. Baka would prove no problem to him. She asked the Brahmin not to disclose to anyone else what she had told him. If others learned of her son’s powers, they would harass him for his knowledge and the power of the mantras would be diminished if they were given to others.

questions.question: Why did Kuntī insist that the Brahmin maintain silence regarding her son's past victory over a Rākṣasa?
options: The son desired fame and would be angered by the disclosure
The mantras' power would be diminished if shared with others
The king would force her son to serve as a royal soldier
Her son was under a vow of silence regarding his combat history
questions.correct_answer: The mantras' power would be diminished if shared with others
questions.explanation: Kuntī warned that the power of the mantras would be diminished if they were disclosed or given to others.

questions.question: What concern did Kuntī express regarding the potential impact of public knowledge of her son's abilities?
options: He would be exiled for his supernatural strength
He would be harassed for his knowledge
He would be required to marry a princess of the royal house
He would lose his position within the local community
questions.correct_answer: He would be harassed for his knowledge
questions.explanation: Kuntī stated that if others learned of her son's powers, they would harass him for his knowledge.

questions.question: Which specific Rākṣasa does Kuntī mention as a threat that her son would easily overcome?
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly mentions that Baka would prove no problem for her son.

1.8.32.paragraph_text: The Brahmin looked carefully at Kuntī’s expression. She was obviously speaking the truth. Her son must surely possess some extraordinary powers. With tears in his eyes he assented to her suggestion.

1.8.33.paragraph_text: Kuntī went to Bhīma and told him everything. She asked him to go to Baka. Bhīma agreed at once. His eyes lit up at the thought that he would be able to exercise his strength, while at the same time show their gratitude to the gentle Brahmin and his family.

questions.question: Whom did Kuntī approach to request that he deal with the demon Baka?
questions.explanation: Kuntī went to Bhīma and told him everything, subsequently asking him to go to Baka.

questions.question: What primary motivation influenced Bhīma’s enthusiasm for confronting Baka?
options: A desire to acquire the demon's kingdom
The opportunity to demonstrate his strength and express gratitude to the Brahmin's family
A command from Yudhiṣṭhira to protect the villagers
The need to prove his loyalty to the Kuru elders
questions.correct_answer: The opportunity to demonstrate his strength and express gratitude to the Brahmin's family
questions.explanation: Bhīma agreed at once because he wanted to exercise his strength and show gratitude to the Brahmin and his family.

1.8.34.paragraph_text: Just as Kuntī and Bhīma finished speaking, the other Pāṇḍavas returned. Yudhiṣṭhira caught Bhīma’s eye and sensed at once that his younger brother was contemplating something wonderful. He sat by his mother and asked quietly, “What does Bhīma have on his mind? It seems he is about to do some extraordinary deed. Is it something you have ordered, or is it some plan of his own?”

questions.question: Upon returning to Kuntī and Bhīma, who immediately sensed that his younger brother was considering an extraordinary action?
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira returned and noticed Bhīma's demeanor, sensing he was contemplating something wonderful.

questions.question: What specifically did Yudhiṣṭhira ask Kuntī regarding the nature of Bhīma's intended deed?
options: Whether it was sanctioned by the elders
If it was a task she ordered or a plan of his own
Why he had decided to keep it secret from his brothers
Whether he sought permission to leave the city
questions.correct_answer: If it was a task she ordered or a plan of his own
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira explicitly asks Kuntī if the deed is something she ordered or a plan of Bhīma's own.

1.8.35.paragraph_text: Kuntī told her son what had transpired. When Yudhiṣṭhira learned that Bhīma was about to go out to meet Baka he became alarmed. “O Mother, you have made this Brahmin a rash promise. Surely it is never sanctioned to sacrifice one’s own son for that of another. All my hopes of overpowering Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s sons and regaining the kingdom are dependent on Bhīma’s power.”

questions.question: Why did Yudhiṣṭhira express alarm upon learning of the plan concerning the demon Baka?
options: He feared for the safety of the Brahmin family.
He believed it was improper to sacrifice his own son for another's.
He felt the plan was an insult to the memory of Dhṛtarāṣṭra.
He was worried that Bhīma lacked the strength to defeat the demon.
questions.correct_answer: He believed it was improper to sacrifice his own son for another's.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira questions the wisdom of the promise by stating that it is never sanctioned to sacrifice one's own son for that of another.

questions.question: What primary reason did Yudhiṣṭhira give for needing Bhīma's continued safety and power?
options: To protect the Pāṇḍava family during their exile.
To serve as a guardian for the kingdom of the Brahmins.
To overpower Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons and regain the kingdom.
To fulfill a long-standing vow made to the gods.
questions.correct_answer: To overpower Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons and regain the kingdom.
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira states that his hopes of overpowering Dhṛtarāṣṭra’s sons and regaining the kingdom are dependent on Bhīma’s power.

questions.question: Who made the promise to the Brahmin regarding the encounter with Baka?
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira tells his mother, Kuntī, that she has made the Brahmin a rash promise regarding the encounter with Baka.

1.8.36.paragraph_text: Kuntī smiled slightly and reassured Yudhiṣṭhira. She reminded him of Bhīma’s superhuman prowess--how even as an infant he had crushed a great rock to powder, how he had easily carried all of them through the forest while running at the speed of the wind, how he had slain the immensely strong Hiḍimba. “It was not out of foolishness that I made my offer to the Brahmin. It is always a ruler’s duty to protect the Brahmins. By this act we will achieve two things: we will reward the Brahmin’s kindness toward us, and we will earn much religious merit.” Kuntī told her son that she had been wondering how to repay the Brahmin for some time. This opportunity was obviously the Lord’s arrangement for them.

questions.question: What demonstration of strength did Bhīma perform as an infant according to Kuntī?
options: Crushing a great rock into powder
Lifting a mountain with one hand
Wrestling a tiger to the ground
Breaking the branches of an iron tree
questions.correct_answer: Crushing a great rock into powder
questions.explanation: Kuntī reminds Yudhiṣṭhira that even as an infant, Bhīma had crushed a great rock to powder.

questions.question: Which demon does Kuntī identify as having been slain by Bhīma to reassure Yudhiṣṭhira?
questions.explanation: Kuntī references the slaying of the immensely strong Hiḍimba as evidence of Bhīma's prowess.

questions.question: What does Kuntī identify as the primary duty of a ruler regarding the Brahmins?
options: To provide them with land and wealth
To seek their counsel in political matters
To perform sacrifices on their behalf
questions.explanation: Kuntī explicitly states that it is a ruler's duty to protect the Brahmins.

questions.question: According to Kuntī, what two objectives would be achieved by helping the Brahmin against the demon?
options: Securing the kingdom and defeating Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons
Testing Bhīma's strength and gaining military alliances
Rewarding the Brahmin's kindness and earning religious merit
Displaying their power to the citizens and satisfying the gods
questions.correct_answer: Rewarding the Brahmin's kindness and earning religious merit
questions.explanation: Kuntī explains that by helping the Brahmin, they will reward his kindness and earn much religious merit.

1.8.37.paragraph_text: Yudhiṣṭhira pondered Kuntī’s words. Looking across at the smiling Bhīma, he replied, “You have spoken well. Your decision is well considered. Because of your compassion toward the Brahmin, Bhīma will surely kill the demon Baka, but you must ensure that no one comes to know that it was him.”

questions.question: Which specific entity did Yudhiṣṭhira agree that Bhīma should eliminate?
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira explicitly acknowledges in the text that Bhīma will kill the demon Baka.

questions.question: What condition did Yudhiṣṭhira impose regarding the death of the demon Baka?
options: That it must be done in front of the Brahmin
That it must be done without the use of weapons
That the identity of the slayer must remain secret
That the body of the demon be returned to the forest
questions.correct_answer: That the identity of the slayer must remain secret
questions.explanation: Yudhiṣṭhira specifically instructs Kuntī to ensure that no one comes to know that it was Bhīma who killed the demon.

1.8.38.paragraph_text: Yudhiṣṭhira was not sure if the Kauravas knew that he and his brothers were still alive. Their spies would soon inform them of Baka’s death. They may well suspect that it was Bhīma who had killed him. Few other men were capable of killing such a powerful Rākṣasa.

questions.question: Why did Yudhiṣṭhira anticipate that the Kauravas would soon learn of the demon Baka's death?
options: Kaurava spies would inevitably report the event
The villagers were planning to send a formal message
Baka had surrendered to the Kauravas
The brothers intended to boast of the feat
questions.correct_answer: Kaurava spies would inevitably report the event
questions.explanation: The text states that the spies of the Kauravas would soon inform them of the death of Baka.

questions.question: What led Yudhiṣṭhira to fear that the Kauravas might identify Bhīma as the individual responsible for killing Baka?
options: Bhīma was the only warrior capable of such a feat
A witness had already identified Bhīma to the spies
Few other men possessed the strength to defeat such a powerful Rākṣasa
Bhīma had left behind evidence of his identity
questions.correct_answer: Few other men possessed the strength to defeat such a powerful Rākṣasa
questions.explanation: The text notes that few other men besides Bhīma were capable of killing such a powerful Rākṣasa, leading to suspicion.

1.8.39.paragraph_text: That night while the village slept Bhīma quietly left the Brahmin’s house. He drove the cart loaded with food toward the forest where Baka dwelt. The aroma of the food was overpowering. After living for so long on meager forest fare and whatever alms the brothers were able to collect, Bhīma was ravenous. He began to eat the food from the cart. On his way into the forest, he shouted Baka’s name.

questions.question: What task did Bhīma undertake while the village slept in the Brahmin’s house?
options: He went to confront Baka with a weapon
He drove a cart loaded with food toward the forest
He searched for a hidden path to the city
He gathered wood for a fire to signal his brothers
questions.correct_answer: He drove a cart loaded with food toward the forest
questions.explanation: The text states that Bhīma quietly left the Brahmin's house at night and drove a cart loaded with food toward the forest where Baka dwelt.

questions.question: Why did Bhīma eat the food intended for Baka?
options: He wanted to mock the Rākṣasa
He was instructed to do so by the Brahmin
He was ravenous after surviving on meager forest fare
He believed the food was poisoned
questions.correct_answer: He was ravenous after surviving on meager forest fare
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that after living on meager forest fare and alms, Bhīma was ravenous and began eating the food from the cart.

questions.question: What specific action did Bhīma take as he entered the forest to engage Baka?
options: He challenged the Rākṣasa by shouting his name
He hid the cart and climbed a tree
He set the food on fire to lure the Rākṣasa
questions.correct_answer: He challenged the Rākṣasa by shouting his name
questions.explanation: The text states that on his way into the forest, Bhīma shouted Baka's name.

1.8.40.paragraph_text: The Rākṣasa heard Bhīma’s approach and became inflamed with anger. He ran toward the Pāṇḍava, yelling, “I am Baka!”

questions.question: What was the immediate emotional reaction of Baka when he heard Bhīma approaching?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that upon hearing Bhīma's approach, the Rākṣasa became inflamed with anger.
